name: HYDROGEN ENGINES AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES

topics.title: The Importance of Hydrogen as a Clean Energy Carrier
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT741 - HYDROGEN ENGINES AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** The Importance of Hydrogen as a Clean Energy Carrier # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Carrier vs. Energy Source — A Critical Distinction** > Hydrogen (H₂) is fundamentally an **energy carrier**, not a primary energy source. It does not exist freely in significant quantities on Earth in its diatomic molecular form and must be extracted from hydrogen-rich compounds (water, hydrocarbons, biomass) through energy-intensive processes. An **energy carrier** is a substance or system that stores, transports, and releases energy in a usable form upon demand. Electricity and hydrogen are the two principal secondary energy carriers in modern energy systems. While electricity is the dominant carrier today, hydrogen uniquely bridges the gap between the electricity, heating, and transportation sectors — a role often termed *sector coupling* or *Power-to-X (PtX)*. The hydrogen molecule (H₂) carries **120 MJ/kg** of usable chemical energy (Lower Heating Value) and produces only **water (H₂O)** as its combustion or electrochemical product, making it the cleanest energy carrier in the carbon cycle. ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: Hydrogen as the "Energy Currency" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: Hydrogen = Universal Bank Account for Energy** > Think of primary energy sources (sunlight, wind, coal, natural gas, uranium) as *your income*. Hydrogen is like a *savings account* — you deposit energy into it (via electrolysis or reforming), store it safely, and withdraw it later (via fuel cells or combustion) anywhere in the world, on demand, in any sector (transport, industry, power). Just as money is not wealth itself but a *medium of exchange*, hydrogen is not energy itself but a *medium of storage and transport*. This is why it is correctly classified as a **secondary energy carrier**, alongside electricity. ## 1.3 Why Hydrogen is Considered "Clean" — The Decarbonization Argument The cleanliness of hydrogen is **conditional** on how it is produced. The lifecycle CO₂ footprint is defined by the *production pathway*: | Production Pathway | Color Code | CO₂ Emissions (kg CO₂eq / kg H₂) | Cleanliness Rating | |---|---|---|---| | Electrolysis (Renewable) | **Green H₂** | ~0 – 1 | ★★★★★ Excellent | | SMR + Carbon Capture & Storage | **Blue H₂** | ~1 – 3 | ★★★★ Very Good | | Nuclear Electrolysis | **Pink H₂** | ~0 – 1 | ★★★★★ Excellent | | Methane Pyrolysis | **Turquoise H₂** | ~0 (solid carbon) | ★★★★★ Excellent | | Steam Methane Reforming (SMR) | **Grey H₂** | ~9 – 12 | ★★ Poor | | Coal Gasification | **Brown/Black H₂** | ~18 – 25 | ★ Very Poor | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Note:** When examiners ask "Is hydrogen a clean fuel?" — the *technically correct* answer is: **"Hydrogen is a clean energy CARRIER; it is only end-use clean. Its true environmental benefit depends entirely on the production pathway."** This conditional phrasing often earns full marks versus the oversimplified "Yes, hydrogen is clean." ## 1.4 Fundamental Properties of Hydrogen Relevant to Energy Applications > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical & Chemical Properties (Standard Reference: 25°C, 1 atm)** > - **Molecular Weight:** 2.016 g/mol > - **Density (Gas, STP):** 0.0899 kg/m³ > - **Density (Liquid, −253°C):** 70.8 kg/m³ > - **Lower Heating Value (LHV):** 120.0 MJ/kg > - **Higher Heating Value (HHV):** 142.0 MJ/kg > - **Flammability Range in Air:** 4.0 – 75.0 vol% > - **Auto-Ignition Temperature:** 500 °C > - **Diffusion Coefficient in Air:** 0.61 cm²/s (fastest of all gases) > - **Energy Content per Unit Mass:** ~2.5 × gasoline > - **Energy Content per Unit Volume (Gas, STP):** ~1/3000 × gasoline # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Hydrogen Value Chain — Operational Logic The complete hydrogen economy operates through four sequential stages: ### Stage 1: Production Hydrogen must be **manufactured** from feedstocks. The dominant industrial process is **Steam Methane Reforming (SMR)**: $$CH_4 + 2H_2O \xrightarrow{700-850°C, \, Ni \text{ catalyst}} CO_2 + 4H_2$$ The cleanest pathway is **electrolysis of water**: $$2H_2O \xrightarrow{Electric \, Current} 2H_2 \uparrow + O_2 \uparrow$$ with theoretical minimum voltage = **1.229 V** (per mole of H₂ at 25°C). ### Stage 2: Storage & Transportation Hydrogen's very low volumetric energy density (0.01005 MJ/L at STP vs. gasoline at 32 MJ/L) necessitates compression (350–700 bar), liquefaction (−253°C), or chemical carriers (NH₃, LOHC, metal hydrides). ### Stage 3: End-Use Conversion Two principal routes: - **Combustion Engine:** $2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + 572 \text{ kJ/mol}$ (releases heat → mechanical work) - **Fuel Cell (Electrochemical):** $\frac{1}{2}O_2 + H_2 \rightarrow H_2O + 237 \text{ kJ/mol (electrical)}$ (no thermal cycle) ### Stage 4: Application Transportation (FCEV), stationary power, industrial feedstock (refining, ammonia, steel), heating. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Hydrogen's Strategic Importance > [!IMPORTANT] > **Five Pillars Justifying Hydrogen's Strategic Role in KTU Module 1:** **Pillar 1 — Decarbonization of Hard-to-Abate Sectors:** Aviation, shipping, steel manufacturing, and heavy-duty trucking cannot be easily electrified via batteries due to weight/volume constraints. Hydrogen fuel cells and hydrogen combustion provide high energy density solutions. **Pillar 2 — Long-Duration Energy Storage:** Batteries are economically viable up to ~4 hours of grid storage. Hydrogen enables **seasonal storage** — excess summer solar/wind can be stored as H₂ and used in winter. **Pillar 3 — Sector Coupling:** Hydrogen uniquely links the electricity, heat, and transport sectors. It converts *stranded renewable electricity* (curtailed wind/solar) into a storable, transportable form. **Pillar 4 — Zero Local Emissions:** At point of use, hydrogen emits only water vapor — eliminating urban air pollution (NOₓ, PM, CO) from FCEV tailpipes. **Pillar 5 — Resource Availability:** Water (the primary feedstock for green hydrogen) is essentially infinite. There is no geopolitical monopoly comparable to OPEC for oil. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Comparison Table > [!NOTE] > **The following table summarizes the key equations and metrics every KTU student must memorize for Module 1 derivations. Note: vertical bars in absolute-value expressions use `\vert` to preserve markdown table integrity.** | # | Concept | Equation / Value | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Lower Heating Value of H₂ | $LHV_{H_2} = 120.0$ | MJ/kg | Excludes latent heat of water vapor | | 2 | Higher Heating Value of H₂ | $HHV_{H_2} = 142.0$ | MJ/kg | Includes latent heat of condensation | | 3 | Energy per Mole | $E_{mol} = LHV \times M = 120 \times 0.002016$ | MJ/mol | $\approx 0.242$ MJ/mol | | 4 | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (mass) | $AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = 34.3$ | kg air / kg H₂ | Derived from 2H₂+O₂+3.76N₂ | | 5 | Minimum Electrolysis Voltage | $V_{min} = 1.229$ | V | 25°C, 1 atm, reversible | | 6 | Thermoneutral Voltage | $V_{tn} = 1.481$ | V | Including HHV to sustain temperature | | 7 | Faradaic H₂ Mass | $m_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ | kg | Faraday's Law of Electrolysis | | 8 | Density of H₂ Gas (STP) | $\rho_{H_2} = 0.0899$ | kg/m³ | Lightest of all gases | | 9 | Energy Density (Volume, 700 bar) | $E_{vol} \approx 4.7$ | MJ/L | Practical compressed storage | | 10 | Octane Number of H₂ | $RON \geq 130$ | — | Excellent knock resistance | | 11 | Diffusion Velocity of H₂ | $v \approx 1.8$ | m/s (typical) | Affects leak safety analysis | | 12 | Flammability Limits | $L_{lower} = 4.0\%$, $L_{upper} = 75.0\%$ | vol% in air | Extremely wide range | | 13 | Energy Density Comparison | $\frac{E_{H_2}}{E_{gasoline}} = \frac{120}{44} \approx 2.73$ | — | By mass (gravimetric) | | 14 | Volume Energy Ratio | $\frac{E_{H_2}}{E_{gasoline}} = \frac{8.5}{32000} \approx 2.7 \times 10^{-4}$ | — | At STP — extremely low! | | 15 | HHV to LHV Ratio | $\frac{HHV}{LHV} = \frac{142}{120} = 1.183$ | — | 18.3% difference (largest for H₂) | ## 2.4 Energy Content Logic — Why the Mass vs. Volume Disparity Matters The **gravimetric** energy density of H₂ is the highest of any chemical fuel (120 MJ/kg). However, at standard conditions, the **volumetric** energy density is the lowest of practical fuels: $$E_{vol, H_2}^{STP} = \rho_{H_2} \times LHV = 0.0899 \times 120 \times 10^6 \text{ J/m}^3$$ $$E_{vol, H_2}^{STP} = 1.078 \times 10^7 \text{ J/m}^3 = 10.79 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$ For gasoline (ρ ≈ 750 kg/m³, LHV = 44 MJ/kg): $$E_{vol, gasoline} = 750 \times 44 = 33{,}000 \text{ MJ/m}^3$$ The ratio is: $$\frac{E_{vol,H_2}^{STP}}{E_{vol,gasoline}} = \frac{10.79}{33{,}000} \approx 3.27 \times 10^{-4}$$ This **~3000× volumetric deficit** is the single most important engineering challenge of the hydrogen economy and is the primary reason for **compression to 700 bar** or **liquefaction at −253°C** in FCEV applications. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio for Hydrogen Combustion > [!IMPORTANT] > **Foundational Reaction (Combustion of Pure H₂ in Air):** $$2H_2 + O_2 + 3.76N_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + 3.76N_2$$ **Step 1:** Identify molecular masses. - M(H₂) = 2 × 1.008 = **2.016 g/mol** - M(O₂) = 2 × 16.00 = **32.00 g/mol** - M(N₂) = 2 × 14.01 = **28.02 g/mol** - M(Air) = M(O₂) + 3.76 × M(N₂) = 32.00 + 3.76 × 28.02 Calculating the air molecular mass: $$M_{air} = 32.00 + 3.76 \times 28.02$$ $$M_{air} = 32.00 + 105.355$$ $$M_{air} = 137.355 \text{ g/mol}$$ **Step 2:** For every 2 moles of H₂ burned, we need 1 mole of O₂ + 3.76 moles of N₂ (i.e., 1 mole of O₂ in air). - Mass of fuel: $m_{fuel} = 2 \times M(H_2) = 2 \times 2.016 = 4.032$ g - Mass of air: $m_{air} = (1 \times 32.00) + (3.76 \times 28.02) = 32.00 + 105.36 = 137.36$ g **Step 3:** Compute the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio on a mass basis. $$AFR_{stoich} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = \frac{137.36}{4.032}$$ $$\boxed{AFR_{stoich} \approx 34.07 \text{ kg air / kg H}_2 \approx 34.3}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Comparison with conventional fuels:** Gasoline AFR ≈ 14.7, Diesel ≈ 14.5, Methanol ≈ 6.4. Hydrogen's AFR is more than **double** that of gasoline — meaning a hydrogen engine must process **2.3× more air mass** to deliver the same energy as gasoline. This directly impacts intake manifold design and volumetric efficiency. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Electrical Energy for Water Electrolysis The thermodynamic minimum energy to split water is governed by the **Gibbs free energy change** at standard conditions: **Step 1:** The reversible cell voltage is given by: $$E^0 = \frac{\Delta G^0}{n \cdot F}$$ where: - $\Delta G^0 = 237.13$ kJ/mol (Gibbs free energy of formation of liquid water) - $n = 2$ electrons transferred per mole of H₂ - $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (Faraday's constant) **Step 2:** Substituting values: $$E^0 = \frac{237{,}130 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}}$$ $$E^0 = \frac{237{,}130}{192{,}970}$$ $$E^0 = 1.229 \text{ V}$$ **Step 3:** Converting this to energy per kg of H₂ produced. The number of moles of H₂ in 1 kg is: $$n_{H_2} = \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{2.016 \text{ g/mol}} = 496.03 \text{ mol}$$ **Step 4:** Minimum electrical energy per kg of H₂: $$E_{min} = n_{H_2} \times \Delta G^0 = 496.03 \times 237.13 \text{ kJ/kg}$$ $$E_{min} = 117{,}619 \text{ kJ/kg} = 117.62 \text{ MJ/kg}$$ **Step 5:** Compare this to the chemical energy content of H₂ (LHV = 120 MJ/kg). The round-trip efficiency lower bound is: $$\eta_{min} = \frac{120}{117.62} = 1.02 \text{ (or } 102\% \text{)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Insight — The Over-unity Apparent Result:** The LHV of H₂ (120 MJ/kg) is slightly *greater* than the electrical minimum input to produce it (117.62 MJ/kg) because the thermoneutral voltage $V_{tn} = 1.481$ V corresponds to $\Delta H$ (HHV = 285.83 kJ/mol), not $\Delta G$. Real electrolyzers require ~50–55 kWh/kg of H₂ due to irreversibilities, achieving 65–80% electrical efficiency. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Faraday's Law — Hydrogen Mass from Electrolysis Current This is a **frequently asked 3-mark derivation** in KTU Module 1. **Step 1:** Faraday's First Law states that the mass of substance liberated at an electrode is directly proportional to the total electric charge passed: $$m = Z \cdot Q = Z \cdot I \cdot t$$ where Z is the electrochemical equivalent (kg/C). **Step 2:** For hydrogen, the electrochemical equivalent is: $$Z_{H_2} = \frac{\text{Atomic Mass}}{n \cdot F} = \frac{2.016 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}}$$ Computing the denominator: $$n \cdot F = 2 \times 96{,}485 = 192{,}970 \text{ C/mol}$$ Therefore: $$Z_{H_2} = \frac{2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{192{,}970}$$ $$Z_{H_2} = 1.0447 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kg/C}$$ **Step 3:** The general working formula for H₂ mass: $$\boxed{m_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F}}$$ Substituting the numerical formula: $$m_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t \times 2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$ $$m_{H_2} \approx 1.0445 \times 10^{-8} \times I \times t \text{ (kg)}$$ **Step 4:** Worked numerical — find H₂ produced in 1 hour at 100 A: Calculate charge: $$Q = I \cdot t = 100 \text{ A} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 360{,}000 \text{ C}$$ Calculate mass: $$m_{H_2} = 1.0445 \times 10^{-8} \times 360{,}000 = 0.0376 \text{ kg} = 37.6 \text{ g}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Sanity check:** 37.6 g of H₂ carries $0.0376 \times 120 = 4.51$ MJ — equivalent to about 0.103 kg of gasoline. This tiny mass flow is why **electrolyzers must operate for long durations** to be commercially viable, and why hydrogen is favored for *energy-intensive but low-mass* applications like FCEV refueling. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Hydrogen vs. Conventional Energy Carriers > [!IMPORTANT] > **This comparative table is the most frequently tested element in KTU Module 1. Memorize the relative orderings, not just numbers.** | Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Gasoline | Diesel | Natural Gas (CNG) | Battery (Li-ion) | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | **LHV (MJ/kg)** | 120 | 44 | 45.5 | 50 | 0.5 – 0.7 (cell only) | | **Density (kg/m³, ambient)** | 0.09 | 750 | 850 | 0.72 (gas) | ~2500 (pack) | | **Volumetric Energy (MJ/L)** | 0.011 (STP) | 32.2 | 36.4 | 0.036 (STP) | 1.5 – 2.0 (pack) | | **CO₂ (kg/GJ, end-use)** | 0 | 73 | 74 | 56 | 0 (electric drivetrain) | | **Refueling Time** | 5 – 10 min | 2 – 3 min | 2 – 3 min | 2 – 3 min | 30 – 60 min (DC fast) | | **Round-trip Efficiency** | 22 – 35% (FC) | 20 – 30% (ICE) | 25 – 35% (ICE) | 22 – 32% | 70 – 90% | | **Toxicity** | None (asphyxiant only) | Carcinogenic | Carcinogenic | Asphyxiant | Heavy metal waste | | **Storage T (°C/Pressure)** | −253°C or 700 bar | Ambient | Ambient | 200 bar | Ambient | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "Hydrogen Paradox":** Hydrogen wins decisively on mass density and refueling speed but loses on volume and round-trip efficiency. The choice of hydrogen vs. battery is therefore **application-dependent**: H₂ for long-haul/aviation/heavy transport, batteries for short-range urban mobility. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Hydrogen Energy Cycle (Full Value Chain) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Solar and Wind

Each arrow represents an energy conversion with associated losses, which collectively determine the well-to-wheel efficiency of the system. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]** ### **Q1. Distinguish between an energy source and an energy carrier. Is hydrogen an energy source or energy carrier? Justify with two examples.** [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** An **energy source** is a naturally occurring reservoir of energy from which useful work can be extracted directly (e.g., sunlight, wind, coal, uranium). An **energy carrier**, in contrast, is a substance that stores, transports, and delivers energy that was originally obtained from a primary source. **Hydrogen is an energy carrier** because: - It does not exist in free molecular form in significant quantities on Earth; it must be *manufactured* from water, hydrocarbons, or biomass. - Energy is *invested* during its production (electrolysis, SMR) and *recovered* during its end-use (combustion, fuel cell). **Examples of carriers:** Electricity and hydrogen. **Examples of sources:** Solar radiation, fossil coal. --- ### **Q2. Why is hydrogen considered a "clean" energy carrier only conditionally? Explain with reference to grey, blue, and green hydrogen.** [3 Marks] **Model Answer:** Hydrogen's cleanliness at the point of use is unconditional (only water is emitted). However, its *lifecycle* carbon footprint depends on the production pathway: - **Grey H₂** (from Steam Methane Reforming without CCS): Emits ~9–12 kg CO₂ per kg H₂ — *not* truly clean. - **Blue H₂** (SMR + Carbon Capture and Storage): Emits only 1–3 kg CO₂/kg H₂ — substantially cleaner. - **Green H₂** (Electrolysis using renewable electricity): Emits ~0 kg CO₂/kg H₂ — truly clean. Therefore, hydrogen is *end-use* clean but *upstream* may be carbon-intensive. The classification "clean" applies *only* to green and blue hydrogen. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]** ### **Question A (14 Marks)** #### **(a)** Discuss the role of hydrogen as a clean energy carrier in the global decarbonization scenario. Compare the gravimetric and volumetric energy densities of hydrogen with gasoline, and explain the engineering implications. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Role of Hydrogen in Decarbonization:** Hydrogen serves as the *missing link* between the renewable electricity sector and the hard-to-electrify sectors (heavy transport, aviation, steel, ammonia, seasonal storage). The pathway termed *Power-to-X* uses surplus renewable electricity to produce green hydrogen via electrolysis. This hydrogen can be stored, transported globally, and reconverted to electricity, heat, or mechanical work with no CO₂ emissions at the point of use. It thereby enables *sector coupling* — a foundational pillar of the net-zero 2050 targets set by the European Union, Japan, South Korea, and increasingly India. **Gravimetric Comparison (Energy per kg):** Using the LHV values: - $LHV_{H_2} = 120$ MJ/kg - $LHV_{gasoline} = 44$ MJ/kg The gravimetric ratio is: $$\frac{E_{H_2}}{E_{gasoline}} = \frac{120}{44} = 2.73$$ Hydrogen is **2.73 times more energetic per unit mass** than gasoline. **Volumetric Comparison (Energy per liter):** For hydrogen at STP (ρ = 0.0899 kg/m³): $$E_{vol,H_2} = 0.0899 \times 120 = 10.79 \text{ MJ/m}^3 = 0.01079 \text{ MJ/L}$$ For gasoline (ρ = 750 kg/m³): $$E_{vol,gasoline} = 750 \times 44 = 33{,}000 \text{ MJ/m}^3 = 33 \text{ MJ/L}$$ The volumetric ratio is: $$\frac{E_{vol,H_2}}{E_{vol,gasoline}} = \frac{0.01079}{33} \approx 3.27 \times 10^{-4}$$ Hydrogen occupies **~3000 times the volume** of gasoline for the same energy. **Engineering Implications:** 1. **Onboard storage volume** in FCEVs must be large — addressed by compression to **700 bar** (raising volumetric density to ~4.7 MJ/L) or liquefaction at **−253°C** (~8.5 MJ/L). 2. **Tank weight** is a concern; however, since the fuel itself is light, the *system* mass penalty is moderate. 3. **Vehicle packaging** is challenging: a 700-bar Type IV tank is bulky but can be packaged in the chassis floor. 4. **Energy to compress/liquefy** hydrogen consumes 10–15% of its LHV — a real cost in well-to-wheel efficiency. 5. **Aircraft and rockets** exploit the gravimetric superiority (liquid H₂ is the fuel of the Space Shuttle external tank and Ariane upper stage). #### **(b)** With neat sketches, explain the four stages of the hydrogen value chain. Discuss at least two challenges in each stage. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Stage 1: Production.** Hydrogen is produced from water, fossil fuels, or biomass. The cleanest route is **electrolysis** ($2H_2O \rightarrow 2H_2 + O_2$), and the cheapest is **SMR** ($CH_4 + 2H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + 4H_2$). *Challenges:* (i) High cost of green hydrogen (~$5–7/kg) versus grey hydrogen (~$1–2/kg); (ii) Renewable electricity is intermittent, so electrolyzers operate at low capacity factor; (iii) Water purity requirements necessitate deionization. **Stage 2: Storage.** Hydrogen is stored as compressed gas (350–700 bar), liquid (−253°C), or in chemical carriers (NH₃, LOHC, metal hydrides). *Challenges:* (i) Compression consumes ~10% of LHV; (ii) Liquefaction requires ~30% of LHV (boil-off losses are ongoing); (iii) Hydrogen embrittlement damages metal tanks; (iv) Metal hydrides have low mass-density reversibility. **Stage 3: Transportation.** Hydrogen is shipped via pipelines, tube trailers, or liquid tanker trucks. *Challenges:* (i) Existing natural gas pipelines cannot be used due to hydrogen embrittlement of steel; (ii) Trucking large volumes is uneconomical (low energy density); (iii) Maritime shipping of LH₂ is in early development. **Stage 4: End-Use.** Hydrogen is converted via fuel cells (PEMFC, SOFC) or combustion engines. *Challenges:* (i) PEM fuel cell platinum catalyst is expensive (~$30–50/kW target); (ii) Hydrogen flame speed is high → pre-ignition risk in ICEs; (iii) Public refueling infrastructure is sparse globally. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Option** #### **(a)** Derive the expression for the minimum mass of hydrogen produced by an electrolysis cell operating at current I for time t. Compute the H₂ produced in 2 hours at a current of 200 A. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Derivation:** The reaction at the cathode is: $$2H^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow H_2 \text{ (gas)}$$ This shows that **2 moles of electrons** (charge = 2F) liberate **1 mole of H₂** (mass = M = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg). By Faraday's First Law of Electrolysis, the mass of substance liberated is proportional to the total charge passed: $$m_{H_2} = Z_{H_2} \times Q$$ where the electrochemical equivalent is: $$Z_{H_2} = \frac{M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F} = \frac{2.016 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 96{,}485}$$ $$Z_{H_2} = 1.0447 \times 10^{-8} \text{ kg/C}$$ Total charge: $$Q = I \times t$$ Therefore: $$\boxed{m_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M_{H_2}}{n \cdot F}}$$ **Numerical Computation:** Given: I = 200 A, t = 2 hours = 7200 s Total charge: $$Q = 200 \times 7200 = 1{,}440{,}000 \text{ C}$$ Mass of H₂: $$m_{H_2} = 1.0447 \times 10^{-8} \times 1{,}440{,}000$$ $$m_{H_2} = 0.01504 \text{ kg} = 15.04 \text{ g}$$ **Sanity check:** 15.04 g of H₂ contains $E = 0.01504 \times 120 = 1.80$ MJ of chemical energy, equivalent to 0.041 kg of gasoline. This represents the small but cumulative output of an electrolyzer — justifying why commercial electrolyzers operate at MW scale. #### **(b)** Compare the Lower Heating Value (LHV) and Higher Heating Value (HHV) of hydrogen. Why is the HHV-LHV difference the largest for hydrogen among all common fuels? Compute the percentage difference. **[7 Marks]** **Model Answer:** **Definitions:** - **HHV (Higher Heating Value):** Total heat released when 1 kg of fuel is combusted and the *water product is condensed to liquid* (latent heat of vaporization recovered). - **LHV (Lower Heating Value):** Total heat released when 1 kg of fuel is combusted and the *water product remains as vapor* (latent heat lost with exhaust). The relationship is: $$HHV = LHV + h_{fg} \times n_{H_2O}$$ where $h_{fg} = 2.44$ MJ/kg is the latent heat of vaporization of water at 25°C and $n_{H_2O}$ is the mass of water produced per kg of fuel. **Values for Hydrogen:** The combustion of 1 kg of H₂ produces 9 kg of water: $$H_2 + \frac{1}{2}O_2 \rightarrow H_2O$$ Numerically: 2.016 g H₂ → 18.015 g H₂O, so 1 kg H₂ → 8.94 kg H₂O. The latent heat associated is: $$h_{fg} \times n_{H_2O} = 2.44 \times 8.94 = 21.81 \text{ MJ/kg}$$ **Standard reported values:** - $HHV_{H_2} = 142.0$ MJ/kg - $LHV_{H_2} = 120.0$ MJ/kg **Percentage Difference:** $$\% \text{ Difference} = \frac{HHV - LHV}{LHV} \times 100 = \frac{142 - 120}{120} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{\% \text{ Difference} = 18.33\%}$$ **Why Hydrogen has the Largest HHV–LHV Gap:** Hydrogen produces the *largest* mass of water per unit energy released, because each H₂ molecule (molecular mass 2.016) yields one H₂O molecule (molecular mass 18.015). The mass ratio of water to fuel is **8.94** — far higher than for hydrocarbon fuels. Since the latent heat is proportional to the water mass, the HHV–LHV gap is correspondingly the largest. For methane, the gap is only ~10%, and for gasoline, ~7%. **Engineering Implication:** In fuel cell vehicles operating below 100°C, the exhaust water is *liquid* (HHV applies), but in engines and gas turbines with hot exhaust, it is *vapor* (LHV applies). Therefore, **LHV is the relevant metric for hydrogen ICEs and high-temperature fuel cells**, while HHV is more accurate for low-temperature PEMFCs. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Module 1:** > 1. **"Hydrogen is the cleanest fuel"** without qualifying the *production pathway* is a 1-mark deduction. Always state the conditional nature. > 2. **Confusing LHV with HHV** in fuel cell vs. ICE calculations is a 2-mark trap. Fuel cell thermoneutral voltage (1.481 V) uses HHV; reversible voltage (1.229 V) uses LHV. > 3. **Volumetric energy density omission** — examiners specifically look for the 3000× penalty vs. gasoline to award full marks in any "importance of hydrogen" question. > 4. **Units in Faraday derivation** — mixing grams and kilograms, or omitting the factor of 2 (electrons per H₂ molecule), is a recurring 1-mark error. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Module 1 Rapid-Revision Bulletins — Memorize Before Exam:** - **Hydrogen is an energy CARRIER, not a source.** Always distinguish these in definitions. - **Three Ls of hydrogen energy:** LHV = 120 MJ/kg; Liquid at −253°C; Lightest molecule (M = 2.016 g/mol). - **HHV/LHV gap for H₂ = 18.3%** — the largest of any common fuel because of the high H₂O mass yield. - **Stoichiometric AFR for H₂ = 34.3** — the highest of all practical fuels (gasoline ≈ 14.7). - **Minimum electrolysis voltage = 1.229 V** (reversible, at 25°C); thermoneutral voltage = 1.481 V. - **Faraday's Law:** $m_{H_2} = \frac{I \cdot t \cdot M}{n \cdot F}$ where n = 2, M = 2.016 × 10⁻³ kg/mol, F = 96,485 C/mol. - **Green H₂** (renewable electrolysis) = truly clean. **Blue** = SMR + CCS. **Grey** = SMR without CCS (dirty). - **Gravimetric energy advantage:** H₂ is 2.73× gasoline per kg. - **Volumetric energy disadvantage:** H₂ is ~3000× worse than gasoline per liter at STP → must compress to 700 bar or liquefy. - **Flammability range 4–75 vol%** in air — extremely wide → significant leak safety implications. - **Auto-ignition temperature = 500°C** — relatively high compared to gasoline (~250°C). - **Diffusion coefficient = 0.61 cm²/s** — fastest of all gases → safety hazard (rapid mixing with air). - **Octane number ≥ 130** — excellent knock resistance, permits high compression ratios in H₂ ICEs. - **Hydrogen value chain:** Production → Storage → Transport → End-use (combustion or fuel cell). - **Sector coupling:** Hydrogen is the principal vector linking electricity, heat, transport, and industrial sectors. - **FCEV refueling time:** 5–10 minutes (vs. 30–60 min for battery EVs at DC fast chargers) — key advantage for heavy transport. - **Round-trip efficiency:** H₂ fuel cell pathway 22–35%; battery EV pathway 70–90%. H₂ is *less* efficient in raw energy terms but wins on mass and refueling speed for long-haul applications. - **Decarbonization niches for H₂:** Aviation, shipping, heavy trucking, steel manufacturing, ammonia, seasonal grid storage, and chemical feedstock.

topics.title: Basic Properties of Hydrogen
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT741 - HYDROGEN ENGINES AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Properties of Hydrogen

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hydrogen (H)** is the **lightest, simplest, and most abundant chemical element** in the universe, with the atomic number **Z = 1** and the standard atomic weight of **1.008 g/mol**. Under standard temperature and pressure (STP: 273.15 K, 101.325 kPa), hydrogen exists as a **diatomic, non-metallic, combustible gas** with the molecular formula **H₂** and a molecular weight of **2.016 g/mol**.

In the context of **Hydrogen Engines and Fuel Cell Vehicles (PEMUT741)**, hydrogen is treated as a **secondary energy carrier** (not a primary energy source) — meaning it must first be produced (via electrolysis, steam methane reforming, or biomass gasification), then stored, transported, and finally converted into useful mechanical or electrical work inside an Internal Combustion Engine (H₂-ICE) or a Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The course outcome **CO1** of PEMUT741 demands that the student "*understand the fundamental physical and chemical properties of hydrogen as an automotive fuel.*" This topic — **Basic Properties of Hydrogen** — is therefore the **foundational building block** for every later module in the syllabus.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Hydrogen as the Universal Currency of Energy"

Think of the energy ecosystem as a financial system. **Electricity is like a debit card** — clean to use, but tricky to store in your wallet. **Petrol and diesel are like gold bars** — extremely energy-dense and easy to stockpile, but heavy, polluting, and finite. **Hydrogen is the new "cash" of the energy world** — it can be:

- **Earned** (produced) from water, natural gas, biomass, or even sunlight.
- **Saved** (stored) in high-pressure tanks, liquid form, or metal hydrides.
- **Spent** (consumed) in either a flame (engine) or a silent electrochemical reaction (fuel cell).
- **Re-converted** (recycled) back to water — leaving **zero carbon emissions** at the tailpipe.

Just as the *colour, smell, and weight* of a currency note don't matter to a banker, what matters to an engineer are the **physical and chemical properties** of hydrogen: its *energy content, flammability, density, and storage behaviour*. This topic is essentially the **"passport details"** of hydrogen as a fuel.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters for KTU Examinations

| KTU Examination Angle | Weightage Tendency | Why It Is Asked |
|---|---|---|
| Direct definition / list-type | **High** (Part A, 3 marks) | Tests rote recall of constants |
| Numerical on heating value or A/F ratio | **High** (Part B, 7 marks) | Tests application of thermochemistry |
| Comparative property table (H₂ vs petrol) | **Moderate** | Tests analytical ability |
| Diagram-based question on H₂ molecule / flame | **Moderate** | Tests conceptual visualisation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory physical constants to memorise for KTU exams:**
> - **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg**
> - **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of H₂ = 142 MJ/kg**
> - **Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (by mass) = 34.3 : 1**
> - **Auto-Ignition Temperature = 500 °C to 585 °C**
> - **Flammability Range in air = 4% to 75% (by volume)**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Hydrogen's Properties

Hydrogen's properties relevant to engine and fuel-cell applications are grouped into **four logical categories**. Understanding this taxonomy is essential because KTU questions are routinely framed around one specific category.

### 2.1.1 Atomic & Molecular Properties
- **Atomic number (Z) = 1** → only one proton and one electron.
- **Standard atomic weight = 1.008 g/mol** → based on weighted average of isotopes ¹H, ²H (deuterium), ³H (tritium).
- **Molecular formula = H₂** → naturally diatomic; held together by a strong covalent **σ-bond** with bond dissociation energy of **436 kJ/mol**.
- **Molecular weight = 2.016 g/mol**.

### 2.1.2 Physical (Thermophysical) Properties

- **State at STP:** colourless, odourless, tasteless gas — hence **leak detection is extremely difficult**, a key safety challenge.
- **Boiling point (1 atm) = −252.87 °C (20.28 K)**.
- **Melting point (1 atm) = −259.16 °C (14.01 K)**.
- **Density at STP = 0.0899 kg/m³** (≈ 1/14 the density of air → it rises and disperses rapidly when leaked, which is a *safety advantage* in open spaces).
- **Liquid hydrogen density = 70.8 kg/m³** at −253 °C.
- **Critical temperature = 33.19 K**, **critical pressure = 1.297 MPa** → above this, H₂ cannot be liquefied by pressure alone.
- **Diffusivity in air = 6.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** (very high → spreads quickly → flame propagation is fast).
- **Specific heat at constant pressure, Cp = 14.32 kJ/(kg·K)**.
- **Thermal conductivity = 0.1815 W/(m·K)** (≈ 7× that of air).
- **Viscosity = 8.76 × 10⁻⁶ Pa·s** at 27 °C (very low).

### 2.1.3 Chemical / Combustion Properties

- **Combustion equation:** 
$$2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + \text{Energy (heat + light)}$$
- **Flammability limits in air (by volume): 4 % to 75 %** — the *widest* of any common fuel.
- **Flammability limits in pure O₂: 4 % to 94 %** (even wider).
- **Detonation limits in air: 18 % to 59 %** → risk of explosion is *substantially higher* than gasoline.
- **Auto-ignition temperature = 500 °C to 585 °C** (in air, depending on methodology).
- **Minimum ignition energy = 0.02 mJ** (≈ 1/10 that of gasoline, ≈ 1/100 that of methane → hydrogen is *extremely* easy to ignite).
- **Flame temperature in air ≈ 2045 °C**; in pure oxygen ≈ 2820 °C.
- **Flame speed = 2.65 m/s** (≈ 8× methane).
- **Quenching distance = 0.64 mm** (very small → flame can sneak past narrow gaps, demanding special flame-arrestor design).
- **Laminar burning velocity = 2.9 m/s** (highest among fuels).

### 2.1.4 Energy / Calorific Properties

- **Higher Heating Value (HHV) = 142 MJ/kg** — water produced is in *liquid* phase.
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) = 120 MJ/kg** — water produced is in *vapour* phase (the value used for engine work because exhaust water leaves as steam).
- **Energy density by volume (gas at STP) = 10.8 MJ/m³** — *very poor*.
- **Energy density by volume (liquid) = 8,491 MJ/m³** — still only 1/4 of gasoline.
- **Octane number = 130+ (RON)** → resists knock, ideal for high-compression engines.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** A high-frequency trick is to ask *"Why is HHV > LHV for hydrogen?"*. The answer: HHV assumes the water vapour in combustion products condenses to liquid, releasing its latent heat of vaporisation (**2,257 kJ/kg at 100 °C**). LHV assumes the water remains as vapour (realistic for an engine exhaust).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Wide Flammability Limits

Hydrogen's extraordinarily wide flammability range (4–75 % in air) arises from three molecular-level factors:

1. **Very low bond-dissociation energy relative to activation barrier** — only 0.02 mJ is needed to rupture the first H–H bond and trigger a chain reaction.
2. **High diffusion coefficient (6.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s)** — H₂ molecules rapidly mix with oxidiser, ensuring the mixture is *always* within flammable range near a leak.
3. **High laminar burning velocity (2.9 m/s)** — once ignited, the flame front propagates ~8 times faster than methane, meaning *transition to detonation* is a real hazard.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Property | Symbol & Value | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Molecular weight of H₂ | M_H₂ = 2.016 | g/mol | Stoichiometry calculations |
| 2 | Higher Heating Value | HHV = 142 | MJ/kg | Total energy released |
| 3 | Lower Heating Value | LHV = 120 | MJ/kg | Useful work available in engines |
| 4 | Stoichiometric A/F ratio (by mass) | A/F_st = 34.3 | kg air / kg H₂ | Combustion chemistry |
| 5 | Stoichiometric A/F ratio (by volume) | (A/F)_v = 2.38 | m³ air / m³ H₂ | Carburetion / mixing |
| 6 | Density at STP | ρ_H₂ = 0.0899 | kg/m³ | Storage tank sizing |
| 7 | Liquid H₂ density | ρ_liq = 70.8 | kg/m³ | Cryogenic tank design |
| 8 | Boiling point | T_b = −252.87 | °C | Liquefaction process |
| 9 | Critical temperature | T_c = −240 | °C | Phase change limits |
| 10 | Critical pressure | P_c = 12.97 | bar | Compression limits |
| 11 | Flammability limits (air) | 4 – 75 | % vol | Safety engineering |
| 12 | Auto-ignition temperature | T_auto = 500 – 585 | °C | Hot-surface safety |
| 13 | Minimum ignition energy | MIE = 0.02 | mJ | ESD / spark safety |
| 14 | Flame temperature (air) | T_flame = 2045 | °C | Material selection |
| 15 | Laminar burning velocity | S_L = 2.9 | m/s | Flame propagation design |
| 16 | Quenching distance | d_q = 0.64 | mm | Flame-arrestor gap |
| 17 | Specific heat (Cp) | Cp = 14.32 | kJ/(kg·K) | Heat transfer analysis |
| 18 | Thermal conductivity | k = 0.1815 | W/(m·K) | Insulation design |
| 19 | Diffusivity in air | D = 6.1 × 10⁻⁵ | m²/s | Leak dispersion modelling |
| 20 | Stoichiometric equation | 2H₂ + O₂ + 3.76 N₂ → 2H₂O + 3.76 N₂ | — | Combustion balance |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section rigorously derives the three most-asked KTU numerical problems on Basic Properties of Hydrogen.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio (By Mass)

**Statement to prove:** The stoichiometric air-to-fuel ratio for pure hydrogen combustion in air is **34.3 : 1 by mass**.

**Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical equation with air (assumed to be 21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂ by volume):**

For every 1 mole of O₂, 3.76 moles of N₂ accompany it (since 79/21 = 3.76).

$$2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 3.76\text{N}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3.76\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Identify the mass of fuel (H₂) per kmol of O₂ consumed:**

- 1 kmol of O₂ consumes 2 kmol of H₂.
- Mass of 2 kmol H₂ = 2 × 2.016 = **4.032 kg**.
- Mass of 1 kmol O₂ = 1 × 32 = **32 kg**.

**Step 3 — Calculate the mass of air supplied per kmol of fuel:**

- Mass of N₂ accompanying 1 kmol O₂ = 3.76 × 28 = **105.28 kg**.
- Mass of air = mass of O₂ + mass of N₂ = 32 + 105.28 = **137.28 kg**.

**Step 4 — Compute the stoichiometric A/F ratio by mass:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/F)_{stoich, \, mass} &= \frac{\text{Mass of air}}{\text{Mass of fuel}} \\
&= \frac{137.28 \text{ kg air}}{4.032 \text{ kg H}_2} \\
&= 34.05 \\
&\approx \mathbf{34.3 : 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result verified.** This is the standard value cited in every KTU textbook for H₂ combustion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy Content Comparison (H₂ vs Gasoline by Mass)

**Given:**
- LHV of H₂ = 120 MJ/kg
- LHV of gasoline ≈ 44 MJ/kg

**Step 1 — Compute the energy per unit mass:**

For 1 kg of fuel:
- Energy from H₂ = 120 MJ
- Energy from gasoline = 44 MJ

**Step 2 — Compute the mass-energy ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\text{Energy from H}_2}{\text{Energy from gasoline}} &= \frac{120 \text{ MJ/kg}}{44 \text{ MJ/kg}} \\
&= 2.727 \\
&\approx \mathbf{2.73 \text{ times}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mass of H₂ required to replace 1 kg of gasoline:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{H_2} &= \frac{\text{Energy of gasoline}}{\text{LHV of H}_2} \\
&= \frac{44 \text{ MJ}}{120 \text{ MJ/kg}} \\
&= 0.367 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** To deliver the same energy as 1 kg of gasoline, we need only **0.367 kg of H₂** — a *63 % mass saving*. This is the fundamental reason hydrogen is attractive for aviation and heavy road transport.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Volumetric Energy Density of H₂ (Gas vs Liquid)

**Step 1 — Energy density of gaseous H₂ at STP:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{vol,gas} &= \text{LHV} \times \rho_{gas,STP} \\
&= 120 \text{ MJ/kg} \times 0.0899 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
&= 10.788 \text{ MJ/m}^3 \\
&\approx \mathbf{10.8 \text{ MJ/m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy density of liquid H₂:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{vol,liq} &= \text{LHV} \times \rho_{liq} \\
&= 120 \text{ MJ/kg} \times 70.8 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
&= 8496 \text{ MJ/m}^3 \\
&\approx \mathbf{8.496 \text{ GJ/m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy density of gasoline (for comparison):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{vol,gasoline} &= 44 \text{ MJ/kg} \times 750 \text{ kg/m}^3 \\
&= 33{,}000 \text{ MJ/m}^3 = \mathbf{33 \text{ GJ/m}^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric penalty ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{E_{vol,gasoline}}{E_{vol,liq \, H_2}} &= \frac{33{,}000}{8{,}496} \\
&\approx \mathbf{3.88 \text{ times}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Liquid hydrogen carries only **1/3.88 ≈ 25.8 %** of the volumetric energy of gasoline. This is the *core challenge of hydrogen storage* — and the primary reason high-pressure 700 bar tanks and metal hydrides are actively researched.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use HHV vs LHV

| Situation in Engine / Fuel Cell | Correct Heating Value to Use | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Spark-Ignition H₂ engine (exhaust water leaves as steam) | **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** | Water does not condense inside the cylinder |
| Fuel cell (product water is liquid at low T) | **HHV = 142 MJ/kg** | Product water condenses, releasing latent heat |
| Adiabatic flame temperature calculation | **HHV** | Total chemical energy released |
| Indicated mean effective pressure (IMEP) calculation | **LHV** | Real work output, water exits as vapour |
| Heating value specification in textbooks (general) | **LHV** (auto), **HHV** (heating) | Industry convention |

---

## 3.5 Mass of Air for Complete Combustion of 1 kg H₂

A frequently asked KTU numerical:

**Step 1:** From Section 3.1, A/F ratio (by mass) = 34.3.

**Step 2:** Therefore, mass of air required for 1 kg of H₂:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{air} &= 1 \text{ kg H}_2 \times 34.3 \frac{\text{kg air}}{\text{kg H}_2} \\
&= \mathbf{34.3 \text{ kg air}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Mass of O₂ alone (21 % of air by mass):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{O_2} &= 0.233 \times 34.3 \\
&= \mathbf{7.993 \text{ kg O}_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Mass of N₂ accompanying (79 % by mass):

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{N_2} &= 0.767 \times 34.3 \\
&= \mathbf{26.31 \text{ kg N}_2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** 7.993 + 26.31 = 34.30 kg ✓
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Taxonomy of Hydrogen's Properties (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Hydrogen H2 Properties"]
    rootB["Atomic Molecular"]
    rootC["Physical Thermophysical"]
    rootD["Chemical Combustion"]
    rootE["Energy Calorific"]

    n1["Atomic number Z = 1"]
    n2["Atomic mass = 1.008 g/mol"]
    n3["Molecular formula H2"]
    n4["Bond energy 436 kJ/mol"]

    p1["Density 0.0899 kg per m3 STP"]
    p2["Boiling point minus 252.87 C"]
    p3["Liquid density 70.8 kg per m3"]
    p4["Diffusivity 6.1e-5 m2 per s"]
    p5["Specific heat Cp 14.32 kJ per kg K"]

    c1["Flammability 4 to 75 percent in air"]
    c2["Auto ignition 500 to 585 C"]
    c3["Min ignition energy 0.02 mJ"]
    c4["Flame speed 2.65 m per s"]
    c5["Quenching distance 0.64 mm"]

    e1["HHV 142 MJ per kg"]
    e2["LHV 120 MJ per kg"]
    e3["Stoich A by F ratio 34.3 to 1"]
    e4["Octane number 130 plus RON"]

    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC
    rootA --> rootD
    rootA --> rootE

    rootB --> n1
    rootB --> n2
    rootB --> n3
    rootB --> n4

    rootC --> p1
    rootC --> p2
    rootC --> p3
    rootC --> p4
    rootC --> p5

    rootD --> c1
    rootD --> c2
    rootD --> c3
    rootD --> c4
    rootD --> c5

    rootE --> e1
    rootE --> e2
    rootE --> e3
    rootE --> e4
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — H₂ as an Energy Vector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA["Primary Energy Source"]
    prodA["Hydrogen Production"]
    storA["Storage and Transport"]
    convA["End Use Conversion"]
    outl["Useful Work Output"]

    e1["Solar"]
    e2["Wind"]
    e3["Natural Gas"]
    e4["Biomass"]
    e5["Grid Electricity"]

    p1["Electrolysis"]
    p2["Steam Methane Reforming"]
    p3["Biomass Gasification"]

    s1["700 bar Compressed Gas"]
    s2["Cryogenic Liquid LH2"]
    s3["Metal Hydrides"]
    s4["Liquid Organic Carriers"]

    c1["Hydrogen ICE"]
    c2["PEM Fuel Cell"]
    c3["Gas Turbine"]

    w1["Mechanical Shaft Power"]
    w2["Electrical Power"]
    w3["Heat and Light"]

    srcA --> e1
    srcA --> e2
    srcA --> e3
    srcA --> e4
    srcA --> e5

    e1 --> prodA
    e2 --> prodA
    e3 --> prodA
    e4 --> prodA
    e5 --> prodA

    prodA --> p1
    prodA --> p2
    prodA --> p3

    p1 --> storA
    p2 --> storA
    p3 --> storA

    storA --> s1
    storA --> s2
    storA --> s3
    storA --> s4

    s1 --> convA
    s2 --> convA
    s3 --> convA
    s4 --> convA

    convA --> c1
    convA --> c2
    convA --> c3

    c1 --> outl
    c2 --> outl
    c3 --> outl

    outl --> w1
    outl --> w2
    outl --> w3
```

## 4.3 Combustion-Process Block Flow (Hydrogen in an Engine Cylinder)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inA["H2 from Tank"]
    inB["Air from Intake"]
    mixA["Mixing in Manifold"]
    compA["Compression Stroke"]
    igA["Ignition Spark or Auto"]
    combA["Combustion 2H2 plus O2"]
    expA["Expansion Power Stroke"]
    exhA["Exhaust H2O Vapour and N2"]

    inA --> mixA
    inB --> mixA
    mixA --> compA
    compA --> igA
    igA --> combA
    combA --> expA
    expA --> exhA

    by1["By product Water Vapour"]
    by2["Zero CO2 emission"]
    by3["Trace NOx from high flame temperature"]

    exhA --> by1
    exhA --> by2
    exhA --> by3
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**"List any six important physical properties of hydrogen that make it suitable as a fuel for IC engines."**

**Model Answer (3 marks — allocate ~0.5 mark per property):**

1. **Wide flammability range (4–75 % in air)** → engine can operate on very lean mixtures, improving thermal efficiency.
2. **High auto-ignition temperature (500–585 °C)** → allows high compression ratios without knock.
3. **High octane rating (130+ RON)** → permits high CR for better efficiency.
4. **High diffusivity (6.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s)** → forms a homogeneous mixture with air, ensuring complete combustion.
5. **High laminar burning velocity (2.9 m/s)** → fast flame propagation, smooth combustion.
6. **Low molecular weight (2.016 g/mol)** → easy to vaporise; clean combustion producing only H₂O.

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**"Distinguish between HHV and LHV of hydrogen. Why is LHV used in SI engine performance calculations?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | HHV (142 MJ/kg) | LHV (120 MJ/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| Product water state | Liquid | Vapour |
| Latent heat recovery | Yes (2,257 kJ/kg) | No |
| Typical use | Boilers, fuel cells | Engines, turbines |
| Magnitude | Higher | Lower (by 22 MJ/kg) |

**Reason LHV is used in engines:** During the exhaust stroke of an SI/CI engine, the water vapour produced from H₂ combustion **does not condense** — it leaves the cylinder as superheated steam because the exhaust gas temperature (≈ 700–900 K) is well above 100 °C. Hence, the latent heat of vaporisation is *not* recovered, and the *useful* energy available for conversion to mechanical work corresponds to the **LHV of 120 MJ/kg**. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

### ⭐ Question A — `[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Module 1]`

**"With the help of a balanced chemical equation, derive the stoichiometric air-fuel ratio for hydrogen combustion. Discuss the significance of the extremely wide flammability limits of hydrogen in engine design."**

#### Part (a) — Derivation of Stoichiometric A/F Ratio (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Write the balanced equation (1 mark):**

$$2\text{H}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 3.76\text{N}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3.76\text{N}_2$$

**Step 2 — Mass of fuel per kmol of O₂ (2 marks):**

- 1 kmol O₂ reacts with 2 kmol H₂.
- Mass of H₂ = 2 × 2.016 = 4.032 kg.

**Step 3 — Mass of air supplied (2 marks):**

- Mass of O₂ = 1 × 32 = 32 kg.
- Mass of N₂ = 3.76 × 28 = 105.28 kg.
- Total air mass = 32 + 105.28 = 137.28 kg.

**Step 4 — Final A/F ratio (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(A/F)_{stoich} &= \frac{137.28}{4.032} = 34.05 \\
&\approx \mathbf{34.3 : 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Part (b) — Significance of Wide Flammability Limits (7 marks)

1. **Lean-burn capability (2 marks):** Because H₂ remains combustible down to 4 % by volume, an engine can operate on *extremely lean mixtures* (equivalence ratio φ → 0.1), improving specific fuel consumption and reducing peak flame temperature.
2. **Better cold-start (1 mark):** At low temperatures, gasoline is hard to vaporise, but H₂ is always gaseous, so the mixture is reliably within flammability limits.
3. **Wide operating range (2 marks):** The same engine can be throttled from very lean (φ = 0.1) to rich (φ = 4), giving a wider power range.
4. **Safety hazard (2 marks):** The same wide limits mean leaks are *always* flammable → demands leak sensors, ventilation, and flame arrestors; KTU expects students to mention **safety implications** as the "other side of the coin."

---

### ⭐ Question B — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Module 1]`

**"Compare the energy content of hydrogen with gasoline on both a mass and volume basis. Comment on the implications for hydrogen storage in vehicles."**

#### Part (a) — Mass & Volumetric Comparison (7 marks)

**Given:**
- LHV_H₂ = 120 MJ/kg
- LHV_gasoline = 44 MJ/kg
- ρ_H₂(STP) = 0.0899 kg/m³
- ρ_liq H₂ = 70.8 kg/m³
- ρ_gasoline = 750 kg/m³

**Step 1 — Energy per kg (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{\text{Energy from 1 kg H}_2}{\text{Energy from 1 kg gasoline}} &= \frac{120}{44} = 2.73
\end{aligned}
$$

Hydrogen carries **2.73× more energy per kg**.

**Step 2 — Energy per m³ gaseous (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{vol,gas \, H_2} &= 120 \times 0.0899 = 10.8 \text{ MJ/m}^3 \\
E_{vol,gasoline} &= 44 \times 750 = 33{,}000 \text{ MJ/m}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

Gasoline carries **~3,056× more energy per m³** in gaseous form.

**Step 3 — Energy per m³ liquid (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{vol,liq \, H_2} &= 120 \times 70.8 = 8{,}496 \text{ MJ/m}^3 \\
\frac{E_{gasoline}}{E_{liq \, H_2}} &= \frac{33{,}000}{8{,}496} = 3.88
\end{aligned}
$$

Even *as a liquid*, hydrogen holds only **~25.8 %** of gasoline's volumetric energy.

**Step 4 — Mass saving vs Volume penalty summary (1 mark):**

| Basis | Hydrogen Advantage | Hydrogen Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|
| Per kg | 2.73× better | — |
| Per m³ (gas) | — | 3,056× worse |
| Per m³ (liquid) | — | 3.88× worse |

#### Part (b) — Implications for Vehicle Storage (7 marks)

1. **Mass advantage (1.5 marks):** On long-range trucks and aircraft, hydrogen saves 60–70 % of fuel-system mass at equivalent energy → useful for aviation.
2. **Volume penalty (1.5 marks):** A car needing 500 km range needs ~5 kg H₂ → at 700 bar compressed, occupies ~120 L; a gasoline tank for the same range is only ~30 L.
3. **Compression to 700 bar (1.5 marks):** Currently the industry standard; increases volumetric density from 10.8 to ~4.7 GJ/m³ — still a penalty but acceptable.
4. **Cryogenic liquid storage (1 mark):** At −253 °C, density rises to 70.8 kg/m³ → 8.5 GJ/m³, but boil-off losses of 0.1–0.3 %/day occur.
5. **Metal hydrides and LOHCs (1 mark):** Emerging technologies (MgH₂, LOHC like dibenzyltoluene) can store H₂ at near-atmospheric pressure; KTU 2024 syllabus lists them as a possible short note question.
6. **Safety packaging (0.5 mark):** Carbon-fibre Type-IV tanks add cost and mass — a real engineering trade-off.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor 3.76 for N₂.** Many students write A/F = 8 (just O₂/H₂ mass ratio) and lose 4 marks. Always use air (O₂ + 3.76 N₂).
> 2. **HHV vs LHV confusion.** In engine problems, the examiner expects LHV = 120 MJ/kg. Using HHV = 142 MJ/kg inflates efficiency by 18 % — instant 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Units trap.** Energy density by *volume* is MJ/m³, by *mass* is MJ/kg. Students often swap them when comparing with gasoline.
> 4. **Flammability in air vs oxygen.** Specifying "4–75 % in air" is correct; "4–94 % in O₂" is a different answer. Mention which oxidiser.
> 5. **Neglecting safety discussion.** In any 14-mark question on H₂ properties, a 1–2 mark allotment is reserved for *safety implications*. Skipping it costs easy marks.

---

## 5.4 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- 🔑 Hydrogen is the **lightest element (Z = 1)**, naturally diatomic (H₂), molecular weight **2.016 g/mol**.
- 🔑 At STP, density is **0.0899 kg/m³** (≈ 1/14 of air) — leaks rise and disperse.
- 🔑 Boiling point **−252.87 °C**; liquid density **70.8 kg/m³**.
- 🔑 **LHV = 120 MJ/kg**, **HHV = 142 MJ/kg** — use LHV for engines.
- 🔑 **Stoichiometric A/F ratio by mass = 34.3 : 1** (in air with 3.76 N₂ factor).
- 🔑 **Flammability range in air = 4–75 %** — widest of any fuel; both an advantage (lean burn) and hazard.
- 🔑 **Auto-ignition temperature = 500–585 °C**; **min ignition energy = 0.02 mJ** (extremely easy to ignite).
- 🔑 **Laminar burning velocity = 2.9 m/s**; **flame speed = 2.65 m/s**; **quenching distance = 0.64 mm**.
- 🔑 On a **mass basis**, H₂ carries **2.73×** more energy than gasoline.
- 🔑 On a **volume basis**, even liquid H₂ holds only **1/3.88** of gasoline's energy — the storage challenge.
- 🔑 Combustion product is **only water (H₂O)** → **zero CO₂** emissions at tailpipe (but possible H₂O-induced greenhouse effect at altitude).
- 🔑 Hydrogen is an **energy carrier, not an energy source** — must be produced from water, fossil fuels, or biomass.
- 🔑 Always cite **air composition (21 % O₂ + 79 % N₂)** with the **3.76 N₂ factor** in stoichiometric derivations.
- 🔑 For a KTU 14-mark question, include: **(i) derivation, (ii) comparison table, (iii) safety implications, (iv) diagrams** — these four together fetch full marks.
- 🔑 Memorise: HHV − LHV ≈ 22 MJ/kg (this difference is the **latent heat of vaporisation of water at 100 °C = 2,257 kJ/kg** × 9 kg water per kg H₂ ≈ 20 MJ/kg).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrogen as a Fuel in SI and CI Engines: Engine Modifications Required for Hydrogen Use in SI and CI Engines, Combustion Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT741 - HYDROGEN ENGINES AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hydrogen as a Fuel in SI and CI Engines: Engine Modifications Required for Hydrogen Use in SI and CI Engines, Combustion Characteristics # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Hydrogen as an Internal Combustion Engine Fuel **Formal Definition:** Hydrogen (H₂) is the simplest, lightest, and most energy-dense (by mass) gaseous fuel that can be combusted in modified Spark Ignition (SI) and Compression Ignition (CI) engines. It is an **energy carrier** rather than an energy source and must be produced via electrolysis, steam methane reforming (SMR), or biomass gasification before it can be utilized. When burned in an IC engine, hydrogen produces only water vapor and trace amounts of NOx as primary emissions, making it a near-zero-carbon alternative to petroleum-based fuels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Hydrogen is the only fuel that can theoretically allow a *true zero-CO₂ internal combustion engine* when burned in pure form, since carbon is not present in the fuel molecule. ## 1.2 Why Hydrogen Requires Engine Modifications Hydrogen possesses a unique combination of physical and chemical properties that differ drastically from gasoline, diesel, and natural gas. These differences, while beneficial in many respects, create several operational challenges that necessitate **substantial engine modifications** for both SI and CI configurations. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Bunsen Burner in a Box" Concept:** > Imagine trying to use a high-performance race-car engine (designed for gasoline) to run a laboratory Bunsen burner. The Bunsen burner has *very* different flame characteristics — it lights easily, burns in a wide range of air mixtures, and has an almost invisible flame. Hydrogen behaves like a controlled Bunsen burner inside an engine cylinder. The engine must be "re-engineered" to handle this fast, lean, and temperamental flame, much like retrofitting a kitchen for a new type of stove. ## 1.3 Key Properties of Hydrogen Relevant to Engine Combustion | Property | Hydrogen (H₂) | Gasoline | Diesel | Methane (CH₄) | |----------|---------------|----------|--------|----------------| | Molecular Weight (kg/kmol) | **2.016** | ~110 | ~170 | 16.04 | | Density at NTP (kg/m³) | **0.0838** | ~750 (liquid) | ~830 (liquid) | 0.717 | | Lower Heating Value (MJ/kg) | **120** | 44 | 42.5 | 50 | | Higher Heating Value (MJ/kg) | **142** | 47 | 45 | 55.5 | | Auto-Ignition Temperature (°C) | **571** | 280 | 210 | 537 | | Flammability Limits (vol % in air) | **4 – 75** | 1.4 – 7.6 | 0.6 – 5.5 | 5 – 15 | | Minimum Ignition Energy (mJ) | **0.02** | 0.25 | — | 0.29 | | Laminar Flame Speed (m/s) | **2.5 – 3.5** | 0.4 | — | 0.4 | | Quenching Distance (mm) | **0.64** | 2.0 | — | 2.0 | | Stoichiometric A/F Ratio (by mass) | **34.3 : 1** | 14.7 : 1 | 14.5 : 1 | 17.2 : 1 | | Octane Number (RON) | **> 130** | 88 – 98 | — | 120 | | Cetane Number | Negligible | — | 45 – 55 | — | | Adiabatic Flame Temperature (K) | **2400** | 2300 | 2300 | 2220 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Memory Aid:** The "**3 EASY 3 HARD**" Rule for Hydrogen: > - **3 EASY to ignite:** Low minimum ignition energy, wide flammability limits, high flame speed. > - **3 HARD to manage:** High auto-ignition temperature, very low quenching distance, abnormal combustion (pre-ignition, backfire, knock). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Thermodynamic Foundations of Hydrogen Combustion The stoichiometric combustion of hydrogen in pure oxygen is governed by the elementary reaction: $$ 2H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2H_2O + \text{Heat} \quad (\Delta H = -571.6 \text{ kJ/mol}) $$ In an actual engine, combustion occurs in air (≈ 21% O₂, 79% N₂ by volume). The corresponding stoichiometric equation becomes: $$ 2H_2 + (O_2 + 3.76\,N_2) \rightarrow 2H_2O + 3.76\,N_2 $$ ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Reasoning Behind the Air-Fuel Ratio The very high stoichiometric A/F ratio of **34.3 : 1** (by mass) for hydrogen arises because: - Each hydrogen molecule (mass = 2) requires one oxygen atom (mass = 16) to form H₂O. - Moles of O₂ per mole of H₂ = **0.5**. - Moles of air per mole of H₂ = **0.5 / 0.21 = 2.38**. - Mass of air = 2.38 × 28.97 = **68.97 kg**. - Mass of fuel (H₂) = **2.016 kg**. - Therefore A/F = 68.97 / 2.016 ≈ **34.3 : 1**. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Consequence:** Because hydrogen needs roughly **2.3 times more air** than gasoline at stoichiometric conditions, the *inducted mixture* occupies a larger volume per unit of energy, which directly reduces the **volumetric efficiency** and hence the peak power output of a hydrogen engine of the same displacement. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units | |---|-------------------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $(A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{2 \times 28.97 \times 2.38}{2.016} \approx 34.3$ | Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio | dimensionless | | 2 | $\phi = \dfrac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}}$ | Equivalence Ratio (φ) — φ < 1 lean, φ > 1 rich | dimensionless | | 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\phi}$ | Excess Air Ratio (λ) | dimensionless | | 4 | $\eta_{vol,H_2} = \eta_{vol,gasoline} \times \dfrac{\rho_{H_2,air\text{-}mix}}{\rho_{gas,air\text{-}mix}}$ | Volumetric Efficiency Ratio | dimensionless | | 5 | $T_{ad} = T_0 + \dfrac{HHV \cdot \eta_c}{m_{mix} \cdot c_{p,avg}}$ | Adiabatic Flame Temperature | K | | 6 | $E_{min,H_2} = 0.02 \text{ mJ}$ | Minimum Ignition Energy of H₂ | mJ | | 7 | $d_{quench,H_2} = 0.64 \text{ mm}$ | Quenching Distance of H₂ | mm | | 8 | $S_{L,H_2} \approx 8 \times S_{L,gasoline}$ | Relative Laminar Flame Speed | m/s | | 9 | $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{V_{dis} \cdot \eta_{vol} \cdot \rho_{H_2}}{N}$ | Mass of H₂ inducted per cycle | kg | | 10 | $Q_{NO_x} \propto \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{R\,T_{peak}}\right)$ | Zeldovich NOx Formation Trend | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pitfall:** In KTU valuation, equivalence ratio and excess air ratio are **not interchangeable**. Always state which is being used. Examiners specifically check for the difference between $\phi$ and $\lambda$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The above relationships govern: - **Engine calibration** for lean-burn hydrogen operation (λ typically 1.5 to 5.0). - **Power density losses** and the design of turbocharged / supercharged H₂ engines to recover lost volumetric efficiency. - **NOx aftertreatment sizing** — because peak flame temperatures are higher than gasoline, EGR and SCR become critical. - **Onboard storage system sizing** — because of the very low density of H₂, high-pressure (350–700 bar) tanks or cryogenic storage is required. ## 2.5 Why Hydrogen is "Difficult" in CI Engines A Compression Ignition engine relies on the **auto-ignition** of the fuel due to high compression temperatures (> 400 °C). Since hydrogen's **auto-ignition temperature is 571 °C** — which is *higher* than that of any hydrocarbon — pure hydrogen cannot self-ignite in a standard diesel cycle without assistance. This single property is the root cause of all CI hydrogen engine modifications. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** This is why hydrogen has an **octane number > 130** (excellent knock resistance in SI mode) but a **cetane number of approximately zero** (cannot auto-ignite easily in CI mode). The same chemical property that makes it great for SI engines makes it unsuitable for unmodified CI engines. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stoichiometric Air-Fuel Ratio of Hydrogen **Objective:** Derive the stoichiometric A/F ratio of pure hydrogen in air from first principles. **Step 1 — Write the balanced chemical equation (oxygen basis):** $$ 2\,H_2 + O_2 \rightarrow 2\,H_2O $$ **Step 2 — Identify the molar relationship:** - For every 2 moles of H₂ consumed, 1 mole of O₂ is required. - Therefore, moles of O₂ per mole of H₂ = $\dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5$. **Step 3 — Convert to air basis (air = 21% O₂, 79% N₂ by volume):** $$ n_{air} = \dfrac{n_{O_2}}{0.21} = \dfrac{0.5}{0.21} = 2.381 \text{ mol air / mol } H_2 $$ **Step 4 — Convert moles of air to mass:** $$ m_{air} = n_{air} \times M_{air} = 2.381 \times 28.97 = 68.97 \text{ kg air / kmol } H_2 $$ **Step 5 — Convert moles of H₂ to mass:** $$ m_{H_2} = 2.016 \text{ kg / kmol } H_2 $$ **Step 6 — Compute the A/F ratio:** $$ (A/F)_{stoich} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{H_2}} = \dfrac{68.97}{2.016} \approx 34.3 : 1 $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** The stoichiometric A/F ratio of hydrogen is **34.3 : 1 by mass**, which is roughly **2.33 times larger** than gasoline (14.7 : 1). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volumetric Efficiency Loss in a Hydrogen-Fuelled SI Engine **Objective:** Show quantitatively why a hydrogen SI engine produces less peak power than an equivalent gasoline engine of the same displacement. **Step 1 — Define volumetric efficiency for an inducted gaseous fuel:** $$ \eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{actual}}{m_{air\text{-}only}} = \dfrac{\rho_{mix}}{\rho_{air}} $$ **Step 2 — Mixture density at stoichiometric condition:** - Mass of mixture = $m_{air} + m_{fuel} = 68.97 + 2.016 = 70.986 \text{ kg}$. - For gasoline: $m_{mix,G} = 14.7 + 1 = 15.7 \text{ kg}$. **Step 3 — Moles comparison:** - For H₂: total moles per kg-mix ≈ $2.381 + 1 = 3.381$ kmol. - For gasoline (approx C₈H₁₈, M = 114): moles per kg-mix ≈ $\dfrac{14.7}{28.97} + \dfrac{1}{114} \approx 0.516$ kmol. **Step 4 — Density ratio (using ideal gas approximation):** Since at the same intake conditions (T, P), gas density is proportional to molecular mass per unit volume: $$ \dfrac{\rho_{mix,H_2}}{\rho_{mix,gasoline}} \approx \dfrac{3.381 / V}{0.516 / V} \approx 6.55 $$ **Step 5 — Volumetric efficiency approximation (displacing effect):** Hydrogen displaces a much larger fraction of intake air, so: $$ \eta_{vol,H_2} \approx \eta_{vol,gasoline} \times \left(1 - y_{H_2}\right) $$ where $y_{H_2}$ is the mole fraction of H₂ in the mixture. This results in a typical **15 – 30% reduction** in volumetric efficiency. > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Solution:** This loss is recovered in practice by **turbocharging / supercharging**, by using **lean burn (λ ≈ 2–4)**, or by **direct injection of hydrogen** into the cylinder at high pressure. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Adiabatic Flame Temperature of Hydrogen **Objective:** Estimate the peak adiabatic flame temperature of a stoichiometric H₂–air mixture. **Step 1 — Energy balance equation:** $$ \sum_i n_i \int_{T_0}^{T_{ad}} C_{p,i}(T)\,dT = -\Delta H_{rxn} $$ **Step 2 — Standard enthalpy of formation of H₂O (g) at 298 K:** $$ \Delta H_f^\circ = -241.8 \text{ kJ/mol} $$ For 2 moles of H₂: $$ \Delta H_{rxn} = 2 \times (-241.8) = -483.6 \text{ kJ} $$ **Step 3 — Heat capacity terms (constant volume approximation, simplified):** - $C_{p,H_2O(g)} \approx 34 + 0.0001\,T$ J/mol·K - $C_{p,N_2} \approx 28 + 0.00008\,T$ J/mol·K For 2 mol H₂O + 7.52 mol N₂: $$ \sum n_i C_{p,i} \approx 2(34) + 7.52(28) = 68 + 210.6 = 278.6 \text{ J/K} $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $T_{ad}$:** $$ T_{ad} \approx T_0 + \dfrac{|\Delta H_{rxn}|}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}} = 298 + \dfrac{483600}{278.6} \approx 298 + 1735 = 2033 \text{ K} $$ **Step 5 — Refined value (with dissociation at high T):** Accounting for partial dissociation of H₂O into H, O, and OH radicals, the **effective** adiabatic flame temperature is approximately: $$ T_{ad,H_2} \approx 2400 \text{ K} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Result:** Hydrogen's adiabatic flame temperature (~2400 K) is **higher than gasoline (~2300 K)**, which is the primary reason for elevated NOx formation in H₂ engines — a critical KTU exam point. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Combustion Characteristics Summary | Characteristic | Behavior in SI Engine | Behavior in CI Engine | |----------------|----------------------|------------------------| | Ignition Method | Spark (high-energy coil) | Difficult — requires pilot diesel, glow plug, or fumigation | | Flame Propagation | Very fast, near-instantaneous | Highly variable, often incomplete | | Flammability Range | Operable from λ = 1.2 to 5.0 | Limited range in dual-fuel mode | | Knocking | Resistant (high RON) | Not applicable (no auto-ignition) | | Pre-ignition | Major concern (residual hot spots) | N/A | | Backfire | Severe problem | Not applicable | | NOx Formation | High (high flame T) | Moderate (lower peak T due to lean burn) | | HC / CO Emissions | Near zero | Higher (unburned H₂ and pilot fuel) | | Volumetric Efficiency | Reduced 15–30% | Slightly less affected | | Cold Start | Difficult (heating of intake required) | Very difficult | | Power Output | 80–90% of gasoline baseline | 70–90% of diesel baseline | | Thermal Efficiency | Higher (lean burn, fast cycle) | Comparable to diesel in dual-fuel mode | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hydrogen SI Engine — Modifications Block Architecture The following block diagram illustrates the major subsystems that must be added or modified when converting a conventional gasoline SI engine to run on hydrogen. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[H2 Storage Tank
350 or 700 bar] --> B[Pressure Regulator
Reduces to 5 to 10 bar] B --> C[Pressure Transducer
Closed loop control] C --> D[H2 Injector Rail
Timed Port or DI] D --> E[Intake Manifold
Modified anti backfire] E --> F[Engine Cylinder
Modified piston crown] F --> G[High Energy Ignition
50 to 100 kV coil] G --> H[Catalyst Three Way
NOx reduction] H --> I[Exhaust H2O only] J[ECU Hydrogen Calibration
Lambda and Timing Maps] --> D J --> G K[Heated Intake Air
Cold start assist] --> E L[Crankcase Ventilation
Hydrogen Leak Removal] --> F M[Pre Ignition Detection
Ion Sense / Knock Sensor] --> J style A fill:#e1f5ff style B fill:#fff4e1 style D fill:#ffe1e1 style F fill:#e1ffe1 style G fill:#f4e1ff style H fill:#ffe1f4 ``` ## 4.2 Hydrogen CI Engine — Dual Fuel / Fumigation Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD A[H2 Storage Tank
High Pressure] --> B[Pressure Regulator] B --> C[Fumigation Mixer
In Air Intake] C --> D[Engine Cylinder
Pilot Diesel Injector] D --> E[Diesel Pilot Injection
5 to 10 percent energy] E --> F[Combustion
Diesel Ignites H2] F --> G[Exhaust Treatment
NOx and HC] H[ECU Dual Fuel Control
H2 Diesel Ratio] --> C H --> E I[Heated Intake
Improve Ignition] --> C J[Cetane Improver Additive
if needed] --> E K[Turbocharger
Recovers Power Density] --> C style A fill:#e1f5ff style C fill:#ffe1e1 style D fill:#e1ffe1 style E fill:#fff4e1 style F fill:#f4e1ff style G fill:#ffe1f4 ``` ## 4.3 Combustion Process Comparison — Flame Propagation Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A0[Spark Plug] --> A1[Flame Kernel
2 to 5 percent of mass burned] A1 --> A2[Flame Growth
Fast due to high SL] A2 --> A3[Main Combustion
5 to 30 percent of mass] A3 --> A4[Late Combustion
30 to 90 percent] A4 --> A5[Completion
100 percent burn] B0[Auto Ignition Sites
Multiple] --> B1[Premixed Burn
H2 Air Cloud] B1 --> B2[Mixing Controlled
Pilot Diesel Diffusion] B2 --> B3[Late Burn
Quasi Steady] B3 --> B4[Exhaust] subgraph SI_Hydrogen[SI Hydrogen Combustion] A0 A1 A2 A3 A4 A5 end subgraph CI_Hydrogen[CI Hydrogen Dual Fuel Combustion] B0 B1 B2 B3 B4 end ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** In the SI engine, a single spark kernel grows into a fast-propagating flame (high $S_L \approx 3$ m/s) that consumes the premixed H₂–air charge. In the CI (dual-fuel) engine, diesel pilot injection creates multiple auto-ignition sites that ignite the surrounding H₂–air mixture, leading to a hybrid premixed-diffusion combustion mode. ## 4.4 Abnormal Combustion Phenomena in Hydrogen Engines ```mermaid flowchart TD X1[Abnormal Combustion
Hydrogen Engine] --> Y1[Backfire
Flame in Intake] X1 --> Y2[Pre Ignition
Premature Combustion] X1 --> Y3[Knock
Rapid Pressure Rise] X1 --> Y4[Misfire
Excessively Lean] Y1 --> Z1[Cause: Hot Residuals
Fix: Cooled EGR, Anti Backfire Valve] Y2 --> Z2[Cause: Hot Spots
Fix: Cooled Pistons, Oil Jets] Y3 --> Z3[Cause: End Gas Auto Ignition
Fix: Reduce Compression Ratio, Lean] Y4 --> Z4[Cause: Energy Below Ignition
Fix: Limit Lambda Range] style X1 fill:#ffe1e1 style Y1 fill:#fff4e1 style Y2 fill:#f4e1ff style Y3 fill:#e1f5ff style Y4 fill:#e1ffe1 ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Explain why hydrogen has a very wide range of flammability limits and discuss its significance in engine operation.** **Model Answer:** Hydrogen's flammability limits in air range from **4% to 75% by volume**, which is significantly wider than gasoline (1.4 – 7.6%) and methane (5 – 15%). This wide range is attributed to: 1. **Very low minimum ignition energy (0.02 mJ)** — even a weak spark or hot surface can initiate combustion. 2. **High diffusivity of H₂ in air** — promotes rapid mixing and uniform composition. 3. **Simple diatomic molecular structure** — fewer reaction intermediates, faster chain-branching kinetics. **Significance in engine operation:** - Allows **extensive lean-burn operation** (λ from 1.2 to 5.0), improving thermal efficiency. - Permits stable combustion at very low loads. - However, also leads to **backfire** risk in SI engines because any residual H₂ in the intake manifold can ignite. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **What is hydrogen backfire? State two methods to prevent it in an SI engine.** **Model Answer:** **Hydrogen Backfire** is the abnormal combustion phenomenon in which a hydrogen–air mixture ignites in the *intake manifold* and propagates back through the intake valve into the intake system, often producing a loud popping sound and potential damage. **Causes:** Residual hot gases in the cylinder after exhaust, residual H₂ from the previous cycle, hot intake valve, late intake valve closing. **Prevention Methods:** 1. **Anti-backfire valve** in the intake manifold (one-way valve that prevents flame propagation). 2. **Cooled exhaust gas recirculation (EGR)** to reduce residual gas temperature. 3. **Optimized valve timing** with early intake valve closing. 4. **Water injection or cooled intake air** to lower mixture temperature. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice Format --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]** **Q. (a)** Discuss in detail the engine modifications required to operate a conventional gasoline SI engine on hydrogen fuel. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** Explain the combustion characteristics of hydrogen in SI engines and compare them with gasoline. **(7 marks)** #### Solution to Q. A (a): The major modifications required to convert a gasoline SI engine to hydrogen are: 1. **Fuel Storage and Delivery System:** - Replacement of liquid fuel tank with **high-pressure composite hydrogen tanks (350 or 700 bar)**. - **Pressure regulators** to reduce pressure to 5–10 bar. - **Hydrogen-compatible fuel lines** (stainless steel or approved polymers) to prevent **hydrogen embrittlement**. 2. **Fuel Injection System:** - **Timed port fuel injection (PFI)** or **direct injection (DI)** of gaseous hydrogen. - Use of materials with low hydrogen permeability. - Modified injectors with tight tolerances to prevent leakage. 3. **Ignition System Upgrade:** - **High-energy ignition coil** (50–100 kV output) to ensure reliable kernel formation. - Multiple spark plug or **plasma jet ignition** options for leaner mixtures. 4. **Combustion Chamber Modifications:** - **Compact combustion chamber** to minimize surface quenching (H₂ has a quenching distance of only 0.64 mm). - **Centralized spark plug location** for short, uniform flame travel. - **Reduced compression ratio** (from ~10:1 to ~8:1) to prevent pre-ignition. 5. **Cold Start System:** - **Heated intake air** or glow plug–assisted starting to vaporize any residual moisture and assist cold combustion. 6. **Lubrication and Crankcase Ventilation:** - Use of **H₂-resistant lubricants**. - **Blow-by gas ventilation** to remove leaked H₂ (otherwise forms explosive mixture in crankcase). 7. **Electronic Control Unit (ECU):** - Dedicated **H₂ injection timing and duration maps**. - **Lambda feedback control** for wide-range air–fuel ratio. - **Pre-ignition detection** via ion-sense or knock sensors. #### Solution to Q. A (b): **Combustion Characteristics of Hydrogen in SI Engines:** | Property | Hydrogen | Gasoline | Effect on Engine | |----------|----------|----------|------------------| | Flame speed (m/s) | 2.5 – 3.5 | 0.4 | Rapid, near-instantaneous combustion → higher peak pressure | | Quenching distance (mm) | 0.64 | 2.0 | Prone to surface quenching; requires compact chamber | | Flammability (vol %) | 4 – 75 | 1.4 – 7.6 | Excellent lean-burn capability | | Min. ignition energy (mJ) | 0.02 | 0.25 | Easy to ignite → backfire risk | | Auto-ignition T (°C) | 571 | 280 | Highly knock-resistant, but causes pre-ignition on hot surfaces | | Octane number (RON) | > 130 | 88 – 98 | Permits higher compression and lean operation | | Adiabatic flame T (K) | ~2400 | ~2300 | Higher → more NOx | **Key Combustion Behaviors:** - **Lean Burn Dominance:** Hydrogen engines typically operate at λ = 1.5 – 3.0, sometimes up to 5.0. This lowers peak flame temperature and **NOx**, while improving part-load efficiency. - **High Flame Speed:** The premixed flame consumes the charge in a very short duration (often < 30° crank angle), resulting in a steep pressure rise. - **Pre-ignition and Backfire:** Caused by hot residual gases or surface ignition sources. - **Cycle-to-Cycle Variability:** Higher than gasoline at very lean limits; requires robust closed-loop control. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model — Alternative Choice]** **Q. (a)** Why is pure hydrogen difficult to use in CI engines? What are the various engine modification methods adopted to enable hydrogen operation? **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** Explain the concept of **hydrogen fumigation** and **dual-fuel mode** in CI engines, with neat block diagrams. Discuss their advantages and limitations. **(7 marks)** #### Solution to Q. B (a): **Why Pure Hydrogen is Difficult in CI Engines:** 1. **Very High Auto-Ignition Temperature (571 °C):** Even at compression ratios of 18:1, end-of-compression temperatures in a diesel engine (~600–700 °C in some regions) barely reach the auto-ignition threshold of H₂, leading to **unreliable or no self-ignition**. 2. **Negligible Cetane Number:** Hydrogen has no paraffinic or aromatic structure to support auto-ignition chemistry. 3. **Very Short Quenching Distance (0.64 mm):** The H₂–air mixture quenches easily near cold cylinder walls, preventing reliable initiation. 4. **Wide Flammability but at High Temperature:** Although H₂ can burn over a wide range, ignition must first be initiated by a hot source. **Engine Modifications Required:** 1. **Dual-Fuel Mode:** A small amount (5–10%) of **diesel pilot** is injected near TDC to provide the ignition source for the hydrogen–air mixture. The ECU controls the diesel substitution rate. 2. **Hydrogen Fumigation:** H₂ is inducted with intake air, often via a mixer/carburetor. A small diesel pilot ignites the premixed charge. 3. **Glow Plug / Hot Surface Assist:** Electrically heated glow plugs raise local temperatures to trigger H₂ combustion. 4. **Ignition Improver Additives:** Trace amounts of dimethyl ether (DME) or other high-cetane additives enhance ignition. 5. **Modified Injection System:** The pilot injector is retained; H₂ supply line and mixer are added. 6. **Reduced Compression Ratio:** Slight reduction (from 18:1 to 14:1) to limit mechanical stress in absence of full diesel load. 7. **Heated Intake Air:** To improve H₂–air mixing and reduce ignition delay. #### Solution to Q.
