B (b): **Hydrogen Fumigation:** - Hydrogen is introduced into the intake air stream, typically through a **fumigation mixer or venturi** mounted upstream of the intake manifold. - The H₂–air mixture enters the cylinder in a homogeneous premixed state. - A **pilot injection of diesel (5–10% of total energy)** provides the ignition source near TDC. - The diesel auto-ignites → initiates combustion of the surrounding H₂–air cloud. **Dual-Fuel Mode (Hydrogen–Diesel):** - Conceptually similar to fumigation, but with **precise ECU control** of H₂ substitution rate (typically 60–90% energy from H₂). - The pilot diesel mass is fixed; the H₂ quantity is varied to match load. - May employ **port injection of H₂** instead of fumigation for finer control. **Advantages:** - Retains existing diesel block (lower conversion cost). - Substantial reduction in diesel consumption → lower CO₂. - Lower NOx in some operating zones due to lean premixed H₂ burn. - High thermal efficiency in mid-load range. **Limitations:** - Difficult cold start (no diesel at low T → no ignition). - Reduced power density at high loads due to lower H₂ energy density. - HC and unburned H₂ emissions higher at low loads. - Knock-like roughness during combustion transitions. - Limited maximum load due to knock/pressure-rise constraints. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often **confuse fumigation with port injection**. Fumigation introduces H₂ with intake air; port injection times H₂ into each port. > - Do NOT state that hydrogen "auto-ignites" in CI engines — it does not, except with assistance. > - Forgetting to mention the **role of pilot diesel** in dual-fuel mode costs 2–3 marks. > - Do not confuse **backfire (SI)** with **misfire (CI dual-fuel)**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - Hydrogen is an **energy carrier**, not a primary energy source; LHV = **120 MJ/kg**, HHV = **142 MJ/kg**. - Stoichiometric A/F ratio = **34.3 : 1** (much higher than gasoline's 14.7 : 1). - Auto-ignition temperature = **571 °C** → CI engine operation is **very difficult** without pilot fuel. - Laminar flame speed = **2.5–3.5 m/s** → rapid pressure rise in SI mode. - Flammability range = **4 – 75%** in air → excellent lean-burn capability, but also **backfire risk**. - Minimum ignition energy = **0.02 mJ** → very easy to ignite unintentionally. - Quenching distance = **0.64 mm** → requires **compact combustion chamber**. - Octane number > 130 → permits **low compression ratio** (~8:1) in SI mode. - Adiabatic flame temperature ≈ **2400 K** → primary cause of high **NOx** emissions. - SI engine modifications: H₂ tanks, regulators, injectors, **high-energy ignition**, compact chamber, **anti-backfire valve**, cooled EGR, ECU remap. - CI engine modifications: **dual-fuel mode** with diesel pilot, fumigation mixer, glow plug, heated intake, ECU dual-fuel maps. - Lean burn (λ = 1.5 – 5.0) is the **primary strategy** to control NOx in H₂ engines. - Pre-ignition, backfire, and knock are the **three main abnormal combustion** issues in H₂ SI engines. - Volumetric efficiency loss of **15–30%** is recovered by **turbocharging / direct injection**. - Cold start is a critical issue: requires **heated intake** or **glow plug assist**. - Hydrogen **embrittlement** mandates use of **stainless steel, aluminium, or composite** materials. - Crankcase ventilation is mandatory to remove leaked H₂ (forms explosive mixture).

topics.title: Dual Fuelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT741 - HYDROGEN ENGINES AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dual Fuelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Dual Fuelling** is a combustion strategy employed in **Internal Combustion Engines (ICE)** wherein **two distinct fuels are simultaneously inducted, injected, or combusted** in a controlled manner within the same combustion chamber during a single engine cycle. One fuel acts as the **primary energy carrier** (bulk fuel, usually a gaseous fuel such as hydrogen, CNG, LPG, biogas, or producer gas), while the second fuel, conventionally a **liquid pilot fuel** (diesel, biodiesel, or a small quantity of gasoline), is used to provide the **ignition source** and to ensure stable, controlled combustion.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, the focus is specifically on **hydrogen-based dual fuelling**, where hydrogen (a gaseous, low-density fuel with high auto-ignition temperature) is introduced as a supplementary or principal fuel into a compression-ignition (diesel) engine that retains a small diesel pilot injection for ignition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** "Dual fuelling is a technique in which a primary gaseous fuel (such as hydrogen, CNG, or LPG) is inducted along with air, while a small quantity of a liquid pilot fuel (typically diesel) is injected near top dead centre to initiate combustion. This allows engines originally designed for diesel operation to operate on cleaner gaseous fuels with minimal hardware modification."

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Starter-and-Main-Course" Model

Imagine you are hosting a dinner where the main course is a **gaseous fuel that refuses to light on its own** (hydrogen has a very high auto-ignition temperature of approximately **585 °C** and a very wide flammability range, but requires an ignition source in a compression-ignition environment). The **main course is hydrogen**, which carries most of the energy, but it cannot begin cooking (combusting) by itself.

The **appetizer/starter** is a tiny squirt of **diesel pilot fuel**. This pilot fuel has low auto-ignition temperature (~250 °C) and auto-ignites due to the high in-cylinder temperature created by compression. The diesel pilot then acts like a **matchstick or pilot flame** that lights the hydrogen-air mixture, which then combusts rapidly and releases the bulk of the energy.

Just as a small matchstick can light a massive gas burner, a **small fraction of diesel** (typically **5 % to 25 % of total energy input**) can ignite a **large quantity of hydrogen**, achieving engine operation primarily on hydrogen while still using diesel's superior auto-ignition characteristics.

### 1.3 Key Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> **Essential KTU 2024 Glossary Terms:**
> - **Pilot Fuel:** The small liquid fuel charge (usually diesel) injected to initiate combustion in a dual-fuel engine.
> - **Primary Fuel / Main Fuel:** The bulk fuel, generally gaseous (H₂, CNG, LPG), that provides most of the energy.
> - **Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR):** The ratio of pilot fuel energy to the total energy input. Lower ESR means more substitution of diesel by the gaseous fuel.
> - **Hydrogen Energy Fraction (HEF):** The fraction of total input energy contributed by hydrogen.
> - **PPCI (Premixed Premixed Combustion Ignition):** The combustion phase dominated by premixed pilot fuel.
> - **Premixed Combustion Duration (PCD):** The duration of the premixed hydrogen-air combustion phase.

### 1.4 Standard Physical Constants (Bolded for Emphasis)

- **Auto-ignition temperature of hydrogen:** **585 °C**
- **Flammability limits of hydrogen in air:** **4 % to 75 %** (by volume) — extremely wide.
- **Minimum ignition energy of hydrogen:** **0.02 mJ** (about 1/10 of gasoline)
- **Diffusion coefficient of hydrogen:** ~**6.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** (very high — mixes rapidly with air)
- **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of hydrogen:** **120 MJ/kg**
- **LHV of diesel (approx.):** **42.5 MJ/kg**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The dual-fuelling process can be broken down into the following structured sequence:

1. **Air Induction (Intake Stroke):**
   - Pure air (or a premixed air + hydrogen mixture) is inducted into the cylinder during the intake stroke.
   - In the **fumigation mode**, hydrogen is admitted into the intake manifold and mixed with incoming air.
   - In the **port-injection mode**, hydrogen is injected into the intake port timed to the intake event.

2. **Compression (Compression Stroke):**
   - The air-hydrogen mixture is compressed to a high pressure and temperature.
   - Compression ratio is typically retained at diesel levels (16:1 to 18:1) for proper auto-ignition of the pilot fuel.
   - Because hydrogen has a very high auto-ignition temperature, it does NOT self-ignite during compression.

3. **Pilot Injection (Near TDC):**
   - A small, precisely metered quantity of diesel is injected by the existing diesel injection system a few degrees before TDC.
   - This diesel auto-ignites due to the high compression temperature.

4. **Combustion Initiation:**
   - The diesel pilot flame kernel acts as multiple ignition points.
   - Hydrogen-air mixture surrounding the diesel spray ignites due to its very low minimum ignition energy (**0.02 mJ**).

5. **Premixed and Diffusion Combustion Phases:**
   - A rapid **premixed hydrogen combustion** occurs (the bulk of hydrogen burns quickly).
   - Followed by a **diffusion-controlled combustion** of the pilot diesel spray.
   - Heat release rate shows a characteristic **two-stage profile**.

6. **Expansion and Power Stroke:**
   - Combustion gases expand, producing work on the piston.

7. **Exhaust:**
   - Exhaust gases, including unburned hydrocarbons, CO, NOₓ, and water vapour, are expelled.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Dual Fuelling:**
> - Hydrogen is clean (produces only H₂O), but difficult to handle in pure compression-ignition mode.
> - Diesel engines are robust and have high thermal efficiency, but pollute.
> - Dual fuelling **combines the high efficiency and durability of diesel with the cleanliness of hydrogen**.

### 2.2 Modes of Hydrogen Dual Fuelling

| Mode | Description | Hydrogen Entry Point | Mixing |
|------|-------------|----------------------|--------|
| **Fumigation** | Hydrogen is drawn in through the intake manifold under slight pressure | Intake manifold | External (premixed) |
| **Port Injection** | Hydrogen injected into the intake port via solenoid/injector | Intake port | External |
| **Direct Injection (HCNG DI)** | Hydrogen injected directly into the cylinder near TDC | Combustion chamber | Internal (in-cylinder mixing) |
| **Manifold Carburetion** | Hydrogen metered through a carburettor-type device | Intake manifold | External |

### 2.3 Performance Parameters Affected

- **Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE):** Generally comparable to or slightly higher than pure diesel at optimal hydrogen energy fractions.
- **Brake Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC):** Reduces because hydrogen carries more energy per kg.
- **NOₓ Emissions:** **Increase** substantially (hydrogen's high adiabatic flame temperature ~2400 K promotes thermal NOₓ).
- **HC and CO Emissions:** Generally **decrease** due to better mixing and complete combustion.
- **Smoke and Particulate Matter:** Drastically **reduce** (almost eliminated) due to absence of carbon in hydrogen.
- **CO₂ Emissions:** Reduce proportional to the diesel displacement fraction.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are exam-critical. Memorize the symbol definitions carefully.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR) | $ESR = \dfrac{m_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel}}{m_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel} \;+\; m_{H_2} \cdot CV_{H_2}}$ | dimensionless | Pilot fuel energy / Total energy input |
| 2 | Hydrogen Energy Fraction (HEF) | $HEF = 1 - ESR$ | dimensionless | Fraction of energy from hydrogen |
| 3 | Equivalent Diesel Mass | $m_{eq} = m_{diesel} + m_{H_2} \cdot \dfrac{CV_{H_2}}{CV_{diesel}}$ | kg | Converts hydrogen energy to diesel equivalent |
| 4 | Brake Thermal Efficiency (Dual Fuel) | $\eta_{th,DF} = \dfrac{BP \cdot 3600}{\dot{m}_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel} \;+\; \dot{m}_{H_2} \cdot CV_{H_2}}$ | fraction or % | BP in kW; $\dot{m}$ in kg/s; CV in kJ/kg |
| 5 | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC) | $BSFC_{DF} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{diesel} \;+\; \dot{m}_{H_2} \cdot (CV_{H_2}/CV_{diesel})}{BP}$ | kg/kWh | Use equivalent diesel mass |
| 6 | Brake Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC) | $BSEC_{DF} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel} \;+\; \dot{m}_{H_2} \cdot CV_{H_2}}{BP}$ | MJ/kWh | Energy-based metric |
| 7 | Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP) | $IMEP = \dfrac{W_{indicated}}{V_{s}}$ | Pa or bar | $V_s$ = swept volume |
| 8 | Volumetric Efficiency (Dual Fuel) | $\eta_{vol} = \dfrac{m_{a,actual}}{\rho_{a,ambient} \cdot V_{s}}$ | fraction | Reduced in fumigation mode due to H₂ displacement |
| 9 | Air-Fuel Ratio (Stoichiometric, mass basis) | $(A/F)_{st} = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}}$ | dimensionless | For H₂: ~34.3; for diesel: ~14.5 |
| 10 | Hydrogen Mass Flow Rate (Energy Balance) | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{HEF \cdot \dot{Q}_{in}}{CV_{H_2}}$ | kg/s | $\dot{Q}_{in}$ = total heat input rate |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values and conditionals in formulas use `\vert` (LaTeX) or are written with clear textual labels like "modulus" to avoid breaking the markdown table pipe structure.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Stationary Power Generation:** Natural gas-diesel dual fuel gensets are widely used in remote areas and oil-field operations.
- **Heavy-Duty Trucks:** Companies like Cummins and Westport offer production HPDI (High-Pressure Direct Injection) natural gas-diesel dual fuel systems.
- **Maritime Applications:** Dual fuel engines (LNG + diesel) are now standard for large container ships (e.g., MAN B&W ME-GI).
- **Hydrogen Economy Transition:** Dual fuelling acts as a **bridge technology** between fossil-only and hydrogen-only engines, allowing gradual introduction of hydrogen without requiring a complete engine redesign.
- **Agricultural Tractors:** Dual fuel retrofits allow farmers to use biogas produced on-site with diesel pilot ignition.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR) and Hydrogen Energy Fraction (HEF)

**Given:**
- Mass of pilot diesel injected per cycle: $m_{diesel}$ (kg/cycle)
- Mass of hydrogen inducted per cycle: $m_{H_2}$ (kg/cycle)
- Calorific value of diesel: $CV_{diesel}$ (kJ/kg)
- Calorific value of hydrogen: $CV_{H_2}$ (kJ/kg)

**To Derive:** Expressions for ESR and HEF.

**Step 1 — Energy contributed by pilot diesel fuel per cycle:**

$$E_{diesel} = m_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel}$$

**Explanation:** The energy released by complete combustion of the pilot diesel is the product of its mass and its calorific value. This is the standard energy-mass relationship used throughout thermodynamics of engines.

**Step 2 — Energy contributed by hydrogen per cycle:**

$$E_{H_2} = m_{H_2} \cdot CV_{H_2}$$

**Explanation:** Similarly, the energy released by complete combustion of hydrogen is its mass times its calorific value. Note that hydrogen's CV is approximately **120,000 kJ/kg**, vastly greater than diesel's ~**42,500 kJ/kg**.

**Step 3 — Total input energy per cycle:**

$$E_{total} = E_{diesel} + E_{H_2} = m_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel} + m_{H_2} \cdot CV_{H_2}$$

**Explanation:** The total energy input to the combustion chamber is the sum of energies from both fuels, assuming complete combustion of each (a standard assumption for ideal energy balance).

**Step 4 — Definition of Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR):**

By definition, ESR is the fraction of total energy supplied by the pilot diesel.

$$ESR = \frac{E_{diesel}}{E_{total}}$$

**Step 5 — Substituting expressions from Step 1 and Step 3:**

$$ESR = \frac{m_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel}}{m_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel} + m_{H_2} \cdot CV_{H_2}}$$

**Explanation:** This is the working formula. Numerator is the diesel energy; denominator is the total. A value of ESR = 0.20 means diesel provides 20 % of the input energy, with hydrogen providing 80 %.

**Step 6 — Definition of Hydrogen Energy Fraction (HEF):**

By conservation of energy, the sum of all energy fractions equals 1.

$$HEF + ESR = 1$$

Therefore:

$$HEF = 1 - ESR = \frac{m_{H_2} \cdot CV_{H_2}}{m_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel} + m_{H_2} \cdot CV_{H_2}}$$

**Explanation:** HEF is a direct measure of how much of the engine's energy comes from hydrogen. In a well-designed hydrogen dual fuel engine, HEF values of 0.75 to 0.95 are typical.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Complete Dual Fuel Engine Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU 2024 Style):**
A single-cylinder, four-stroke hydrogen-diesel dual fuel engine develops **5 kW** of brake power at **1500 rpm**. The hydrogen energy fraction is **0.80** (i.e., HEF = 80 %). The calorific value of diesel is **42,500 kJ/kg** and of hydrogen is **120,000 kJ/kg**. The engine consumes **1.2 kg/h** of diesel in this mode. Calculate:
(a) The mass flow rate of hydrogen.
(b) The Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR).
(c) The Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE).
(d) The Brake Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC) in MJ/kWh.

**Solution — Step-by-Step:**

**Given Data:**
- Brake Power, $BP = 5$ kW
- Speed, $N = 1500$ rpm
- $HEF = 0.80$, so $ESR = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20$
- $CV_{diesel} = 42,500$ kJ/kg
- $CV_{H_2} = 120,000$ kJ/kg
- $\dot{m}_{diesel} = 1.2$ kg/h

**Part (a): Mass flow rate of hydrogen**

**Step 1:** Convert diesel mass flow rate to kg/s.

$$\dot{m}_{diesel} = \frac{1.2}{3600} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2:** Compute energy input rate from diesel.

$$\dot{Q}_{diesel} = \dot{m}_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{diesel} = 3.333 \times 10^{-4} \times 42{,}500 = 14.167 \text{ kW}$$

**Explanation:** Multiplying mass flow (kg/s) by calorific value (kJ/kg) gives kJ/s, which is equivalent to kW.

**Step 3:** Use the HEF definition to find hydrogen energy rate.

Since $HEF = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{H_2}}{\dot{Q}_{total}}$ and $ESR = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{diesel}}{\dot{Q}_{total}}$, the ratio gives:

$$\frac{\dot{Q}_{H_2}}{\dot{Q}_{diesel}} = \frac{HEF}{ESR} = \frac{0.80}{0.20} = 4.0$$

Therefore:

$$\dot{Q}_{H_2} = 4.0 \times 14.167 = 56.667 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the hydrogen mass flow rate.

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{H_2}}{CV_{H_2}} = \frac{56.667}{120{,}000}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 4.722 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

Converting to kg/h:

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = 4.722 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 1.7 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Part (a) Answer:** $\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.7$ kg/h

---

**Part (b): Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR)**

$$ESR = 1 - HEF = 1 - 0.80 = 0.20$$

**Part (b) Answer:** $ESR = 0.20$ (or 20 %)

---

**Part (c): Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE)**

**Step 1:** Total energy input rate.

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{Q}_{diesel} + \dot{Q}_{H_2} = 14.167 + 56.667 = 70.834 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 2:** Apply BTE formula.

$$\eta_{th,DF} = \frac{BP}{\dot{Q}_{total}} = \frac{5}{70.834}$$

$$\eta_{th,DF} = 0.0706 = 7.06\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Sanity Check Alert:** This BTE is unusually LOW (7 %). In a real engine, BTE for dual fuel mode is typically **25 % to 35 %**. The low value here suggests the problem is hypothetical or the given BP is not the only output. In KTU exams, ALWAYS check the units and dimensional consistency before submitting.

**Part (c) Answer:** $\eta_{th,DF} \approx 7.06\%$ (using stated values; in practice, real dual fuel engines achieve 28–35 %).

---

**Part (d): Brake Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC)**

**Step 1:** Total energy input per hour.

$$E_{total,hour} = \dot{Q}_{total} \times 3600 \text{ s} = 70.834 \times 3600 = 255{,}002 \text{ kJ/h}$$

**Step 2:** Apply BSEC formula.

$$BSEC = \frac{E_{total,hour}}{BP_{kWh}}$$

**Step 3:** Compute kWh of brake work per hour.

$$BP_{kWh} = 5 \text{ kW} \times 1 \text{ h} = 5 \text{ kWh} = 5 \times 3.6 = 18{,}000 \text{ kJ}$$

**Step 4:** Compute BSEC.

$$BSEC = \frac{255{,}002}{5} = 51{,}000 \text{ kJ/kWh} = 51 \text{ MJ/kWh}$$

**Part (d) Answer:** $BSEC = 51$ MJ/kWh

**Realistic benchmark value:** For actual engines, BSEC is typically **11–14 MJ/kWh**. The high value confirms the same dimensional/realism issue noted in part (c).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Hydrogen Energy Fraction on Performance

| HEF (Hydrogen Energy Fraction) | BTE Trend | NOₓ Trend | HC/CO Trend | Smoke | Combustion Stability |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 % (Pure Diesel) | Baseline | Moderate | Moderate | High | Excellent |
| 20 % | Slight increase | +10 % | −20 % | −40 % | Excellent |
| 40 % | Slight increase | +25 % | −40 % | −60 % | Good |
| 60 % | Peak/Plateau | +50 % | −60 % | −80 % | Acceptable |
| 80 % | Slight decrease | +80 % | −75 % | −95 % | Marginal (knock/misfire risk) |
| 100 % (Theoretical) | N/A | N/A | N/A | N/A | Impossible without external ignition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Increasing hydrogen content **always increases NOₓ** (because hydrogen's adiabatic flame temperature is much higher than diesel's). Hydrogen dual fuel engines therefore require **NOₓ after-treatment** (SCR, EGR, or LNT) for emissions compliance.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Hydrogen-Diesel Dual Fuel Engine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H2Tank[Hydrogen Storage Tank] --> H2PR[Hydrogen Pressure Regulator]
    H2PR --> H2MV[Hydrogen Mixing Valve]
    H2MV --> IntakeMan[Hydrogen Carburettor or Port Injector]
    IntakeMan --> IntakePort[Intake Manifold Air-H2 Premix]
    IntakePort --> Cyl[Combustion Chamber Cylinder]
    AirIn[Ambient Air Filter] --> IntakeMan
    DieselTank[Diesel Fuel Tank] --> DieselPump[High Pressure Diesel Pump]
    DieselPump --> DieselInj[Diesel Injector]
    DieselInj --> Cyl
    Cyl --> PistonMotion[Piston Power Stroke]
    PistonMotion --> Crank[Crankshaft Output Brake Power]
    Cyl --> ExhaustMan[Exhaust Manifold]
    ExhaustMan --> EmSys[Emissions and After Treatment]
    EmSys --> TailPipe[Tailpipe H2O CO2 NOx]
    Cyl -.combustion heat release.-> CombMon[Combustion Pressure Sensor]
    CombMon --> ECU[Engine Control Unit ECU]
    ECU -.H2 mass flow command.-> H2MV
    ECU -.pilot timing command.-> DieselInj
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- **Left column:** Fuel supply paths (hydrogen and diesel).
- **Center:** Cylinder is the focal point where dual combustion occurs.
- **Bottom feedback loop:** ECU monitors combustion and adjusts both hydrogen and diesel flow for stable operation.
- **Right column:** Mechanical output and emissions handling.

### 4.2 Process Flow — Combustion Phases in a Dual Fuel Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Intake Stroke: Air + H2 Induction] --> B[Compression Stroke: Adiabatic Compression]
    B --> C[Pilot Diesel Injection 8 to 12 deg BTDC]
    C --> D[Pilot Auto Ignition: Diesel Flame Kernels Form]
    D --> E[Premixed H2 Combustion: Rapid Pressure Rise]
    E --> F[Diffusion Combustion: Pilot Diesel Spray Burns]
    F --> G[Expansion Stroke: Work on Piston]
    G --> H[Exhaust Stroke: Emissions Out]
    H --> A
    style A fill:#cce5ff
    style B fill:#cce5ff
    style C fill:#ffe0b3
    style D fill:#ffcccc
    style E fill:#ff9999
    style F fill:#ffcc99
    style G fill:#cce5ff
    style H fill:#cce5ff
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- The **red-shaded stages (D, E, F)** are the combustion phases — the heart of dual fuel operation.
- **C** marks the pilot injection event that triggers the entire combustion sequence.
- The **two distinct heat release peaks** correspond to the premixed H₂ burn (E) and the diffusion-controlled diesel burn (F).

### 4.3 Energy Distribution Sankey-style Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EIn[Total Chemical Energy Input: Q_total] --> E1[Diesel Energy Q_d 20 percent]
    EIn --> E2[Hydrogen Energy Q_H2 80 percent]
    E1 --> P1[Diesel Combustion Work]
    E2 --> P2[Hydrogen Combustion Work]
    P1 --> WOut[Brake Work Output: BP]
    P2 --> WOut
    WOut --> Eff[Useful Mechanical Work 30 percent of input]
    E1 --> Loss1[Heat Losses 70 percent]
    E2 --> Loss2[Heat Losses 70 percent]
    Loss1 --> Wall[Wall Heat Transfer]
    Loss1 --> Exh[Exhaust Energy]
    Loss2 --> Wall
    Loss2 --> Exh
```

**Diagram Interpretation:**
- Shows how the combined energy input is split between useful work and losses.
- A typical dual fuel engine converts only about **25–35 %** of input energy to brake work, with the rest lost to exhaust and wall heat transfer.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
> *Define dual fuelling. List any two advantages and two disadvantages of hydrogen-diesel dual fuel engines.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Dual fuelling is a combustion strategy in which two fuels are simultaneously used in an engine — a primary gaseous fuel (hydrogen) and a small liquid pilot fuel (diesel). The pilot diesel auto-ignites due to compression and serves as the ignition source for the main hydrogen-air mixture.

**Advantages:**
1. **Significant reduction in smoke and particulate emissions** because hydrogen contains no carbon, displacing the carbon-based diesel combustion.
2. **Higher brake thermal efficiency at moderate hydrogen energy fractions** (HEF 40–70 %) due to faster, more complete combustion of hydrogen.
3. Easy retrofitting into existing diesel engines without major hardware changes.
4. Reduced dependence on petroleum diesel.

**Disadvantages:**
1. **Increased NOₓ emissions** due to hydrogen's higher adiabatic flame temperature (~2400 K) promoting thermal NOₓ formation.
2. **Combustion instability at high hydrogen fractions (HEF > 80 %)** causing pressure oscillations, knock, or misfire.
3. Onboard hydrogen storage challenges (low density, high-pressure tanks).
4. Hydrogen embrittlement of metallic engine components.

> **Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 1]**
> *What is the Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR)? A dual fuel engine has a hydrogen mass flow rate of 0.4 kg/h and a diesel mass flow rate of 0.8 kg/h. Calculate the ESR, given $CV_{H_2} = 120$ MJ/kg and $CV_{diesel} = 42.5$ MJ/kg.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR) is the ratio of the energy supplied by the pilot (diesel) fuel to the total energy input in a dual fuel engine.

**Formula:**

$$ESR = \frac{m_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel}}{m_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel} + m_{H_2} \cdot CV_{H_2}}$$

**Calculation:**

$$E_{diesel} = 0.8 \times 42.5 = 34.0 \text{ MJ/h}$$

$$E_{H_2} = 0.4 \times 120 = 48.0 \text{ MJ/h}$$

$$ESR = \frac{34.0}{34.0 + 48.0} = \frac{34.0}{82.0} = 0.4146$$

**Answer:** $ESR \approx 0.415$ (i.e., 41.5 % of input energy is from diesel pilot fuel).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split as **Part (a) for 7 marks** and **Part (b) for 7 marks**, with **internal choice** between Question A or Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a hydrogen-diesel dual fuel engine. Discuss the role of the pilot fuel and the various modes of hydrogen induction (fumigation, port injection, and direct injection).

**(b) [7 Marks]** A hydrogen-diesel dual fuel engine consumes 1.5 kg/h of hydrogen and 0.6 kg/h of diesel. The brake power developed is 8 kW. Given $CV_{H_2} = 120$ MJ/kg and $CV_{diesel} = 42.5$ MJ/kg, calculate:
(i) The Energy Substitution Ratio (ESR)
(ii) The Hydrogen Energy Fraction (HEF)
(iii) The Brake Thermal Efficiency (BTE)
(iv) The Brake Specific Energy Consumption (BSEC) in MJ/kWh

**Model Solution for Question A:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle and Modes of Hydrogen Induction:**

**Working Principle (Block Diagram Description):**
A hydrogen-diesel dual fuel engine is essentially a modified compression-ignition (CI) engine. The diesel injection system is retained for pilot injection, while a hydrogen supply system is added. The functional block diagram consists of:

1. **Hydrogen storage and supply** (high-pressure tank, pressure regulator, solenoid valves).
2. **Hydrogen induction system** (carburettor / port injector / direct injector depending on mode).
3. **Diesel pilot injection system** (existing fuel pump, injector, high-pressure lines).
4. **Combustion chamber** (cylinder, piston).
5. **Control unit (ECU)** to coordinate both fuel systems.

During the intake stroke, air (and hydrogen, if fumigation or port injection is used) enters the cylinder. During the compression stroke, the mixture is compressed. Near TDC, a small diesel pilot dose is injected, which auto-ignites. The diesel flames ignite the surrounding hydrogen-air mixture, causing rapid premixed combustion of hydrogen followed by diffusion combustion of the pilot spray.

**Role of Pilot Fuel:**
- Acts as the **ignition source** since hydrogen has a very high auto-ignition temperature (585 °C).
- Provides **combustion stability** at low loads.
- Maintains **cold-start capability** of the engine.
- Prevents **hydrogen backfire** into the intake manifold by ensuring rapid, controlled ignition.

**Modes of Hydrogen Induction:**

| Mode | Description | Advantages | Disadvantages |
|------|-------------|------------|---------------|
| **Fumigation** | H₂ admitted into intake manifold under low pressure; mixes with air externally | Simple, low-cost retrofit | Non-uniform mixture, backfire risk |
| **Port Injection** | H₂ injected into intake port close to intake valve | Better mixture control, less backfire | Requires timed injectors, sealing issues |
| **Direct Injection (HCNG-DI)** | H₂ injected directly into cylinder near TDC | Best mixture control, no backfire, high power | Complex high-pressure injectors, sealing problems |

**(Marks Distribution Guidance: 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for working, 2 marks for modes comparison)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Given Data:**
- $\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.5$ kg/h
- $\dot{m}_{diesel} = 0.6$ kg/h
- $BP = 8$ kW
- $CV_{H_2} = 120$ MJ/kg = 120,000 kJ/kg
- $CV_{diesel} = 42.5$ MJ/kg = 42,500 kJ/kg

**Step 1: Convert mass flow rates to kg/s:**

$$\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{1.5}{3600} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

$$\dot{m}_{diesel} = \frac{0.6}{3600} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2: Compute energy input rates:**

$$\dot{Q}_{H_2} = 4.167 \times 10^{-4} \times 120{,}000 = 50.0 \text{ kW}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{diesel} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4} \times 42{,}500 = 7.083 \text{ kW}$$

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = 50.0 + 7.083 = 57.083 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 (i): Calculate ESR:**

$$ESR = \frac{\dot{Q}_{diesel}}{\dot{Q}_{total}} = \frac{7.083}{57.083} = 0.1241$$

**Answer (i):** $ESR = 0.1241$ or **12.41 %**

**Step 4 (ii): Calculate HEF:**

$$HEF = 1 - ESR = 1 - 0.1241 = 0.8759$$

**Answer (ii):** $HEF = 0.8759$ or **87.59 %**

**Step 5 (iii): Calculate BTE:**

$$\eta_{th,DF} = \frac{BP}{\dot{Q}_{total}} = \frac{8}{57.083} = 0.1402 = 14.02\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Note:** This BTE is low (14 %). In a real engine, BTE of dual fuel mode is typically **25–35 %**. This indicates the given fuel consumption is high relative to power output, or the problem is a textbook simplification. The calculation method is correct; the values reflect the problem statement.

**Answer (iii):** $\eta_{th,DF} = 14.02\%$

**Step 6 (iv): Calculate BSEC:**

$$BSEC = \frac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{BP} = \frac{57.083}{8} = 7.135 \text{ kW/kW}$$

Wait — correction. BSEC is energy per kWh of work. The calculation above gives kW input per kW output, not kJ/kWh. Correct procedure:

$$BSEC = \frac{\dot{Q}_{total} \times 3600 \text{ s}}{BP \times 3600 \text{ s}} = \frac{\dot{Q}_{total} \text{ (kJ/s)}}{\text{BP (kJ/s)}}$$

But this gives a ratio. The proper BSEC in kJ/kWh is:

$$BSEC = \frac{\dot{Q}_{total} \text{ (kJ/s)} \times 3600 \text{ s}}{BP \text{ (kW)} \times 1 \text{ h}}$$

$$BSEC = \frac{57.083 \times 3600}{8 \times 1} = \frac{205{,}500}{8} = 25{,}687 \text{ kJ/kWh} = 25.69 \text{ MJ/kWh}$$

**Answer (iv):** $BSEC = 25.69$ MJ/kWh

**(Marks Distribution Guidance: 1 mark each for i, ii, iv; 2 marks for iii; 2 marks for setup and unit conversions)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice for Internal Selection]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare hydrogen dual fuelling with neat hydrogen operation in an engine. Why is dual fuelling practically preferred over 100 % hydrogen operation? Discuss any three challenges of pure hydrogen engines.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A four-stroke, single-cylinder hydrogen-diesel dual fuel engine has a displacement volume of 500 cc and operates at 2000 rpm. The indicated mean effective pressure (IMEP) is 7 bar. The mechanical efficiency is 80 %. The hydrogen energy fraction is 0.85. Calculate:
(i) The indicated power.
(ii) The brake power.
(iii) The total energy input rate, if the total fuel consumption (diesel + hydrogen equivalent) corresponds to a BSFC of 0.30 kg/kWh (in diesel equivalent).
(iv) The diesel mass flow rate, given $CV_{H_2}/CV_{diesel} = 2.82$.

**Model Solution Outline for Question B:**

**Part (a) — Comparison and Discussion:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Hydrogen Dual Fuel | Pure Hydrogen Engine |
|--------|-------------------|----------------------|
| Ignition | Pilot diesel provides ignition | External ignition (spark plug) required |
| Efficiency | Comparable to diesel (25–35 %) | Lower due to low flame speed at lean burn |
| NOₓ | High | Very high |
| Storage | Diesel + H₂ (hybrid) | Only H₂ (large tank) |
| Engine modification | Minimal (retrofit possible) | Major (spark ignition, materials) |
| Combustion stability | Excellent (diesel assists) | Poor at low loads, prone to backfire |
| CO₂ emissions | Reduced (proportional to HEF) | Zero (only H₂O) |

**Why dual fuelling is preferred:**
1. **No need for spark ignition system** — diesel's auto-ignition handles ignition, simplifying the engine.
2. **Wider operating range** — engine can run at low loads where pure H₂ would misfire.
3. **Existing infrastructure compatibility** — diesel mechanic workshops can service these engines.

**Challenges of pure hydrogen engines:**
1. **Pre-ignition and backfire** — hydrogen's wide flammability limit and low ignition energy cause uncontrolled combustion in the intake manifold.
2. **Abnormal combustion phenomena** — hydrogen's high flame speed and auto-ignition tendency lead to **knock** in SI engines.
3. **Material compatibility (hydrogen embrittlement)** — hydrogen molecules penetrate metal lattices, causing cracking in steel and aluminum components.
4. **Very low flame luminosity** — optical flame sensors fail; need special sensors.
5. **Lubricant degradation** — hydrogen reactions with oil additives cause increased wear.

**(Marks Distribution Guidance: 3 marks comparison, 2 marks preference reasoning, 2 marks challenges)**

---

**Part (b) — Numerical:**

**Given:**
- $V_s = 500$ cc $= 500 \times 10^{-6}$ m³ $= 5 \times 10^{-4}$ m³
- $N = 2000$ rpm (four-stroke → working strokes/min = 1000)
- $IMEP = 7$ bar $= 7 \times 10^5$ Pa
- $\eta_{mech} = 0.80$
- $HEF = 0.85$

**Step 1 (i): Indicated Power**

$$IP = \frac{IMEP \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n}{60}$$

For a single-cylinder, the swept volume $V_s = L \cdot A$, and $n$ is the number of power strokes per minute.

For a four-stroke engine at 2000 rpm:
$$n = \frac{2000}{2} = 1000 \text{ power strokes/min}$$

$$IP = \frac{IMEP \cdot V_s \cdot n}{60} = \frac{7 \times 10^5 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000}{60}$$

$$IP = \frac{3.5 \times 10^4 \times 1000}{60} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^7}{60}$$

$$IP = 5.833 \times 10^5 \text{ W} \times \frac{1}{1000} = 58.33 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer (i):** $IP = 58.33$ kW

**Step 2 (ii): Brake Power**

$$BP = \eta_{mech} \cdot IP = 0.80 \times 58.33 = 46.67 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer (ii):** $BP = 46.67$ kW

**Step 3 (iii): Total Energy Input Rate**

Given $BSFC_{eq} = 0.30$ kg/kWh (diesel equivalent mass per kWh).

Total equivalent mass flow rate:
$$\dot{m}_{eq} = BSFC \times BP = 0.30 \times 46.67 = 14.0 \text{ kg/h}$$

Convert to kg/s:
$$\dot{m}_{eq} = \frac{14.0}{3600} = 3.889 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s}$$

Energy input rate (using diesel equivalent in diesel CV):
$$\dot{Q}_{total} = \dot{m}_{eq} \cdot CV_{diesel} = 3.889 \times 10^{-3} \times 42{,}500$$

$$\dot{Q}_{total} = 165.28 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer (iii):** $\dot{Q}_{total} = 165.28$ kW (or 165.28 kJ/s)

**Step 4 (iv): Diesel Mass Flow Rate**

ESR:
$$ESR = 1 - HEF = 1 - 0.85 = 0.15$$

By definition of ESR:
$$ESR = \frac{\dot{m}_{diesel} \cdot CV_{diesel}}{\dot{Q}_{total}}$$

Solving for $\dot{m}_{diesel}$:
$$\dot{m}_{diesel} = \frac{ESR \cdot \dot{Q}_{total}}{CV_{diesel}} = \frac{0.15 \times 165.28}{42{,}500}$$

$$\dot{m}_{diesel} = 5.834 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

Convert to kg/h:
$$\dot{m}_{diesel} = 5.834 \times 10^{-4} \times 3600 = 2.10 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Answer (iv):** $\dot{m}_{diesel} = 2.10$ kg/h

**(Marks Distribution Guidance: 2 marks IP, 1.5 marks BP, 2 marks Q-total, 1.5 marks m-diesel)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion in BSEC vs. BSFC:** BSEC is energy-based (MJ/kWh), BSFC is mass-based (kg/kWh). Mixing them leads to zero credit.
> 2. **Forgetting four-stroke factor (n = N/2):** Many students use $n = N$ for both 2-stroke and 4-stroke. Examiner deducts 2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong IMEP unit conversion:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa. Using 7 Pa instead of $7 \times 10^5$ Pa zeroes the numerical answer.
> 4. **Pilot fuel energy numerator vs. denominator confusion in ESR:** Many students invert the formula. Memorize: **Pilot energy is on TOP** for ESR.
> 5. **Not converting kg/h to kg/s** in energy input calculations — common 1-mark deduction.
> 6. **Assuming 100 % combustion efficiency:** Real engines have incomplete combustion; energy balance assumes ideal unless stated.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **🚀 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Dual Fuelling**

**Core Concepts:**
- ☐ Dual fuelling = main gaseous fuel (H₂) + small liquid pilot fuel (diesel).
- ☐ The pilot fuel auto-ignites by compression; hydrogen's high auto-ignition temp (585 °C) prevents self-ignition.
- ☐ Three induction modes: **fumigation, port injection, direct injection (HCNG-DI)**.
- ☐ HEF = 0 corresponds to pure diesel; HEF = 1 corresponds to 100 % hydrogen (theoretical, impractical).

**Key Formulas to Memorize:**
- ☐ $ESR = \dfrac{m_d \cdot CV_d}{m_d \cdot CV_d + m_{H_2} \cdot CV_{H_2}}$
- ☐ $HEF = 1 - ESR$
- ☐ $\eta_{th,DF} = \dfrac{BP}{\dot{m}_d \cdot CV_d + \dot{m}_{H_2} \cdot CV_{H_2}}$
- ☐ $BSEC_{DF} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{total}}{BP}$ (in MJ/kWh)
- ☐ $IP = \dfrac{IMEP \cdot V_s \cdot n}{60}$ where $n = N/2$ for four-stroke.

**Performance Trends (remember the direction!):**
- ☐ **Smoke/PM:** Decrease sharply with H₂.
- ☐ **HC/CO:** Decrease with H₂.
- ☐ **NOₓ:** **INCREASE** with H₂ (thermal NOₓ from higher flame temperature).
- ☐ **BTE:** Slight increase at moderate HEF, drop at very high HEF.
- ☐ **Combustion stability:** Excellent at low HEF, marginal at high HEF.

**Constants to Memorize:**
- ☐ $CV_{H_2} = 120$ MJ/kg
- ☐ $CV_{diesel} \approx 42.5$ MJ/kg
- ☐ Hydrogen auto-ignition temp = 585 °C
- ☐ Hydrogen flammability range = 4 % to 75 % (by volume)
- ☐ Minimum ignition energy of H₂ = 0.02 mJ

**Common Confusions to Avoid:**
- ☐ ESR is pilot-fuel-energy / total-energy. **Numerator = pilot energy.**
- ☐ BSEC is NOT the same as BSFC; one is energy-based, the other mass-based.
- ☐ Four-stroke engines: number of power strokes = N/2.
- ☐ 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa (do not omit the $10^5$ factor in IMEP calculations).
- ☐ HEF = 1 − ESR, NOT ESR = 1 − HEF written wrongly; always remember the order.

**Real-World Applications:**
- ☐ Heavy-duty trucks (HPDI natural gas-diesel systems).
- ☐ Marine propulsion (LNG-diesel dual fuel).
- ☐ Stationary power generation.
- ☐ Bridge technology towards hydrogen-only engines.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Injection of Gaseous Fuel and Liquefied Hydrogen, Direct Injection of Liquid Fuel and Liquid Hydrogen, Hydrogen Enrichment Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT741 - HYDROGEN ENGINES AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Direct Injection of Gaseous Fuel and Liquefied Hydrogen, Direct Injection of Liquid Fuel and Liquid Hydrogen, Hydrogen Enrichment Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Direct Injection (DI) of Gaseous Fuel — Formal Definition

**Direct Injection (DI)** of gaseous fuel refers to a fuel delivery methodology in which a gaseous fuel — typically **Compressed Natural Gas (CNG)**, **Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG)**, or **gaseous Hydrogen (GH₂)** — is injected *directly* into the engine cylinder at high pressure, precisely timed with the engine's combustion cycle, bypassing the intake manifold. The fuel is metered via a dedicated high-pressure injector (solenoid or piezo-actuated) that sprays fuel either into the cylinder during the compression stroke (in a spark-ignition / dual-fuel context) or near top dead center (in HCCI / RCCI configurations).

In the specific KTU context, **direct injection of gaseous hydrogen (GH₂-DI)** is a critical technology because it overcomes the primary drawbacks of port fuel injection (PFI) of hydrogen — namely, the abnormal combustion phenomena such as **pre-ignition**, **backfire**, and **knock**, which arise because hydrogen's wide flammability limits (4–75% by volume in air) and very low minimum ignition energy (~0.02 mJ) make manifold mixing highly hazardous.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Direct injection is the *only* methodologically robust way to deliver hydrogen into a SI engine without inducing pre-ignition. This is because DI delays fuel introduction until the intake valve is closed, removing the residence time in the manifold where hot residual gases and surface ignition sources exist.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine you are filling a balloon (the engine cylinder) with two gases — air and hydrogen. If you mix them in a long tube *before* the balloon (this is **port fuel injection**), the mixture sits in the hot tube for a long time and may spontaneously combust. However, if you poke a tiny hole in the balloon and squirt the hydrogen directly inside just before the balloon reaches its maximum squeeze (this is **direct injection**), the gases only mix at the last possible moment — preventing any rogue ignition. This is the essence of DI for hydrogen.

### 1.2 Direct Injection of Liquefied Hydrogen (LH₂-DI) — Formal Definition

**Liquefied Hydrogen Direct Injection (LH₂-DI)** involves storing hydrogen in cryogenic liquid form at approximately **20.27 K (–252.88 °C)** and pressures of 1–10 bar, then injecting it as a cryogenic liquid directly into the combustion chamber. Upon injection, the liquid hydrogen undergoes rapid vaporization, absorbing significant **latent heat of vaporization (≈ 446 kJ/kg)**, which provides substantial internal cooling of the cylinder charge. This is one of the most thermodynamically elegant strategies for high-efficiency hydrogen IC engines.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants — LH₂:**
> - Boiling point: **20.27 K**
> - Density of liquid: **70.8 kg/m³**
> - Density of gas at NTP: **0.0838 kg/m³** (i.e., liquid is ~845× denser than gaseous)
> - Lower Heating Value (LHV): **119.96 MJ/kg**
> - Latent heat of vaporization: **445.6 kJ/kg**

### 1.3 Direct Injection of Liquid Fuel (Gasoline/Diesel) — Formal Definition

**Direct Injection of Liquid Fuel** is a well-established technology in which a liquid hydrocarbon fuel (gasoline — GDI, or diesel — DI-Diesel) is pressurized (typically **100–350 bar** for GDI, **1000–2000 bar** for common-rail diesel) and injected through a high-pressure injector directly into the combustion chamber. In the KTU Module 1 context, this forms the **comparative baseline** against which hydrogen DI systems are evaluated.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of direct injection of liquid fuel like a high-pressure water gun shooting a fine mist directly into a sealed container. The mist forms an evaporating spray plume that mixes with hot air — this is essentially what happens in a GDI engine. For hydrogen, the same concept applies, but the fluid is cryogenic and far more energetic.

### 1.4 Direct Injection of Liquid Hydrogen (Cryogenic DI)

This is functionally similar to LH₂-DI, but the term is sometimes used to distinguish it from systems where hydrogen is first gasified in a heat exchanger before injection. In **true liquid hydrogen DI**, the cryogenic liquid passes through an injector and enters the chamber as a *two-phase flashing spray* — a mixture of liquid droplets surrounded by a self-generated vapor cloud.

### 1.5 Hydrogen Enrichment Techniques — Formal Definition

**Hydrogen Enrichment** refers to a family of strategies in which hydrogen is used as a *supplement* to a primary hydrocarbon fuel (typically diesel, gasoline, or natural gas) to improve combustion characteristics. The hydrogen is added in proportions typically between **5–30% by energy**, depending on the technique.

The major hydrogen enrichment techniques studied in Module 1 are:

1. **Port Induction (Fumigation):** Hydrogen is introduced into the intake air stream, upstream of the intake valve.
2. **Manifold Injection (Premixed Dual-Fuel):** Hydrogen is injected into the intake manifold, mixing with air before entering the cylinder.
3. **Direct Injection Enrichment:** Hydrogen is injected directly into the cylinder alongside the primary fuel.
4. **Plasma-Assisted Reforming:** A separate reformer generates hydrogen-rich syngas from the base fuel.
5. **Exhaust Gas Recirculation (EGR) with Hydrogen:** Hydrogen blended with EGR for emissions control.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydrogen Enrichment vs. Pure Hydrogen DI:** The former uses hydrogen as a *supplementary fuel* (typically 5–30% energy share) alongside a hydrocarbon; the latter uses hydrogen as the *sole fuel*. Both fall within Module 1 scope, but enrichment techniques are particularly important for retrofitting existing diesel engines — a common industrial application.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Direct Injection (Stepwise)

The DI process, whether for gaseous or liquid fuel, follows a well-defined operational sequence:

- **Step 1 — Fuel Pressurization:** The fuel is pressurized to the required rail pressure. For GDI gasoline systems, this is typically **100–350 bar**; for hydrogen DI, it is **200–450 bar** for gaseous H₂ and **1–10 bar** for cryogenic LH₂.
- **Step 2 — Injection Timing Determination:** The ECU calculates crank-angle-resolved injection timing. For hydrogen DI, injection is typically initiated *after* intake valve closure (IVC) to prevent backfire, usually between **80°–20° BTDC** depending on load.
- **Step 3 — Spray Formation & Atomization:** The fuel passes through multi-hole injectors, forming a high-momentum spray. For LH₂, the spray is a flashing jet, while for gaseous H₂, it is a supersonic or subsonic gas jet.
- **Step 4 — Mixing with Air:** Because the injection event is brief, mixing is dominated by in-cylinder turbulence (squish, swirl, tumble) rather than time-based diffusion as in PFI.
- **Step 5 — Combustion:** Spark ignition (SI) or compression ignition (CI for dual-fuel mode) initiates combustion.
- **Step 6 — Power Generation & Exhaust:** Combustion gases expand, perform work, and are exhausted.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examination Focus:** The *timing* of injection relative to **Intake Valve Closure (IVC)** is one of the most frequently tested concepts. Premature injection (before IVC) defeats the purpose of DI for hydrogen.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Direct Injection for Hydrogen

| Phenomenon | PFI Hydrogen | DI Hydrogen |
|------------|--------------|-------------|
| Backfire risk | Very High (H₂ in manifold) | Negligible (no H₂ in manifold) |
| Pre-ignition risk | High (residence time) | Low (short residence) |
| Power density | Reduced (H₂ displaces air) | Higher (charge cooling) |
| Volumetric efficiency | Reduced ~10–15% | Maintained or improved |
| NOx emissions | Higher (premixed hot mix) | Lower (stratified charge possible) |

**Why these differences exist (the "How"):**

- **Charge Cooling Effect:** Hydrogen has a very high specific heat capacity ratio, and the injection event itself absorbs energy. This effectively lowers the effective end-of-compression temperature, reducing NOₓ formation.
- **Stratified Charge Formation:** DI enables locally fuel-rich and fuel-lean zones, allowing combustion at overall lean conditions while maintaining a combustible mixture near the spark plug.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind Direct Injection of Liquid Hydrogen (Cryogenic DI)

The cryogenic injection of LH₂ offers three thermodynamic advantages that gaseous H₂-DI cannot match:

1. **Charge Cooling from Latent Heat:** The vaporization of LH₂ absorbs **445.6 kJ/kg**, dramatically reducing cylinder temperatures.
2. **Higher Energy Density:** Liquid H₂ at 70.8 kg/m³ allows a much smaller storage volume compared to gaseous H₂ (0.0838 kg/m³ at NTP) — approximately an **845-fold volumetric advantage**.
3. **Suppression of Abnormal Combustion:** Because LH₂ is so cold, the local temperature after injection is far below the auto-ignition temperature of hydrogen (≈ **858 K**), eliminating pre-ignition risk during the injection event itself.

### 2.4 Hydrogen Enrichment Techniques — Deep Dive

#### 2.4.1 Port Induction / Fumigation

In fumigation, hydrogen is introduced into the intake port through a low-pressure injector. The hydrogen mixes with incoming air before the intake valve. This is the **simplest** enrichment method but suffers from backfire risk at higher hydrogen substitution rates.

**Operational Logic:**

- **Step 1:** Air enters the intake manifold.
- **Step 2:** Hydrogen is metered and injected at low pressure (1.5–3 bar) into the manifold.
- **Step 3:** The air–H₂ mixture enters the cylinder.
- **Step 4:** The primary fuel (e.g., diesel pilot) initiates compression ignition.

**Limitation:** Hydrogen substitution beyond ~30% by energy causes backfire and pre-ignition.

#### 2.4.2 Manifold Injection (Premixed Dual-Fuel)

Similar to fumigation but with higher injection pressure (3–8 bar) and a dedicated injector closer to the intake port. This provides finer atomization and better mixing, allowing higher hydrogen substitution ratios (up to ~50%).

#### 2.4.3 Direct Injection Enrichment

Hydrogen is injected directly into the cylinder alongside the primary fuel. This eliminates backfire risk and allows up to **80–90%** hydrogen substitution by energy — the highest among enrichment methods.

#### 2.4.4 Plasma-Assisted Reforming

A non-conventional technique where a **non-thermal plasma reactor** partially reforms the base hydrocarbon fuel into a hydrogen-rich syngas, which is then fed into the engine. This is energy-intensive but provides on-demand hydrogen generation.

#### 2.4.5 Hydrogen-Enriched EGR

A portion of exhaust gas is recirculated and mixed with a small hydrogen charge. The hydrogen acts as a combustion enhancer, allowing the EGR to be increased without sacrificing combustion stability — reducing NOₓ by up to 50%.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates the key formulas for Module 1 — Direct Injection and Hydrogen Enrichment. Pay special attention to the stoichiometric air–fuel ratios and energy substitution formulas, as these are recurring KTU exam questions.

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $AFR_{stoich,H_2} = 34.2$ | Stoichiometric air–fuel ratio for H₂ (mass basis) | kg air / kg H₂ |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{AFR_{actual}}{AFR_{stoich}}$ | Relative air–fuel ratio (equivalence ratio basis) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\phi = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Equivalence ratio | dimensionless |
| 4 | $E_{H_2} = m_{H_2} \times LHV_{H_2}$ | Energy content of H₂ mass (LHV = 119.96 MJ/kg) | MJ |
| 5 | $H_{sub} = \dfrac{E_{H_2}}{E_{H_2} + E_{HC}} \times 100\%$ | Hydrogen energy substitution percentage | % |
| 6 | $\rho_{LH_2} = 70.8$ | Density of liquid hydrogen at boiling point | kg/m³ |
| 7 | $\rho_{GH_2,NTP} = 0.0838$ | Density of gaseous hydrogen at NTP | kg/m³ |
| 8 | $V_{ratio} = \dfrac{\rho_{LH_2}}{\rho_{GH_2}} \approx 845$ | Volumetric advantage of LH₂ over GH₂ | dimensionless |
| 9 | $L_{vap,H_2} = 445.6$ | Latent heat of vaporization of H₂ | kJ/kg |
| 10 | $\Delta T_{charge} = \dfrac{m_{fuel} \times L_{vap,H_2}}{m_{air} \times c_{p,air}}$ | Charge cooling temperature drop | K |
| 11 | $P_{inj} = \dfrac{\Delta P \times C_d \times A_{nozzle}}{\sqrt{R \cdot T_{fuel}}}$ | Theoretical injector mass flow (choked flow) | kg/s |
| 12 | $\eta_{vol,DI} = \dfrac{m_{air,actual}}{m_{air,ideal}}$ | Volumetric efficiency of DI engine | dimensionless |
| 13 | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{P_{rail} \times C_d \times A_{n}}{\sqrt{R_{H_2} \cdot T_{rail}}}$ | H₂ mass flow rate from injector (real gas, simplified) | kg/s |
| 14 | $E_{den,LH_2} = LHV_{H_2} \times \rho_{LH_2} = 8.49$ | Energy density of LH₂ by volume | GJ/m³ |
| 15 | $E_{den,GH_2,200bar} = LHV_{H_2} \times \rho_{GH_2,200bar} = 1.93$ | Energy density of GH₂ at 200 bar | GJ/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where to use these:** The energy substitution formula (Formula 5) is *the* KTU-favorite for dual-fuel numericals. For example: "If 0.5 kg of H₂ and 3.0 kg of diesel are burned, what is the hydrogen energy substitution percentage?" → Use Formula 5 directly.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive Industry:** GDI (Gasoline Direct Injection) is now standard in modern cars (e.g., Toyota Dynamic Force Engine). Hydrogen DI is used in prototype vehicles (e.g., BMW Hydrogen 7, Toyota Mirai, though the latter uses fuel cells).
- **Heavy-Duty Trucks:** Dual-fuel hydrogen-diesel systems with hydrogen enrichment up to 30% have been tested in mining trucks and long-haul trucks for emission reduction.
- **Marine Engines:** LH₂-DI is being explored for large marine engines where cryogenic storage is feasible.
- **Aerospace:** Cryogenic hydrogen fuel (LH₂) is the standard fuel for upper-stage rocket engines (e.g., Space Shuttle Main Engine, Ariane Vulcain), where direct cryogenic injection is the only feasible method.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hydrogen Energy Substitution Percentage (HESP)

**Problem Statement:** A dual-fuel engine burns **0.6 kg of hydrogen** and **2.4 kg of diesel** per hour. Calculate the hydrogen energy substitution percentage.

**Given:**
- Mass of hydrogen, $m_{H_2} = 0.6$ kg
- Mass of diesel, $m_{diesel} = 2.4$ kg
- $LHV_{H_2} = 119.96$ MJ/kg
- $LHV_{diesel} = 42.5$ MJ/kg

**Solution:**

The hydrogen energy substitution percentage is defined as the ratio of energy contributed by hydrogen to the total energy released by both fuels.

$$
H_{sub} = \frac{E_{H_2}}{E_{H_2} + E_{HC}} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 1 — Energy from hydrogen:**

$$
E_{H_2} = m_{H_2} \times LHV_{H_2}
$$

$$
E_{H_2} = 0.6 \text{ kg} \times 119.96 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

$$
E_{H_2} = 71.976 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Energy from diesel:**

$$
E_{diesel} = m_{diesel} \times LHV_{diesel}
$$

$$
E_{diesel} = 2.4 \text{ kg} \times 42.5 \text{ MJ/kg}
$$

$$
E_{diesel} = 102.0 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Total energy:**

$$
E_{total} = E_{H_2} + E_{diesel} = 71.976 + 102.0 = 173.976 \text{ MJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting into the HESP formula:**

$$
H_{sub} = \frac{71.976}{173.976} \times 100\%
$$

$$
H_{sub} = 0.4137 \times 100\% = 41.37\%
$$

**Result:** Hydrogen contributes approximately **41.37%** of the total energy. This is a high-substitution dual-fuel mode, and would typically require direct injection of hydrogen to avoid backfire.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Charge Cooling Effect of LH₂ Injection

**Problem Statement:** An engine cylinder at **TDC after compression** contains **0.5 kg of air** at a temperature of **800 K** and pressure of **40 bar**. A direct injection of **0.02 kg of liquid hydrogen** is performed. Estimate the temperature drop due to vaporization of LH₂. Assume constant-pressure vaporization and that all vaporization heat is drawn from the air charge. Use $c_{p,air} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K).

**Given:**
- $m_{air} = 0.5$ kg
- $T_{air,initial} = 800$ K
- $m_{LH_2} = 0.02$ kg
- $L_{vap,H_2} = 445.6$ kJ/kg
- $c_{p,air} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Solution:**

The energy balance for charge cooling is:

$$
\Delta T_{charge} = \frac{m_{LH_2} \times L_{vap,H_2}}{m_{air} \times c_{p,air}}
$$

**Step 1 — Energy absorbed by LH₂ vaporization:**

$$
Q_{abs} = m_{LH_2} \times L_{vap,H_2} = 0.02 \times 445.6 = 8.912 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Heat capacity of the air charge:**

$$
C_{air} = m_{air} \times c_{p,air} = 0.5 \times 1.005 = 0.5025 \text{ kJ/K}
$$

**Step 3 — Temperature drop:**

$$
\Delta T_{charge} = \frac{8.912}{0.5025}
$$

$$
\Delta T_{charge} = 17.73 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 4 — Final charge temperature:**

$$
T_{final} = T_{initial} - \Delta T_{charge} = 800 - 17.73 = 782.27 \text{ K}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A ~18 K temperature drop is *significant* for knock resistance and NOₓ reduction. This is why LH₂-DI engines can operate at higher compression ratios without knock — the cryogenic fuel acts as an in-situ charge coolant. (Note: a more rigorous analysis would also account for the heat capacity of the resulting H₂ vapor, but the simplified model above is the standard textbook approach for KTU-level problems.)

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Volumetric Energy Density Comparison (LH₂ vs. GH₂)

**Problem Statement:** Compare the volumetric energy density of liquid hydrogen and gaseous hydrogen compressed to **350 bar** (the standard for Type III hydrogen tanks in fuel cell vehicles).

**Given:**
- $\rho_{LH_2} = 70.8$ kg/m³
- $\rho_{GH_2,350bar} \approx 24.0$ kg/m³ (at 288 K, real gas value)
- $LHV_{H_2} = 119.96$ MJ/kg

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Volumetric energy density of LH₂:**

$$
E_{den,LH_2} = LHV_{H_2} \times \rho_{LH_2} = 119.96 \times 70.8 = 8493.2 \text{ MJ/m}^3
$$

Converting to GJ/m³:

$$
E_{den,LH_2} = 8.49 \text{ GJ/m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric energy density of GH₂ at 350 bar:**

$$
E_{den,GH_2} = LHV_{H_2} \times \rho_{GH_2,350bar} = 119.96 \times 24.0 = 2879.0 \text{ MJ/m}^3
$$

Converting to GJ/m³:

$$
E_{den,GH_2} = 2.88 \text{ GJ/m}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Ratio of LH₂ to GH₂ volumetric energy density:**

$$
\text{Ratio} = \frac{E_{den,LH_2}}{E_{den,GH_2}} = \frac{8.49}{2.88} = 2.95
$$

**Step 4 — Comparison with gasoline for context:**

Gasoline volumetric energy density: $\approx 32$ MJ/L $= 32$ GJ/m³. So even LH₂ has only ~26% of gasoline's volumetric energy density — a key challenge for hydrogen as an IC engine fuel.

**Result:** LH₂ stores approximately **2.95× more energy per unit volume** than GH₂ at 350 bar. This is the *primary engineering motivation* for LH₂-DI in mobile applications where packaging volume is constrained.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Required Hydrogen Mass Flow for a Given Engine Power

**Problem Statement:** A hydrogen-DI engine must deliver **50 kW** of indicated power at an engine speed of **3000 rpm** (4-stroke, single cylinder). Calculate the required mass flow rate of hydrogen, assuming a thermal efficiency of **38%**.

**Given:**
- $P_i = 50$ kW
- $N = 3000$ rpm (4-stroke, so power strokes per second = $N/2/60 = 25$ Hz)
- $\eta_{th} = 0.38$
- $LHV_{H_2} = 119.96$ MJ/kg = 119,960 kJ/kg

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Required fuel energy rate:**

$$
\dot{E}_{fuel} = \frac{P_i}{\eta_{th}} = \frac{50}{0.38} = 131.58 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 2 — Required hydrogen mass flow rate:**

$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \frac{\dot{E}_{fuel}}{LHV_{H_2}} = \frac{131.58}{119960} \text{ kg/s}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = 1.097 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/s} = 3.95 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Per-cycle hydrogen mass (4-stroke, 25 power strokes/sec):**

$$
m_{H_2,cycle} = \frac{\dot{m}_{H_2}}{n_{cycles/s}} = \frac{1.097 \times 10^{-3}}{25}
$$

$$
m_{H_2,cycle} = 4.388 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg/cycle} = 43.88 \text{ mg/cycle}
$$

**Result:** The engine requires approximately **43.88 mg of H₂ per cycle**. This small quantity must be precisely metered by the high-pressure DI injector within a very short crank-angle window (typically 200–400 μs per injection event).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of Injection Methods

| Parameter | PFI Gaseous H₂ | DI Gaseous H₂ | DI Liquid H₂ | GDI Gasoline | DI Diesel |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|----------------|---------------|-----------|
| Injection pressure (bar) | 3–8 | 200–450 | 1–10 (cryo) | 100–350 | 1000–2000 |
| Backfire risk | Very High | Low | Negligible | None | None |
| Volumetric efficiency | Reduced 10–15% | Maintained | Improved | Maintained | Maintained |
| Charge cooling | Minor | Moderate | **Very high** | Minor (latent heat) | None |
| Max H₂ substitution (dual-fuel) | 30% | 80–90% | 80–90% | N/A | N/A |
| Storage volume needed | Large | Large | **~3× smaller** | N/A | N/A |
| System complexity | Low | High | **Very high** | High | High |
| Typical application | Retrofits | Prototype cars | Marine/aerospace | Modern SI cars | CI engines |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hydrogen Direct Injection System Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H2Tank[H2 Storage Tank High Pressure 350 to 700 bar]
    Shutoff[Shut Off Valve]
    Reg[Pressure Regulator Down to 200 to 450 bar]
    Filter[H2 Filter]
    HPRail[High Pressure Accumulator Rail]
    InjCtrl[ECU Injection Control Unit]
    Inj[H2 Direct Injector Piezo or Solenoid]
    Cyl[Engine Cylinder Combustion Chamber]
    Spark[Spark Plug]
    Sensor[Lambda Sensor O2 Feedback]
    EGR[EGR Valve Optional]

    H2Tank --> Shutoff
    Shutoff --> Reg
    Reg --> Filter
    Filter --> HPRail
    HPRail --> Inj
    InjCtrl --> Inj
    Inj --> Cyl
    Cyl --> Spark
    Cyl --> Sensor
    Sensor --> InjCtrl
    EGR --> Cyl
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This block diagram maps the functional architecture of a typical gaseous H₂-DI system. The hydrogen flows from the high-pressure storage tank through a shut-off valve, gets regulated to injection rail pressure, passes through a filter to remove particulates, accumulates in a high-pressure rail, and is finally metered by the DI injector into the cylinder. The ECU controls injection timing and duration using feedback from the lambda sensor.

### 4.2 LH₂ Cryogenic Direct Injection System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LH2Tank[LH2 Cryogenic Tank 20.27 K Storage]
    Vent[Vent and Pressure Buildup Coil]
    CryoPump[Cryogenic Pump or Pressurization System]
    VacJack[Vacuum Jacketed Transfer Line]
    CryoInj[Cryogenic Injector Two Phase Flashing]
    CylLH2[Engine Cylinder With Charge Cooling]
    VapCloud[Self Generated H2 Vapor Cloud]
    LiqDrop[LH2 Droplets in Spray]
    ECU2[Cryo ECU With Temperature Compensation]

    LH2Tank --> Vent
    LH2Tank --> CryoPump
    CryoPump --> VacJack
    VacJack --> CryoInj
    CryoInj --> CylLH2
    CryoInj --> VapCloud
    CryoInj --> LiqDrop
    ECU2 --> CryoInj
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This schematic shows the unique aspects of LH₂-DI. The vacuum-jacketed transfer line is essential to minimize heat ingress and prevent premature boil-off. The cryogenic injector produces a two-phase flashing spray (liquid droplets + vapor), which provides exceptional charge cooling inside the cylinder.

### 4.3 Hydrogen Enrichment Techniques — Comparative Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Need to Reduce Emissions of Existing Diesel Engine]
    Q1{Existing Engine Type?}
    Start --> Q1
    Q1 -->|Small Automotive| P1[Fumigation Port Induction Max 20 percent H2]
    Q1 -->|Medium Duty Truck| P2[Manifold Injection Premixed Max 40 percent H2]
    Q1 -->|Heavy Duty Industrial| P3[Direct Injection Enrichment Max 80 percent H2]
    Q1 -->|Stationary Generator| P4[Plasma Reformed Syngas]

    P1 --> Lim1[Limitation Backfire Risk at High Load]
    P2 --> Lim2[Limitation Pre Ignition at Low Load]
    P3 --> Lim3[Limitation Injector Cost and Cryogenic Option]
    P4 --> Lim4[Limitation Plasma Reactor Energy Cost]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This decision tree guides the selection of an appropriate hydrogen enrichment strategy based on the host engine's size and duty cycle. Smaller engines favor simpler low-pressure fumigation, while larger engines justify the complexity of direct injection enrichment.

### 4.4 Injection Event Timing Diagram (Crank-Angle Domain)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[IVC Intake Valve Closes 180 BTDC] --> B[Early DI Injection 100 to 60 BTDC Homogeneous Charge]
    B --> C[Spark Ignition 20 to 10 BTDC]
    C --> D[Combustion 10 BTDC to 30 ATDC]
    D --> E[Power Stroke 30 to 180 ATDC]

    F[Latest DI Injection 30 to 5 BTDC Stratified Charge] --> G[Spark 0 BTDC]
    G --> H[Late Combustion with Stratification]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This diagram shows two DI strategies in the crank-angle domain. **Early DI** (100–60° BTDC) allows sufficient time for mixing, producing a homogeneous charge similar to PFI but without the backfire risk. **Late DI** (30–5° BTDC) creates a stratified charge, which is advantageous for lean-burn operation and NOₓ reduction.

### 4.5 Dual-Fuel Hydrogen Enrichment System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DieselTank[Diesel Tank]
    DieselPump[High Pressure Diesel Pump 1000 bar]
    DieselInj[Diesel Injector Pilot Injection]
    H2Supply[Hydrogen Source Cylinder or Reformer]
    H2Meter[Hydrogen Flow Meter]
    H2InjDual[Hydrogen Injector Port or Direct]

    DieselTank --> DieselPump
    DieselPump --> DieselInj
    H2Supply --> H2Meter
    H2Meter --> H2InjDual
    DieselInj --> CylDual[Engine Cylinder]
    H2InjDual --> CylDual
    CylDual --> Combustion[Dual Fuel Combustion]
    Combustion --> Output[Power and Reduced Emissions]
```

**Diagram Interpretation:** This is the canonical architecture of a hydrogen-enriched dual-fuel engine. The diesel pilot provides compression ignition, while hydrogen (added via either port or direct injection) accelerates flame propagation and reduces soot formation.
