## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**"Define direct injection of gaseous hydrogen and explain how it overcomes the backfire problem associated with port fuel injection."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Direct injection of gaseous hydrogen refers to the delivery of pressurized gaseous hydrogen (200–450 bar) directly into the engine cylinder through a high-pressure injector, *after* the intake valve has closed. This is in contrast to port fuel injection, where hydrogen is introduced into the intake manifold upstream of the intake valve.

The backfire problem in PFI arises because hydrogen, with its wide flammability limits (4–75% in air) and very low minimum ignition energy (~0.02 mJ), can ignite in the hot intake manifold due to contact with hot residual gases or backflow from the cylinder. Because DI introduces hydrogen only *after* intake valve closure, the residence time in the manifold is eliminated, and there is no hydrogen-air mixture present where backfire can occur. This makes DI the only robust method for delivering hydrogen to a spark-ignition engine without abnormal combustion. **(3 marks)**

---

**Question 2: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**"List any three hydrogen enrichment techniques and state the maximum hydrogen substitution percentage typically achievable with each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Port induction (fumigation):** Up to **~20–30%** hydrogen by energy. (1 mark)
2. **Manifold injection (premixed dual-fuel):** Up to **~40–50%** hydrogen by energy. (1 mark)
3. **Direct injection enrichment:** Up to **~80–90%** hydrogen by energy. (1 mark)

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

**Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Expected 14-Mark Module 1 Question]**

**(a) Explain in detail the direct injection of gaseous hydrogen in SI engines, with a neat block diagram of the fuel system. Discuss the advantages and limitations of the system. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition and Concept (2 marks):**
Direct injection of gaseous hydrogen (GH₂-DI) is a fuel delivery method in which high-pressure gaseous hydrogen (typically 200–450 bar rail pressure) is injected *directly* into the engine cylinder via a dedicated high-pressure injector, timed to occur *after* the intake valve has closed. The injector is controlled by the ECU, which calculates the start of injection (SOI) and injection duration based on load, speed, and feedback from the lambda sensor.

**Block Diagram Description (2 marks):**
The fuel system consists of: (i) high-pressure hydrogen storage tank (350–700 bar, Type III or Type IV composite cylinder), (ii) shut-off solenoid valve, (iii) pressure regulator to step down the pressure to rail pressure, (iv) high-pressure hydrogen filter to remove particulates, (v) high-pressure accumulator rail, (vi) piezo- or solenoid-actuated direct injector, and (vii) ECU with crank-angle-resolved injection control. The lambda sensor provides closed-loop feedback.

**Advantages (2 marks):**
1. **Elimination of backfire and pre-ignition** — hydrogen is not present in the intake manifold.
2. **Improved volumetric efficiency** — air is inducted without fuel displacement.
3. **Charge cooling effect** — hydrogen injection absorbs heat, reducing knock and NOₓ.
4. **Stratified charge capability** — late DI enables lean-burn stratified operation.
5. **High hydrogen substitution ratio** — up to 80–90% in dual-fuel applications.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. **High system cost** — high-pressure components are expensive.
2. **Injector durability** — hydrogen embrittlement affects injector life.
3. **Storage pressure requirements** — vehicle tank must be 350–700 bar.
4. **Complexity of cryogenic option** — for LH₂-DI, additional cryogenic handling is required.

**(b) A hydrogen-diesel dual-fuel engine operates on 0.8 kg/hr of hydrogen and 3.2 kg/hr of diesel. Compute the hydrogen energy substitution percentage. Given: LHV of H₂ = 119.96 MJ/kg, LHV of diesel = 42.5 MJ/kg. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Compute energy from hydrogen.

$$
E_{H_2} = m_{H_2} \times LHV_{H_2} = 0.8 \times 119.96 = 95.968 \text{ MJ/hr}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute energy from diesel.

$$
E_{diesel} = m_{diesel} \times LHV_{diesel} = 3.2 \times 42.5 = 136.0 \text{ MJ/hr}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Total energy.

$$
E_{total} = 95.968 + 136.0 = 231.968 \text{ MJ/hr}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply HESP formula.

$$
H_{sub} = \frac{E_{H_2}}{E_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{95.968}{231.968} \times 100\%
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):**

$$
H_{sub} = 0.4137 \times 100\% = 41.37\%
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Conclusion. The hydrogen energy substitution percentage is **41.37%**. This is a moderate-substitution dual-fuel mode, achievable with manifold or low-pressure direct injection. **(1 mark for conclusion)**

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Engineering interpretation. A 41% substitution level typically requires either manifold injection or low-pressure direct injection of hydrogen; it cannot be achieved with simple port fumigation (limited to ~20–30%).

---

**Question B: [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected 14-Mark Module 1 Question, Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Explain the direct injection of liquid hydrogen in IC engines. Discuss its thermodynamic advantages over gaseous hydrogen DI. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (2 marks):**
Direct injection of liquid hydrogen (LH₂-DI) involves storing hydrogen in cryogenic liquid form at approximately 20.27 K (–252.88 °C) and injecting it as a cryogenic liquid directly into the combustion chamber. The injection event is characterized by a two-phase flashing spray — a mixture of liquid droplets surrounded by self-generated vapor. The injector is typically a cryogenically-rated solenoid or a special cryo-piezo injector with thermal isolation.

**Thermodynamic Advantages (4 marks):**

1. **Charge cooling from latent heat (1 mark):** The vaporization of LH₂ absorbs 445.6 kJ/kg, providing substantial cooling of the in-cylinder charge. This reduces the peak combustion temperature by 15–25 K, decreasing NOₓ emissions and suppressing knock.

2. **Higher volumetric energy density (1 mark):** LH₂ at 70.8 kg/m³ stores approximately 8.49 GJ/m³ of energy, which is 2.95× more than GH₂ at 350 bar (2.88 GJ/m³). This reduces storage volume by a factor of ~3 compared to 350 bar gaseous storage.

3. **Suppression of abnormal combustion (1 mark):** Because LH₂ is so cold (20.27 K), the local temperature at the injection point is far below the auto-ignition temperature of hydrogen (~858 K), making pre-ignition virtually impossible during the injection event.

4. **Higher achievable compression ratio (1 mark):** Due to the extreme charge cooling, LH₂-DI engines can operate at compression ratios of 15:1 to 18:1 without knock, approaching the thermodynamic optimum of the Otto cycle.

**Limitations (1 mark):**
1. **Cryogenic storage complexity** — boil-off losses of 0.1–0.3% per day.
2. **Vacuum insulation required** — adds cost and weight.
3. **Materials challenges** — embrittlement and thermal cycling fatigue.
4. **Refueling infrastructure** — cryogenic hydrogen stations are rare.

**(b) An LH₂-DI engine receives 0.05 kg of liquid hydrogen per cycle in a cylinder containing 0.4 kg of air at 750 K. Estimate the charge temperature drop due to vaporization. Use $L_{vap} = 445.6$ kJ/kg and $c_{p,air} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K). (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Identify the energy balance equation.

$$
\Delta T = \frac{m_{LH_2} \times L_{vap}}{m_{air} \times c_{p,air}}
$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Compute energy absorbed.

$$
Q = 0.05 \times 445.6 = 22.28 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 3 (1 mark):** Compute heat capacity of air.

$$
C_{air} = 0.4 \times 1.005 = 0.402 \text{ kJ/K}
$$

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Compute temperature drop.

$$
\Delta T = \frac{22.28}{0.402} = 55.42 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Compute final temperature.

$$
T_{final} = 750 - 55.42 = 694.58 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 6 (1 mark):** Engineering interpretation. A 55 K drop is *very significant* — it allows the engine to operate at a higher compression ratio (e.g., 16:1) without knock, and reduces NOₓ formation by approximately 30–40% compared to a non-cooled baseline.

**Step 7 (1 mark):** Conclusion. The cryogenic LH₂ injection provides a substantial **55.42 K** temperature drop, demonstrating the unique thermodynamic advantage of LH₂-DI over GH₂-DI for high-efficiency, low-emission hydrogen IC engines.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit consistency:** Always convert LHV to consistent units (kJ/kg or MJ/kg) before calculation. Mixing MJ and kJ is the most common mistake, costing 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Mass vs. Volumetric:** When asked about "energy density," specify whether you mean mass-basis (MJ/kg) or volumetric (MJ/m³). Examiners deduct 1 mark for ambiguity.
> 3. **Stoichiometric AFR confusion:** Hydrogen has a stoichiometric AFR of **34.2 (mass basis)**, NOT 14.7 (which is gasoline's). This is a classic trap.
> 4. **IVC vs. SOI:** Students often confuse *intake valve closure* (IVC) with *start of injection* (SOI). DI requires SOI to be **after** IVC; stating the reverse will cost 1 mark.
> 5. **Backfire causes:** Examiners expect a *cause-effect* explanation (e.g., "low ignition energy → manifold ignition → backfire"), not a one-line answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Direct Injection (DI)** of hydrogen means injecting hydrogen *directly* into the cylinder *after* IVC, not into the intake manifold.
- **DI solves three problems of PFI hydrogen:** backfire, pre-ignition, and reduced volumetric efficiency.
- **GH₂-DI rail pressure** is typically **200–450 bar**; **LH₂-DI** is at **1–10 bar** (cryogenic).
- **Stoichiometric AFR for hydrogen** = **34.2 (mass basis)**, much higher than gasoline's 14.7, meaning hydrogen engines need to move more air mass.
- **LH₂ has ~845× volumetric advantage** over GH₂ at NTP, and **~3× advantage** even over GH₂ at 350 bar.
- **Latent heat of vaporization of H₂** = **445.6 kJ/kg** — this is the source of the charge cooling effect.
- **Hydrogen energy substitution percentage (HESP)** formula: $H_{sub} = \dfrac{E_{H_2}}{E_{H_2} + E_{HC}} \times 100\%$.
- **Hydrogen enrichment techniques** in order of increasing substitution: Fumigation (~20–30%) < Manifold (~40–50%) < Direct Injection (~80–90%).
- **Hydrogen DI injector timing** is critical: too early → homogeneous charge, too late → wall wetting and unburned H₂.
- **LH₂-DI** allows compression ratios up to **15–18:1** without knock due to extreme charge cooling.
- **Hydrogen's flammability limits in air:** 4% to 75% by volume — much wider than gasoline (1–7.6%).
- **Hydrogen's minimum ignition energy:** ~0.02 mJ — about 10× lower than gasoline, making it highly susceptible to backfire in PFI.
- **Cryogenic storage** of LH₂ requires **vacuum-jacketed** insulated tanks to minimize boil-off.
- **Hydrogen enrichment of diesel** reduces soot by up to 50% but can increase NOₓ unless EGR is also used.
- **Real-world DI hydrogen vehicles:** BMW Hydrogen 7 (2006) used GH₂-DI; Toyota Mirai uses fuel cells (not DI).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Fuel Cells: Historical Development of Hydrogen Fuel Cell Technology, Comparison Between Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicles and Conventional Internal Combustion Engine Vehicles, Overview of Global Initiatives and Policies Promoting Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT741 - HYDROGEN ENGINES AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Fuel Cells: Historical Development of Hydrogen Fuel Cell Technology, Comparison Between Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicles and Conventional Internal Combustion Engine Vehicles, Overview of Global Initiatives and Policies Promoting Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Fuel Cell?

A **fuel cell** is an electrochemical energy conversion device that directly converts the chemical energy of a fuel (typically **hydrogen**) and an oxidant (typically **oxygen from air**) into **electrical energy**, with **water and heat** as the primary by-products. Unlike a conventional battery, a fuel cell does not run down or require recharging — it produces electricity as long as fuel and oxidant are supplied.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A fuel cell is a static energy conversion device that generates electricity through an electrochemical reaction between a fuel (hydrogen) and an oxidant (oxygen), bypassing the intermediate step of combustion. The fundamental principle was discovered by **Sir William Grove** in **1839** and is often referred to as the inverse of electrolysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Fuel Cell as a "Refillable Battery that Breathes":**
> - A conventional battery is like a sealed water bottle — once the water is consumed, you must replace the entire bottle.
> - A fuel cell is like an **electric kettle with a continuous water tap** — as long as you keep feeding it hydrogen and air, it keeps producing electricity, and the only "exhaust" is pure water vapor.
> - The hydrogen acts like the "fuel" at a petrol pump, and the fuel cell stack acts like a "silent, pollution-free engine room" that converts it directly into electricity to drive a vehicle's motor.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Constants (KEEPMEMORIZE):**
> - **Standard cell potential of H₂/O₂ fuel cell:** **1.229 V** (theoretical)
> - **Higher Heating Value (HHV) of Hydrogen:** **141.86 MJ/kg**
> - **Lower Heating Value (LHV) of Hydrogen:** **119.96 MJ/kg**
> - **Energy content per kg of gasoline (equivalent):** **~44 MJ/kg**
> - **Faraday's Constant (F):** **96,485 C/mol**
> - **Universal Gas Constant (R):** **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
> - **Avogadro's Number (Nₐ):** **6.022 × 10²³ /mol**

### 1.4 What is a Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicle (HFCV)?

> [!NOTE]
> A **Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicle (HFCV)**, also called a **Fuel Cell Electric Vehicle (FCEV)**, is a type of electric vehicle that uses a **fuel cell stack** (typically a **Proton Exchange Membrane / PEM fuel cell**) to power an electric traction motor. Hydrogen stored in high-pressure tanks (usually **350 bar** or **700 bar**) is fed into the fuel cell where it electrochemically reacts with atmospheric oxygen to generate electricity.

### 1.5 Conventional Internal Combustion Engine Vehicle (ICEV) - Brief

A **Conventional ICE Vehicle** is a vehicle propelled by an **Internal Combustion Engine** in which the combustion of a hydrocarbon fuel (petrol, diesel, CNG) with an oxidizer (air) occurs in a combustion chamber that is an integral part of the working fluid flow circuit. The combustion releases **thermal energy**, which is converted into **mechanical work** via a piston-crankshaft arrangement.

### 1.6 Module Learning Outcomes (Mapped to KTU 2024 COs)

By the end of this topic, the student should be able to:
1. **CO₂ (Understand):** Trace the historical evolution of fuel cell technology from 1839 to the present.
2. **CO₂ (Apply):** Compare the working principles, emissions, and efficiency of HFCVs and ICEVs.
3. **CO₃ (Analyze):** Evaluate the global policy framework supporting HFCV adoption.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Historical Development of Hydrogen Fuel Cell Technology

The evolution of fuel cell technology spans over **180 years** of scientific inquiry, commercialization efforts, and policy support. Below is a structured chronological narrative aligned with KTU examination expectations.

### 2.1.1 The Foundational Era (1839–1900)

- **1839 — Sir William Robert Grove (Welsh Physicist):**
  - Built the first working fuel cell, called the **"Gas Battery"**.
  - Used **two platinum electrodes** immersed in sulfuric acid electrolyte, with separate supplies of **hydrogen and oxygen**.
  - Generated a small but measurable voltage and current.
  - Grove's principle is essentially the **reverse of water electrolysis**.

- **1889 — Ludwig Mond and Charles Langer:**
  - Coined the term **"Fuel Cell"** for the first time.
  - Improved Grove's design using **coal-derived fuels**, achieving a current density of about **3–4 mA/cm²** with a cell voltage of ~0.73 V.

- **1893 — Friedrich Wilhelm Ostwald:**
  - Provided the **theoretical foundation** of the fuel cell.
  - Experimentally explained the **electrochemical processes** occurring at the electrodes.
  - Defined the roles of the **fuel electrode (anode)**, **oxidant electrode (cathode)**, **electrolyte**, and **electrons/ions** involved.

### 2.1.2 The Space Race Era (1950s–1970s)

- **1950s–1960s — Bacon's Pioneering Work:**
  - **Francis Thomas Bacon** at Cambridge, England, developed the **alkaline fuel cell (AFC)** using **nickel electrodes** and **potassium hydroxide (KOH) electrolyte**.
  - By **1959**, Bacon demonstrated a **5 kW fuel cell stack** capable of powering a forklift.

- **NASA — Gemini and Apollo Missions:**
  - **1960s — Project Gemini** (1962–1966): The **first commercial use of fuel cells**. General Electric (GE) supplied **PEM fuel cells** for the Gemini program, providing electrical power and **drinking water** for astronauts.
  - **1960s — Apollo Missions (1968–1972):** **Alkaline fuel cells (AFC)** built by **Pratt & Whitney** powered the Apollo spacecraft, providing **1.1 kW** continuous power and producing potable water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:**
> The **Apollo Fuel Cell** is the most likely historical question. Each Apollo mission used **3 fuel cell stacks**, providing an average of **600–1400 watts** continuous power and producing **about 0.9 kg of water per hour** — used for astronaut consumption.

### 2.1.3 The Oil Crisis and Research Acceleration (1970s–1990s)

- **1973 — Oil Crisis Impact:**
  - Geopolitical tensions triggered global research into **alternative energy**.
  - The **U.S. Department of Energy (DOE)** and **Japanese METI** significantly increased fuel cell R&D funding.

- **Technology Diversification:**
  - **Phosphoric Acid Fuel Cell (PAFC)** — developed in this era, commercialized in 1992 by **Tokyo Electric Power Company (TEPCO)**.
  - **Molten Carbonate Fuel Cell (MCFC)** — designed for high-temperature stationary power.
  - **Solid Oxide Fuel Cell (SOFC)** — developed by **Westinghouse** (now Siemens Energy).
  - **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** — General Electric and DuPont (Nafion membrane) led development.

- **1993 — Ballard Power Systems (Canada):**
  - Demonstrated the **first PEM fuel cell-powered bus**, marking the **birth of modern HFCV technology**.

### 2.1.4 The Commercialization Era (2000s–Present)

- **2003 — U.S. President George W. Bush:** Launched the **Hydrogen Fuel Initiative** ($1.2 billion over 5 years).
- **2008 — Honda FCX Clarity:** First **mass-produced HFCV sedan** leased in Japan and the U.S.
- **2014 — Toyota Mirai:** First **commercial HFCV sedan** sold to retail customers. **"Mirai"** means *"future"* in Japanese.
  - Range: **~500 km** on a single tank.
  - Tank pressure: **70 MPa (700 bar)**.
- **2015–2020:** Hyundai NEXO, Honda Clarity, Mercedes-Benz GLC F-CELL.
- **2020–2024:** Global policy surge, with **EU, Japan, South Korea, China, and India** announcing major hydrogen roadmaps.

## 2.2 Detailed Comparison: HFCVs vs. Conventional ICE Vehicles

The following structured analysis addresses the KTU 2024 Scheme's emphasis on **comparative analysis** under **CO₂ (Understand)** and **CO₃ (Apply)**.

### 2.2.1 Energy Conversion Pathway

| Parameter | HFCV (Fuel Cell) | Conventional ICEV |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Hydrogen (H₂) | Petrol/Diesel/CNG |
| Conversion Mechanism | **Electrochemical** (no combustion) | **Thermochemical** (combustion) |
| Intermediate Steps | H₂ → Electricity → Motor | Fuel → Heat → Mechanical → Wheels |
| Theoretical Efficiency | **83%** (HHV basis) | **~30–40%** (Carnot limited) |
| Practical Efficiency | **50–60%** | **20–30%** (incl. transmission losses) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight:** The **Carnot efficiency limit** does not apply to fuel cells because there is **no heat-to-work conversion**. This is the fundamental reason HFCVs can theoretically achieve **2× the efficiency** of ICEVs.

### 2.2.2 Emissions Profile

| Emission Type | HFCV | Conventional ICEV |
|---|---|---|
| **CO₂ (Tank-to-Wheel)** | **Zero** | 120–250 g/km |
| **NOx** | **Zero** | 0.05–0.5 g/km |
| **CO, HC, PM** | **Zero** | Significant |
| **Water Vapor** | Yes (pure H₂O) | Yes (along with other gases) |
| **Well-to-Wheel CO₂** | Depends on H₂ source (green = near zero) | 160–320 g/km |

### 2.2.3 Refueling and Range

| Parameter | HFCV (700 bar) | ICEV (Petrol) |
|---|---|---|
| **Refueling Time** | **5–10 minutes** | 3–5 minutes |
| **Driving Range** | **400–650 km** | 500–900 km |
| **Energy Density (system)** | Lower | Higher (volumetric) |
| **Energy Density (gravimetric)** | Comparable to gasoline | Higher |

### 2.2.4 Operational and Mechanical Characteristics

| Parameter | HFCV | ICEV |
|---|---|---|
| **Moving Parts** | Few (rotating motor) | Many (pistons, valves, crankshaft) |
| **Noise Level** | **Very low (~50 dB)** | High (~70–80 dB) |
| **Vibration** | Minimal | Significant |
| **Cold Start Capability** | Challenging (PEM) | Excellent |
| **Maintenance Cost** | Low (no oil change) | High (regular servicing) |
| **Lifespan** | 5,000–8,000 hours (stack) | 200,000–300,000 km |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following formulas are essential for solving numerical and theoretical questions in the KTU 2024 Scheme examination on this topic.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E^0 = -\dfrac{\Delta G^0}{nF}$ | Standard cell potential from Gibbs free energy | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $E_{cell} = 1.229 - 0.00085(T - 298.15)$ | Temperature correction for PEM cell voltage | V (T in K) |
| 3 | $\eta_{FC} = \dfrac{V_{cell}}{1.229} \times 100\%$ | Fuel cell efficiency (HHV basis) | % |
| 4 | $\eta_{ICE,max} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$ | Carnot efficiency (ICE upper limit) | % |
| 5 | $\eta_{system} = \eta_{FC} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{transmission}$ | HFCV drivetrain efficiency | % |
| 6 | $P_{out} = V_{cell} \times I \times N_{cells}$ | Power output of a fuel cell stack | Watts |
| 7 | $E_{stored} = m_{H_2} \times LHV$ | Energy stored in hydrogen tank | Joules |
| 8 | $CO_2 = FC \times EF \times D$ | Total CO₂ emissions (Fuel Consumption × Emission Factor × Distance) | grams |

**Where:**
- $\Delta G^0$ = Standard Gibbs free energy change (J/mol)
- $n$ = Number of electrons transferred (= 2 for H₂)
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
- $N_{cells}$ = Number of cells in the stack
- $m_{H_2}$ = Mass of hydrogen (kg)
- $LHV$ = Lower Heating Value of H₂ = **119.96 MJ/kg**

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The fuel cell technology studied here is directly applied in:
- **Automotive Industry:** Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO, Honda Clarity.
- **Heavy-Duty Transport:** Ballard Power Systems fuel cell buses, hydrogen-powered trucks (Hyundai XCIENT).
- **Stationary Power Generation:** Bloom Energy SOFC units, Plug Power PAFC installations.
- **Backup Power:** Data centers, hospitals, telecom towers.
- **Space Exploration:** NASA's Space Launch System (SLS), Lunar Gateway, Mars rovers (future).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Efficiency of a Hydrogen Fuel Cell (HHV Basis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation is a high-weightage KTU 2024 question. Practice writing this out completely.**

### 3.1.1 Given Data
- Reaction: $\text{H}_2 + \dfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O (l)}$
- $\Delta G^0$ (Standard Gibbs free energy) = **−237.13 kJ/mol**
- $\Delta H^0$ (Standard enthalpy change) = **−285.83 kJ/mol** (HHV basis)
- Number of electrons transferred, $n = 2$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Maximum Electrical Work from the Reaction**

The maximum useful electrical work obtainable from a galvanic cell is equal to the negative of the Gibbs free energy change:

$$W_{max} = -\Delta G^0$$

**Step 2: Substitute the Standard Value**

$$W_{max} = -(-237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}) = +237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

**Step 3: Theoretical Cell Voltage from Gibbs Free Energy**

The electrical work done per mole of electrons transferred is given by:

$$W_{max} = nFE^0$$

Rearranging for $E^0$ (Standard Electrode Potential):

$$E^0 = \dfrac{-\Delta G^0}{nF}$$

**Step 4: Substitute Numerical Values**

$$E^0 = \dfrac{237.13 \times 10^3 \text{ J/mol}}{2 \times 96{,}485 \text{ C/mol}}$$

$$E^0 = \dfrac{237{,}130}{192{,}970} \text{ V}$$

$$E^0 = 1.229 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5: Fuel Cell Efficiency Definition**

The efficiency of a fuel cell is defined as the ratio of useful electrical energy output to the total chemical energy input (HHV basis):

$$\eta_{FC} = \dfrac{W_{max}}{\Delta H^0} = \dfrac{-\Delta G^0}{-\Delta H^0}$$

**Step 6: Calculate the Theoretical Efficiency**

$$\eta_{FC} = \dfrac{237.13 \text{ kJ/mol}}{285.83 \text{ kJ/mol}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{FC} = 0.8295 \text{ or } 82.95\%}$$

**Step 7: Engineering Interpretation**

This means that, theoretically, **~83% of the chemical energy** stored in hydrogen can be converted directly into electrical energy. The remaining **~17%** is lost as heat (mainly due to entropy generation, with $T\Delta S$ representing the irreversibility).

## 3.2 Derivation: Comparison of Practical System Efficiencies

### 3.2.1 HFCV System Efficiency (Toyota Mirai – Gen 2)

**Given:**
- Fuel cell efficiency at operating point: $\eta_{FC} = 55\%$
- Electric motor efficiency: $\eta_{motor} = 95\%$
- Transmission efficiency: $\eta_{trans} = 96\%$
- Power electronics efficiency: $\eta_{PE} = 97\%$

**Calculation:**

$$\eta_{HFCV,system} = \eta_{FC} \times \eta_{PE} \times \eta_{motor} \times \eta_{trans}$$

Substituting values:

$$\eta_{HFCV,system} = 0.55 \times 0.97 \times 0.95 \times 0.96$$

$$\eta_{HFCV,system} = 0.4870 \text{ or } 48.7\%$$

### 3.2.2 Conventional ICEV System Efficiency (Typical Petrol Car)

**Given:**
- Engine thermal efficiency (Otto cycle, gasoline): $\eta_{engine} = 30\%$
- Transmission efficiency: $\eta_{trans} = 88\%$
- Accessories (alternator, pumps, etc.) loss: $\eta_{acc} = 95\%$

**Calculation:**

$$\eta_{ICEV,system} = \eta_{engine} \times \eta_{trans} \times \eta_{acc}$$

Substituting values:

$$\eta_{ICEV,system} = 0.30 \times 0.88 \times 0.95$$

$$\eta_{ICEV,system} = 0.2508 \text{ or } 25.1\%$$

### 3.2.3 Analytical Comparison

$$\dfrac{\eta_{HFCV}}{\eta_{ICEV}} = \dfrac{48.7}{25.1} = 1.94$$

**Conclusion:** The HFCV drivetrain is approximately **1.94× more efficient** than the conventional ICEV drivetrain — meaning an HFCV requires only **~52% of the primary energy** to travel the same distance.

## 3.3 Derivation: Energy Stored in a Hydrogen Tank

### 3.3.1 Given (Toyota Mirai Gen 2)
- Hydrogen tank pressure: $P = 70 \text{ MPa} = 700 \text{ bar}$
- Hydrogen mass in tank: $m_{H_2} = 5.6 \text{ kg}$ (approximate, Gen 1 was 4.6 kg)
- LHV of hydrogen: $119.96 \text{ MJ/kg}$

### 3.3.2 Calculation

**Step 1: Energy Stored**

$$E_{stored} = m_{H_2} \times LHV$$

$$E_{stored} = 5.6 \text{ kg} \times 119.96 \text{ MJ/kg}$$

$$E_{stored} = 671.78 \text{ MJ}$$

**Step 2: Convert to kWh (1 kWh = 3.6 MJ)**

$$E_{stored} = \dfrac{671.78}{3.6} = 186.6 \text{ kWh}$$

**Step 3: Driving Range Estimate**

Assuming a vehicle consumes **0.7–1.0 kWh/km** (electric equivalent):

$$R = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{\text{consumption}} = \dfrac{186.6}{0.85} \approx 219.5 \text{ km (if all H}_2 \text{ used directly)}$$

But the fuel cell operates at **~50% efficiency**, so the **practical range** is approximately:

$$R_{practical} = 219.5 \times 0.55 \approx 500 \text{ km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This simple calculation explains why **higher-pressure hydrogen tanks (700 bar)** and **larger tank volumes** are essential for commercial HFCV competitiveness. The volumetric energy density of hydrogen at 700 bar is approximately **4.4 MJ/L**, compared to gasoline's **~32 MJ/L** — a major engineering challenge.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (HFCV vs ICEV)

| Attribute | Weight (KTU priority) | HFCV | ICEV | Winner |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Well-to-Wheel Efficiency** | ★★★★★ | 22–32% (with green H₂: 28–36%) | 13–20% | HFCV (if H₂ is green) |
| **Tailpipe Emissions** | ★★★★★ | 0 g CO₂/km | 120–250 g CO₂/km | HFCV |
| **Refueling Time** | ★★★★ | 5–10 min | 3–5 min | ICEV (slight edge) |
| **Range** | ★★★★ | 400–650 km | 500–900 km | ICEV |
| **Noise & Vibration** | ★★★ | Very low | High | HFCV |
| **Maintenance** | ★★★ | Low (no oil, fewer parts) | High | HFCV |
| **Infrastructure Cost** | ★★★★★ | Very high (new H₂ stations) | Low (existing) | ICEV |
| **Vehicle Cost** | ★★★★★ | $50,000–$80,000 | $20,000–$40,000 | ICEV |
| **Cold Start (-20°C)** | ★★★ | Requires thermal mgmt | Excellent | ICEV |
| **Energy Density (volumetric)** | ★★★★ | Low (~4.4 MJ/L @ 700 bar) | High (~32 MJ/L) | ICEV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** When answering "Compare HFCV and ICEV" questions, always structure your answer using the **5-point framework**: (1) Energy Source, (2) Conversion Method, (3) Efficiency, (4) Emissions, (5) Challenges/Limitations.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part A questions test **CO₁ (Remember)** and **CO₂ (Understand)** cognitive levels. Answers should be **3–4 sentences** with key values in bold.

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a fuel cell. Who is credited with inventing the first fuel cell, and what was it called?**

**Model Answer:**
A **fuel cell** is an electrochemical device that converts the chemical energy of a fuel (hydrogen) and an oxidant (oxygen) directly into **electrical energy**, with water and heat as by-products. **Sir William Robert Grove**, a Welsh physicist, is credited with inventing the first fuel cell in **1839**, and he called it the **"Gas Battery."** It consisted of two platinum electrodes immersed in sulfuric acid with separate supplies of hydrogen and oxygen, producing a small but measurable voltage.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List any three advantages of Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicles (HFCVs) over conventional ICE vehicles.**

**Model Answer:**
The three main advantages of HFCVs over ICEVs are:
1. **Zero tailpipe emissions** — the only by-product is **pure water vapor**, eliminating CO₂, NOx, CO, and particulate matter.
2. **Higher energy conversion efficiency** — HFCVs achieve **~50–60%** practical efficiency, compared to **20–30%** for ICEVs, because they bypass the **Carnot limit**.
3. **Lower noise and vibration** — fuel cell systems have very few moving parts, producing noise levels of **~50 dB** versus **70–80 dB** for ICEVs.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Both options are provided below.

### ⭐ OPTION A: `[KTU University Exam - Model Question]`

**Question A:**
**(a)** Explain in detail the historical development of hydrogen fuel cell technology, highlighting at least **five key milestones** from 1839 to the present. Mention the role of **NASA** in popularizing this technology. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicles (HFCVs) and conventional Internal Combustion Engine Vehicles (ICEVs) on the basis of: energy conversion mechanism, efficiency, emissions, refueling time, and key challenges. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL ANSWER (a) — Historical Development (7 Marks)

The historical development of hydrogen fuel cell technology can be divided into the following key phases:

**1. Discovery Phase (1839–1900):**
In **1839**, **Sir William Robert Grove** demonstrated the first working fuel cell, the **"Gas Battery,"** using platinum electrodes in sulfuric acid with separate supplies of H₂ and O₂. In **1889**, **Ludwig Mond and Charles Langer** coined the term **"fuel cell"** and improved the design to use coal-derived fuels. In **1893**, **Wilhelm Ostwald** provided the theoretical foundation by explaining the electrochemical processes occurring at the electrodes.

**2. Mid-20th Century Development (1950s–1970s):**
**Francis Thomas Bacon** at Cambridge, England, developed the **alkaline fuel cell (AFC)** with nickel electrodes and KOH electrolyte, demonstrating a **5 kW stack** by 1959. The most significant event was the **adoption of fuel cells by NASA**. General Electric (GE) supplied **PEM fuel cells** for the **Gemini program (1962–1966)**, providing both electrical power and drinking water. Later, **Pratt & Whitney's AFC** powered the **Apollo missions (1968–1972)**, with three stacks per mission providing **1.1 kW** continuous power and producing **~0.9 kg of water per hour** for astronauts.

**3. Oil Crisis & Diversification (1970s–1990s):**
The **1973 oil crisis** triggered massive R&D investment, leading to the development of **PAFC, MCFC, SOFC**, and improved **PEMFC** technologies. In **1993**, **Ballard Power Systems (Canada)** demonstrated the first **PEM fuel cell-powered bus**, marking the birth of modern HFCV technology.

**4. Commercialization Era (2000s–Present):**
The U.S. **Hydrogen Fuel Initiative (2003)** allocated **$1.2 billion** over 5 years. Honda launched the **FCX Clarity (2008)**, and Toyota launched the **Mirai (2014)** — the first mass-produced commercial HFCV, with a **500 km** range and **700 bar** hydrogen tanks. Hyundai's **NEXO (2018)** further advanced the technology.

**5. Global Policy Era (2020–2024):**
The **EU Hydrogen Strategy (2020)**, **India's National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023)**, and similar global programs have positioned HFCVs as a cornerstone of the **decarbonized transport** future.

#### MODEL ANSWER (b) — HFCV vs ICEV Comparison (7 Marks)

| Comparison Aspect | HFCV (Fuel Cell) | Conventional ICEV |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy Source** | Hydrogen (H₂) stored at 700 bar | Petrol/Diesel/CNG |
| **Conversion Method** | **Electrochemical** (no combustion) | **Thermochemical** (combustion) |
| **Theoretical Efficiency** | **~83%** (HHV, not Carnot-limited) | **~30–40%** (Carnot-limited) |
| **Practical Efficiency** | **50–60%** (system) | **20–30%** (system) |
| **Tailpipe Emissions** | **Zero CO₂**, only water vapor | **120–250 g CO₂/km** plus NOx, CO, PM |
| **Refueling Time** | **5–10 minutes** | 3–5 minutes |
| **Driving Range** | **400–650 km** | 500–900 km |
| **Noise Level** | **~50 dB** (very low) | **~70–80 dB** (high) |
| **Moving Parts** | Few (motor + compressor) | Many (pistons, valves, etc.) |
| **Cold Start** | Challenging (PEM hydration) | Excellent |
| **Key Challenges** | H₂ infrastructure, tank cost, green H₂ | CO₂, air pollution, oil dependence |

**Conclusion:** HFCVs offer significant advantages in efficiency and emissions but face major challenges in **hydrogen production, storage, distribution, and cost**. The **Well-to-Wheel efficiency** of HFCVs becomes competitive only when hydrogen is produced from **renewable sources (green hydrogen)**.

---

### ⭐ OPTION B: `[KTU University Exam - Model Question]`

**Question B:**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of a Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicle (HFCV). Identify the function of the **fuel cell stack**, **electric motor**, **high-pressure hydrogen tank**, and **power inverter** in the system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **global initiatives and policies** promoting HFCV adoption. Provide specific examples from at least **three countries** (e.g., Japan, EU, India) and their targets. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL ANSWER (a) — Working of HFCV (7 Marks)

A **Hydrogen Fuel Cell Vehicle (HFCV)** is a zero-emission electric vehicle that uses a **Proton Exchange Membrane (PEM) fuel cell stack** to generate electricity on-board from compressed hydrogen gas.

**Block Diagram (Textual Representation):**

$$\text{H}_2 \text{ Tank (700 bar)} \rightarrow \text{Regulator} \rightarrow \text{Fuel Cell Stack} \rightarrow \text{DC-DC Converter} \rightarrow \text{Power Inverter} \rightarrow \text{Electric Motor} \rightarrow \text{Transmission} \rightarrow \text{Wheels}$$

**Working Principle:**

1. **Hydrogen Storage and Delivery:** Hydrogen gas is stored in **carbon-fiber reinforced Type IV tanks** at **700 bar (70 MPa)**. A pressure regulator reduces the pressure before the gas enters the fuel cell stack. The tank typically holds **4.6–6.0 kg of H₂**, providing a range of **400–650 km**.

2. **Fuel Cell Stack:** The **PEM fuel cell stack** is the heart of the HFCV. It consists of **300–400 individual cells** stacked in series, each producing ~0.6–0.7 V, giving a total stack voltage of **250–650 V DC**. At the anode, H₂ is split into protons (H⁺) and electrons (e⁻). Protons pass through the membrane to the cathode, while electrons flow through the external circuit, generating **direct current (DC)** electricity.

3. **DC-DC Converter and Power Inverter:** The DC-DC converter **boosts the stack voltage** to a stable level (typically **650 V**). The power inverter then converts **DC to AC** to drive the **three-phase AC induction motor** (or permanent magnet synchronous motor).

4. **High-Voltage Battery (Buffer):** A small lithium-ion battery (typically **1–2 kWh**) is included to:
   - Capture **regenerative braking energy**.
   - Provide **peak power** during acceleration.
   - Support the fuel cell during transient loads.

5. **Electric Traction Motor:** The motor produces **113–134 kW (152–180 hp)** for the Toyota Mirai, providing smooth, instant torque. A **single-speed reduction gearbox** transmits power to the wheels.

6. **Thermal Management:** Because fuel cells generate heat, a sophisticated **radiator and coolant system** maintains the stack at **60–80°C** for optimal PEM performance. Below **0°C**, a **heater** is required to evaporate excess water and prevent membrane damage.

7. **Exhaust:** The only tailpipe emission is **pure water vapor**, typically **~9 liters per 100 km**.

#### MODEL ANSWER (b) — Global Initiatives and Policies (7 Marks)

**1. Japan — Basic Hydrogen Strategy (2017, revised 2023):**
- Japan was the **first country** to adopt a national hydrogen strategy.
- Target: **800,000 FCEVs** on roads by **2030**.
- Plan to build **900 hydrogen refueling stations** by 2030.
- Government subsidies: **~₹20–30 lakh per HFCV** in initial rollout.
- Success story: **Toyota Mirai (2014)** and **Honda Clarity (2016)**.

**2. European Union — EU Hydrogen Strategy (2020):**
- Ambitious target: **10 million tonnes of renewable hydrogen** produced in the EU by **2030**.
- **Clean Hydrogen Partnership:** Funding of **€2.1 billion (2021–2027)**.
- **AFIR (Alternative Fuels Infrastructure Regulation, 2023):** Mandates hydrogen refueling stations every **200 km** along **TEN-T core network** by 2030.
- Target: **~5.4 million FCEVs** in Europe by 2030.

**3. India — National Green Hydrogen Mission (2023):**
- Outlay: **₹19,744 crore (~USD 2.4 billion)**.
- Target: **5 million tonnes of green hydrogen** production by **2030**.
- Pilot projects for HFCV adoption in **heavy-duty transport (buses, trucks)**.
- Targets: **100 hydrogen refueling stations** in major cities by 2030.

**4. United States — Hydrogen Shot Initiative (2021, DOE):**
- Target: Reduce the cost of clean hydrogen to **$1/kg** within a decade ("**1-1-1**" goal).
- **$9.5 billion** allocated for clean hydrogen under the **Bipartisan Infrastructure Law (2021)**.
- Target: **50 million tonnes** of clean hydrogen annually by 2050.

**5. South Korea — H2 Economy Roadmap (2019):**
- Target: **6.2 million FCEVs** by **2040**.
- **1,200 hydrogen refueling stations** by 2040.
- **Hyundai NEXO** is the flagship commercial HFCV.

**6. China — 14th Five-Year Plan:**
- Target: **50,000 FCEVs** by **2025**.
- **1,000 hydrogen refueling stations** by 2025.
- Heavy investment in **commercial vehicle (bus/truck) hydrogen technology**.

**Conclusion:** Global policies are aligned with a **net-zero by 2050** pathway, and HFCVs play a critical role in **decarbonizing heavy-duty and long-range transport**, complementing battery EVs in the broader e-mobility transition.

---

## 5.3 KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Mistakes in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing the inventors:** Grove invented the fuel cell in **1839**, but Mond **coined the term** in **1889** and Ostwald provided **theoretical explanation** in **1893**. Do NOT interchange them.
> 2. **Confusing NASA programs:** **Gemini** used **PEM fuel cells (GE)**, while **Apollo** used **Alkaline fuel cells (Pratt & Whitney)**. Many students write this wrong.
> 3. **Wrong efficiency values:** Theoretical HFCV efficiency is **~83%** (HHV) — NOT **100%**. Practical HFCV system efficiency is **~50–60%**, not 80–90%.
> 4. **Confusing Well-to-Wheel (WTW) with Tank-to-Wheel (TTW):** HFCVs are **zero emission TTW**, but WTW emissions depend on **hydrogen source** (green, blue, or grey). If you state "HFCV is always zero emission" without qualifying, you lose marks.
> 5. **Carnot limit confusion:** ICEVs are subject to **Carnot's theorem**, but fuel cells are **NOT** (because they don't involve heat-to-work conversion). This is a frequently asked conceptual question.
> 6. **Forgetting the date of Toyota Mirai:** It was **December 2014** (commercial launch in Japan), not 2013 or 2015.
> 7. **Mixing up 350 bar vs 700 bar:** The Mirai uses **700 bar (70 MPa)** Type IV tanks for higher range.

## 5.4 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU exam prep):**

- ✅ Fuel cell = **electrochemical** energy conversion device (NOT combustion).
- ✅ Invented by **Sir William Grove in 1839**, called **"Gas Battery"**.
- ✅ Term "Fuel Cell" coined by **Ludwig Mond in 1889**.
- ✅ Theoretical foundation by **Wilhelm Ostwald in 1893**.
- ✅ First practical AFC by **Francis Bacon in 1959** (5 kW).
- ✅ **NASA Gemini** (1962–66) used **PEM fuel cells by GE**.
- ✅ **NASA Apollo** (1968–72) used **AFC by Pratt & Whitney** (1.1 kW, water for drinking).
- ✅ First modern HFCV bus: **Ballard, 1993**.
- ✅ First commercial HFCV: **Toyota Mirai, 2014** (700 bar, 500 km range, "Mirai" = future).
- ✅ Standard H₂/O₂ cell voltage: **1.229 V**.
- ✅ Theoretical FC efficiency (HHV): **~83%**; practical: **50–60%**.
- ✅ Carnot limit **does NOT apply** to fuel cells.
- ✅ LHV of H₂: **119.96 MJ/kg**; HHV: **141.86 MJ/kg**.
- ✅ HFCV tailpipe emissions: **zero CO₂, only water vapor** (~9 L/100 km).
- ✅ Refueling time: **5–10 minutes**; Range: **400–650 km**.
- ✅ **Green hydrogen** (renewable electrolysis) = only path to true zero WTW emissions.
- ✅ **Japan**: 800,000 FCEVs by 2030. **EU**: 10 Mt green H₂ by 2030. **India**: 5 Mt green H₂ by 2030. **US**: $1/kg H₂ by 2031.
- ✅ HFCV is **~1.94× more efficient** than ICEV at the drivetrain level.
- ✅ Key challenge: **Hydrogen production, storage, and distribution infrastructure**.

---
