## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
List any **three advantages** of Type IV composite hydrogen tanks over Type I all-metal tanks for FCEV applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

1. **Higher gravimetric density** — Type IV tanks using carbon fiber overwrap store ~4–6 wt% H₂ vs. ~1–2 wt% for Type I, enabling 500+ km driving range.
2. **Higher operating pressure capability** — Type IV tanks safely operate at 700 bar vs. typical 200–300 bar for Type I, doubling on-board storage mass per unit volume.
3. **Superior corrosion resistance and fatigue life** — Polymer liner is immune to hydrogen embrittlement (a critical failure mode in steel tanks), and composite overwrap has fatigue life exceeding 20 years per ECPE/AIAG standards.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
What is **ortho-para hydrogen conversion** and why is it critical during LH₂ storage?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Ortho-para conversion refers to the spontaneous spin-isomerization of molecular hydrogen between two nuclear spin configurations: **ortho-hydrogen** (parallel nuclear spins, 75% at room temperature) and **para-hydrogen** (anti-parallel spins, 99.79% at 20 K). The conversion is **exothermic**, releasing **527 kJ/kg** of heat. If normal (75% ortho) hydrogen is liquefied without prior catalytic conversion, this heat of conversion is released slowly over several days inside the cryogenic tank, **continuously boiling off the liquid** and causing unacceptable BOR. Industrial LH₂ plants therefore use iron hydroxide or chromium oxide catalysts to pre-convert H₂ to >95% para form before liquefaction — a **mandatory step per ISO 13984**.

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Compressed Gas Hydrogen Storage**

**Q3(a) [7 marks]** With a neat schematic, explain the construction and working of a **Type IV 700 bar hydrogen storage tank** used in modern FCEVs. Discuss why carbon fiber composites are mandatory at this pressure level.

**Model Answer:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
A Type IV tank is a pressure vessel with the following layered construction:
- **Liner:** HDPE polymer (high density polyethylene), 3–5 mm thick, providing the gas-tight inner surface and permeation barrier.
- **Permeation barrier:** EVOH (ethylene vinyl alcohol) co-extruded layer preventing H₂ molecular diffusion through the polymer.
- **Structural overwrap:** Continuous carbon-fiber tows impregnated in epoxy resin, wound in two patterns — helical (load-bearing along axis) and hoop (resisting radial pressure) — totaling 20–30 mm thickness.
- **Boss fittings:** Aluminum or steel threaded ports at the ends for valves and pressure relief devices.
- **Valve assembly:** Combination of manual shut-off valve, **TPRD** (thermally activated pressure relief device, set at 110 °C), and **ECVT** (electronic control valve for telematics/diagnostics).

**Working (2 marks):**
Hydrogen is charged from the dispenser at 87.5 MPa (filling pressure) through the ECVT. The tank stores H₂ until vehicle demand, when it is delivered to the fuel cell stack via a pressure regulator. The TPRD provides passive safety: in a fire scenario, the fusible alloy melts at 110 °C, releasing H₂ upward through a vent stack at controlled rate, preventing catastrophic tank rupture.

**Why Carbon Fiber is Mandatory (2 marks):**
At 700 bar service pressure, the hoop stress in a thin-walled cylindrical vessel is computed by **Barlow's formula**:
$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2t}$$
For $P = 70$ MPa and $D = 300$ mm, an all-steel tank requires $t \approx 40$ mm, resulting in a tank mass of ~250 kg — exceeding the vehicle's payload capacity. Carbon-fiber composites have tensile strength of ~4000 MPa (vs. ~500 MPa for steel) at one-fifth the density (1.8 vs. 7.8 g/cm³), achieving the same burst pressure at a total tank mass of ~100 kg for 5 kg H₂ storage.

---

**Q3(b) [7 marks]** A 700 bar hydrogen refueling station fills a 60 L Type IV tank from 100 bar to 700 bar in 4 minutes. The station's chiller pre-cools the incoming H₂ to −40 °C. Determine: (i) the mass of H₂ delivered, (ii) the average mass flow rate, and (iii) comment on whether the SAE J2601 ΔT limit of 85 °C is satisfied during this fill.

Assume $Z_{avg} = 1.28$ and initial/final temperatures are 298 K and 313 K (40 °C rise without chiller).

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Mass delivered (3 marks):**

Using real gas equation:
$$m = \frac{P V}{Z R_{sp} T}$$

For initial state ($P_1 = 100$ bar, $T_1 = 298$ K, $Z_1 = 1.05$):
$$m_1 = \frac{(100 \times 10^5)(60 \times 10^{-3})}{(1.05)(4124)(298)} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^5}{1.290 \times 10^6} = 0.465 \text{ kg}$$

For final state ($P_2 = 700$ bar, $T_2 = 313$ K, $Z_2 = 1.32$):
$$m_2 = \frac{(700 \times 10^5)(60 \times 10^{-3})}{(1.32)(4124)(313)} = \frac{4.2 \times 10^6}{1.704 \times 10^6} = 2.466 \text{ kg}$$

$$\Delta m = m_2 - m_1 = 2.466 - 0.465 = \boxed{2.001 \text{ kg of H}_2}$$

**(ii) Average mass flow rate (2 marks):**
$$\dot{m}_{avg} = \frac{\Delta m}{\Delta t} = \frac{2.001 \text{ kg}}{240 \text{ s}} = \boxed{8.34 \text{ g/s}}$$

**(iii) SAE J2601 compliance check (2 marks):**
Without chilling, adiabatic temperature rise for H₂ at these conditions is:
$$\Delta T_{adiabatic} \approx \frac{T_1}{Z} \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(Z-1)/Z} - 1 \right] \approx 102 \text{ °C}$$

Without chiller: $\Delta T \approx 102$ °C, **violating** the 85 °C limit.
With chiller at −40 °C inlet (233 K): effective rise $\approx 102 - 65 = 37$ °C, **well within** limit. **Chilling is mandatory.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Liquid Hydrogen Storage & Safety**

**Q4(a) [7 marks]** Explain the cryogenic storage of liquid hydrogen in a road tanker with a suitable block diagram. Discuss the role of **multi-layer insulation (MLI)** and **ortho-para conversion catalysts** in maintaining LH₂ quality.

**Model Answer:**

**LH₂ Road Tanker Construction (3 marks):**
A typical LH₂ road tanker consists of:
- **Inner vessel:** Stainless steel 304L, double-walled, holding up to 65,000 L of LH₂.
- **Outer jacket:** Carbon steel pressure shell.
- **Annular space insulation:** High vacuum ($10^{-6}$ mbar) + **MLI** consisting of 30–60 alternating layers of double-aluminized Mylar (each ~6 μm) separated by low-conductivity Spacers (fiberglass net). Effective $k_{eff} \approx 5 \times 10^{-5}$ W/(m·K).
- **Pressure control system:** Pressure building coil, vent stack with flame arrester, and economizer circuit.
- **Instrumentation:** Level gauge (differential pressure or capacitance), temperature sensors, safety relief valves, and pressure regulators.

**Working (2 marks):**
LH₂ is loaded from the production plant at 20.37 K and 1.05 bara, transported under vacuum-insulated conditions, and delivered to refueling stations. During transit, residual heat ingress (~30–50 W) vaporizes a small fraction of LH₂; the boil-off vapor is collected in a pressure building coil that maintains the vessel at 4–8 bar to suppress further vaporization. On arrival, LH₂ is transferred via vacuum-jacketed lines using a cryogenic pump or by pressurization.

**MLI Role (1 mark):**
MLI reduces radiative heat transfer by **N+1 reflective layers** blocking infrared radiation. Each aluminized layer reflects >99% of incident IR. Combined with vacuum, this reduces heat ingress by ~1000× compared to still air, dropping BOR to ~0.3%/day.

**Ortho-Para Catalyst Role (1 mark):**
A bed of Fe(OH)₃ or Cr₂O₃ catalyst is installed in the LH₂ production plant (not on the truck) to pre-convert normal hydrogen to >95% para-hydrogen. Without this, the slow exothermic para-ortho conversion (releasing **527 kJ/kg**) inside the truck tank would vaporize up to 50% of LH₂ in 7–10 days, making transport uneconomical.

---

**Q4(b) [7 marks]** List and explain **any four unique safety hazards** of hydrogen compared to gasoline. Describe two engineered mitigation measures used in hydrogen refueling stations.

**Model Answer:**

**Hazard 1: Wide Flammability Range (2 marks)**
Hydrogen is flammable in air from **4% to 75%** by volume, versus gasoline which has a narrow flammability range of **1.4% to 7.6%**. This means hydrogen-air mixtures are flammable under most leak scenarios, whereas gasoline vapor requires specific concentration conditions.

**Hazard 2: Extremely Low Ignition Energy (1 mark)**
H₂ requires only **0.02 mJ** to ignite — comparable to the static electricity from human skin. Gasoline needs **0.24 mJ**. This means almost any spark (including ESD) can ignite hydrogen, demanding intrinsically safe electrical systems (ATEX/IECEx certified) throughout the infrastructure.

**Hazard 3: Invisible Flame (1 mark)**
Hydrogen flames are nearly transparent in daylight (luminance ~10 cd/m² vs. ~10,000 cd/m² for gasoline). Operators cannot visually detect a hydrogen fire, requiring **IR/UV flame detectors** at every refueling bay.

**Hazard 4: High Diffusivity and Buoyancy (1 mark)**
H₂ has diffusion coefficient of **6.1 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** (vs. 5 × 10⁻⁶ for gasoline vapor) and rises at 1.2 m/s in still air. It escapes through tiny leaks and accumulates in **enclosed spaces at ceilings**, demanding ceiling-level leak sensors and 6+ air changes per hour ventilation.

**Engineered Mitigation Measures (2 marks):**
1. **Catalytic Recombiners:** Passive Pd/Al₂O₃ catalysts installed in vent stacks that oxidize vented H₂ with trace O₂, converting it to water vapor at <100 °C — eliminating the explosion risk of vented H₂.
2. **Forced Ventilation with Redundancy:** Hydrogen detection sensors at <25% LFL trigger automatic shutdown and activate N+1 redundant exhaust fans rated for 6 air changes per hour, with explosion-proof motors per IEC 60079.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forget to use real gas Z-factor** — students commonly use ideal gas equation, leading to 25–30% mass error in 700 bar calculations. Always state Z assumption.
> 2. **Confuse latent heat units** — boil-off mass calculations fail when students use J vs kJ inconsistently.
> 3. **Misidentifying Type III vs Type IV** — Type III uses **metal liner** (Al), Type IV uses **polymer liner** (HDPE). This is a high-frequency KTU mistake worth 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Confusing ortho-para conversion catalyst** with storage vessel component — the catalyst is in the **liquefaction plant**, not on the storage tank itself.
> 5. **Skipping units in derivations** — KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark per missing unit in numerical problems.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)**

- ✅ **CGH₂ storage** uses **Type IV tanks at 700 bar** with HDPE liner + carbon-fiber overwrap.
- ✅ Real gas equation $m = \frac{PV}{ZRT}$ requires $Z \approx 1.30$ for H₂ at 700 bar — **never assume ideal gas** at high pressure.
- ✅ **Barlow's formula** $\sigma_h = \frac{PD}{2t}$ explains why carbon fiber is mandatory at 70 MPa.
- ✅ **LH₂ storage** is cryogenic at 20.37 K with density 70.85 kg/m³ and latent heat 445.6 kJ/kg.
- ✅ **Boil-off rate (BOR)** of 0.1–0.3%/day for properly designed automotive LH₂ tanks with MLI + vacuum.
- ✅ **Ortho-para conversion** releases **527 kJ/kg** — pre-conversion in liquefaction plant is essential.
- ✅ Hydrogen **flammability range 4–75%**, **ignition energy 0.02 mJ**, **invisible flame**.
- ✅ **SAE J2601** limits tank ΔT to **85 °C** during refueling, requiring **chiller at −40 °C**.
- ✅ Hydrogen refueling stations cost **$2.5–4.5 million USD** with 4-bank cascade storage.
- ✅ Safety hierarchy: **Inherent design → Engineering controls → Detection → Mitigation → Administrative**.
- ✅ **MLI** with 30–60 layers gives $k_{eff} \approx 5 \times 10^{-5}$ W/(m·K).
- ✅ **TPRD** (Thermally activated PRD) at 110 °C is mandatory passive safety on every Type IV tank.
- ✅ **Catalytic recombiners** + **redundant forced ventilation** are the two key engineered safety controls at refueling stations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Energy Storage in Hybrid and Electric Vehicles: Battery-Based, Fuel Cell-Based, Supercapacitor-Based, and Flywheel-Based Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT741 - HYDROGEN ENGINES AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Energy Storage in Hybrid and Electric Vehicles: Battery-Based, Fuel Cell-Based, Supercapacitor-Based, and Flywheel-Based Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Energy Storage Systems (ESS)** in Hybrid Electric Vehicles (HEVs) and Electric Vehicles (EVs) refer to the integrated electrochemical, electrical, mechanical, and chemical subsystems responsible for capturing, retaining, and delivering propulsive and auxiliary energy on demand. Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, ESS is classified into four primary categories:

1. **Battery-Based Energy Storage (BESS)** — electrochemical cells (Li-ion, NiMH, Lead-acid, Solid-state).
2. **Fuel Cell-Based Energy Storage (FCESS)** — hydrogen-fed Proton Exchange Membrane (PEMFC) or Solid Oxide Fuel Cells (SOFC) acting as range extenders.
3. **Supercapacitor-Based Energy Storage (SC-ESS)** — Electric Double-Layer Capacitors (EDLC) and hybrid supercapacitors.
4. **Flywheel-Based Energy Storage (FESS)** — kinetic energy reservoirs (mechanical KERS).

The defining performance metrics for any vehicular ESS are:

- **Specific Energy (Wh/kg)** — energy per unit mass (range indicator).
- **Energy Density (Wh/L)** — energy per unit volume (packaging indicator).
- **Specific Power (W/kg)** — power per unit mass (acceleration indicator).
- **Cycle Life (cycles)** — durability against repeated charge/discharge.
- **Round-Trip Efficiency (%)** — usable energy out ÷ energy in.
- **C-Rate** — discharge/charge current normalized to rated capacity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly connects hydrogen fuel cell vehicles with their energy storage counterparts. A fuel cell vehicle is essentially a *FCESS + Battery Hybrid*, where the FC provides continuous baseline power and the battery handles transient peaks (regenerative braking + acceleration bursts).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a water delivery system for a city:

- **Battery** is like a **large overhead water tank** — stores a lot of water (energy) slowly, releases it steadily over hours.
- **Supercapacitor** is like a **pressurized fire-hose nozzle** — cannot hold much water, but can release it in violent bursts within seconds.
- **Flywheel** is like a **spinning heavy disc on a shaft** — keeps spinning (kinetic energy) and can be tapped instantly for short, high-power delivery.
- **Fuel Cell (with hydrogen tank)** is like a **continuous water pump fed by an underground well** — it generates electricity as long as hydrogen is supplied; the tank is the "fuel reservoir," not the energy itself.

Just as a city may combine a tank (battery) + pump (fuel cell) + fire hose (supercapacitor) + flywheel (regulator), a modern HEV/EV combines these technologies, each chosen for a specific mission profile.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Reference Metrics (Bolded)

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Faraday constant | $F$ | **96,485 C/mol** |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| Standard hydrogen potential | $E^0$ | **1.229 V** at 25 °C |
| Standard gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Lower Heating Value of H₂ | LHV | **120 MJ/kg** |
| Higher Heating Value of H₂ | HHV | **142 MJ/kg** |
| Energy density of gasoline (benchmark) | — | **~ 8,800 Wh/kg** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Anchor:** Module 3 establishes the engineering rationale for choosing *which* storage system supports *which* load profile. This directly feeds into Module 4 (Powertrain configuration) and Module 5 (Energy Management Strategies).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Battery-Based Energy Storage (BESS)

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

A battery converts chemical energy into electrical energy via **faradaic redox reactions** at two electrodes separated by an electrolyte. During discharge:
- The **anode** is oxidized (loses electrons).
- The **cathode** is reduced (gains electrons).
- Electrons flow through the external circuit (the vehicle traction motor).

### 2.1.2 Key Battery Parameters

- **Open-Circuit Voltage (OCV):** Theoretical voltage when no current flows.
- **Nominal Voltage:** Operating voltage under load.
- **Capacity $Q$:** Measured in **Ampere-hours (Ah)**.
- **State of Charge (SOC):** Fractional remaining capacity, $0 \le \text{SOC} \le 1$.
- **Depth of Discharge (DOD):** $\text{DOD} = 1 - \text{SOC}$.
- **State of Health (SOH):** Ratio of current maximum capacity to rated capacity.

### 2.1.3 Step-by-Step Cell Energy Derivation

The energy delivered by a battery cell is given by integration of the voltage–charge product:

$$E_{\text{cell}} = \int_{0}^{Q} V_{\text{cell}}(q) \, dq$$

For a constant-voltage approximation (valid for Li-ion in mid-SOC range):

$$E_{\text{cell}} \approx V_{\text{nom}} \times Q \quad [\text{Wh}]$$

For an $n$-cell series pack with pack voltage $V_{\text{pack}} = n V_{\text{cell}}$:

$$E_{\text{pack}} = V_{\text{pack}} \times Q_{\text{pack}} \quad [\text{Wh}]$$

### 2.1.4 C-Rate and Peukert's Law

The **C-rate** expresses current relative to rated capacity. A 1C discharge of a 50 Ah battery means **50 A** is drawn, completing discharge in **1 hour**. Peukert's empirical law captures capacity loss at high rates:

$$Q_{\text{actual}} = Q_{\text{rated}} \left( \frac{I_{\text{rated}}}{I} \right)^{k - 1}$$

where $k$ is the Peukert exponent ($k \approx 1.05$–$1.2$ for Li-ion, $k \approx 1.2$–$1.4$ for lead-acid).

### 2.1.5 Equivalent Circuit Model

A practical Li-ion cell is modeled as:

$$V_{\text{term}} = V_{\text{OC}} - I R_{\text{int}} - V_{\text{pol}}$$

where $V_{\text{pol}}$ is the polarization overpotential (function of current density, temperature, and SOC).

---

## 2.2 Fuel Cell-Based Energy Storage (FCESS)

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle (PEMFC Context)

A **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)** is the primary fuel cell type used in KTU syllabus PEMUT741. The half-reactions are:

- **Anode (HOR):** $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$
- **Cathode (ORR):** $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$
- **Overall:** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$

The theoretical reversible potential at standard conditions is $E_{\text{rev}}^0 = 1.229$ V per cell.

### 2.2.2 Nernst Equation (Real-Cell Voltage)

$$E_{\text{rev}} = E_{\text{rev}}^0 + \frac{RT}{2F} \ln \left( \frac{p_{\text{H}_2} \, p_{\text{O}_2}^{1/2}}{p_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}} \right)$$

Under activation, concentration, and ohmic losses, the actual cell voltage becomes:

$$V_{\text{cell}} = E_{\text{rev}} - \eta_{\text{act}} - \eta_{\text{conc}} - IR_{\text{ohmic}}$$

### 2.2.3 Thermodynamic Efficiency

$$\eta_{\text{FC}} = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H} \times 100\%$$

- Using **HHV**: $\eta_{\text{HHV}} \approx 83\%$ at 25 °C.
- Using **LHV**: $\eta_{\text{LHV}} \approx 94\%$ at 25 °C.

Practical FC system efficiency (stack + BoP) is **40–60%**.

### 2.2.4 Hydrogen Tank as Energy Reservoir

The energy stored in a compressed hydrogen tank (typically **350 bar** or **700 bar**) is:

$$E_{\text{tank}} = m_{\text{H}_2} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2}$$

For a 5 kg H₂ tank at 700 bar:
$$E_{\text{tank}} = 5 \times 120 = 600 \text{ MJ} = 166.7 \text{ kWh}$$

---

## 2.3 Supercapacitor-Based Energy Storage (SC-ESS)

### 2.3.1 Operating Principle (EDLC)

A supercapacitor stores energy **electrostatically** at the electrode–electrolyte interface, forming an **Electric Double Layer (EDL)**. No faradaic reaction occurs (true EDLC), making charge/discharge highly reversible.

The capacitance of a double layer follows:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$$

where $d \approx$ a few **nanometers** (atomic-scale separation), yielding enormous capacitance per unit area.

### 2.3.2 Stored Energy

$$E_{\text{SC}} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$

Maximum deliverable energy between $V_{\text{max}}$ and $V_{\text{min}}$:

$$E_{\text{del}} = \frac{1}{2} C (V_{\text{max}}^2 - V_{\text{min}}^2)$$

### 2.3.3 Equivalent Series Resistance (ESR)

Supercapacitors have very low ESR ($< 1$ mΩ per cell), enabling peak power:

$$P_{\text{peak}} = \frac{V^2}{4 \cdot \text{ESR}}$$

---

## 2.4 Flywheel-Based Energy Storage (FESS)

### 2.4.1 Operating Principle

A flywheel stores energy in **rotational kinetic form**. Energy is input by a motor–generator (M/G) accelerating the rotor, and recovered by the same M/G acting as a generator during deceleration.

### 2.4.2 Stored Kinetic Energy

For a rotor with moment of inertia $I$ rotating at angular velocity $\omega$:

$$E_{\text{kin}} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

For a solid cylindrical rotor of mass $m$ and radius $r$:

$$I = \frac{1}{2} m r^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{\text{kin}} = \frac{1}{4} m r^2 \omega^2$$

For a thin-walled hollow cylinder (preferred for higher $E/m$):

$$I = m r^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{\text{kin}} = \frac{1}{2} m r^2 \omega^2$$

### 2.4.3 Specific Energy (Tensile Stress Limit)

Modern composite flywheels operate at the rim-stress limit $\sigma_{\max}$:

$$\frac{E}{m} = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\rho}$$

For carbon-fiber composites, $\sigma_{\max} \approx 4$ GPa, $\rho \approx 1600$ kg/m³, giving theoretical specific energy up to **2.5 MJ/kg ≈ 694 Wh/kg**.

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL MARKDOWN RULE:** Inside table cells, absolute-value bars and conditional bars are written using `\vert` or `\mid` (LaTeX), NOT the vertical pipe `\vert`. This preserves table syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Battery cell energy | $E = V_{\text{nom}} \cdot Q$ | Wh | Range estimation |
| 2 | Pack energy | $E_{\text{pack}} = n_s V_{\text{cell}} \cdot n_p Q_{\text{cell}}$ | Wh | EV pack sizing |
| 3 | Specific energy | $\frac{E}{m}$ | Wh/kg | Cell technology comparison |
| 4 | Energy density | $\frac{E}{V_{\text{vol}}}$ | Wh/L | Packaging |
| 5 | C-rate definition | $\text{C} = \frac{I}{Q_{\text{rated}}}$ | h$^{-1}$ | Charge/discharge speed |
| 6 | Peukert capacity | $Q_{\text{act}} = Q_{\text{rated}} (I_{\text{rated}} / I)^{k-1}$ | Ah | High-rate derating |
| 7 | Nernst (PEMFC) | $E = E^0 + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln Q_r$ | V | FC reversible voltage |
| 8 | FC efficiency | $\eta = \frac{\Delta G}{\Delta H}$ | — | Thermodynamic limit |
| 9 | H₂ tank energy | $E = m_{\text{H}_2} \cdot \text{LHV}$ | J | Range from H₂ |
| 10 | Capacitor energy | $E = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | J | Supercapacitor sizing |
| 11 | Capacitance (EDL) | $C = \frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 A}{d}$ | F | EDLC physics |
| 12 | Flywheel KE | $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | J | FESS rating |
| 13 | Solid cyl. inertia | $I = \frac{1}{2} m r^2$ | kg·m² | FESS geometry |
| 14 | Hollow cyl. inertia | $I = m r^2$ | kg·m² | Optimized rotor |
| 15 | Specific KE limit | $\frac{E}{m} = \frac{\sigma_{\max}}{\rho}$ | J/kg | Rim-stress design |
| 16 | FC reversible EMF | $E^0 = 1.229$ V (25 °C) | V | Nernst base |
| 17 | Peak power (SC) | $P_{\text{peak}} = V^2 / (4 \cdot \text{ESR})$ | W | Burst capability |
| 18 | Round-trip eff. | $\eta_{\text{RT}} = \frac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}}$ | — | System-level loss |
| 19 | DOD | $\text{DOD} = 1 - \text{SOC}$ | — | Cycle-life derating |
| 20 | Cell mass for range | $m_{\text{cell}} = \frac{R \cdot E_{\text{req}}}{\eta_{\text{chain}} \cdot \text{SE}_{\text{cell}}}$ | kg | Vehicle design |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Utility of These Systems

| Storage System | Real Application in HEV/EV | Engineering Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **Li-ion Battery** | Tesla Model 3, Tata Nexon EV, Hyundai Kona EV | High specific energy → long range |
| **PEMFC + H₂ tank** | Toyota Mirai, Hyundai Nexo | Refuels in 5 min, zero local emissions |
| **Supercapacitor** | Mazda i-ELOOP, Formula 1 KERS (hybrid with battery) | Captures regenerative braking peaks |
| **Flywheel (FESS)** | Williams F1 KERS, Gyrobus (Switzerland) | Burst power delivery, mechanical simplicity |
| **Battery + SC Hybrid** | Toyota Prius (NiMH + SC), Honda Insight | Battery for energy, SC for transient power |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Requirement for a Target Vehicle Range

**Problem Statement:** Compute the usable battery pack mass for an EV that must travel **300 km** at an average energy consumption of **150 Wh/km**, assuming Li-ion cells with specific energy **250 Wh/kg** and drivetrain efficiency **88%**.

### Step 1 — Compute total propulsive energy required at the wheels

$$E_{\text{wheels}} = \text{Range} \times e_{\text{cons}} = 300 \times 150$$

$$E_{\text{wheels}} = 45{,}000 \text{ Wh}$$

*Explanation:* Multiply the target distance by the average energy consumed per kilometre. This gives the gross mechanical energy that must reach the tyres.

### Step 2 — Compute energy that must leave the battery

$$E_{\text{batt,out}} = \frac{E_{\text{wheels}}}{\eta_{\text{drivetrain}}}$$

$$E_{\text{batt,out}} = \frac{45{,}000}{0.88}$$

$$E_{\text{batt,out}} = 51{,}136.36 \text{ Wh}$$

*Explanation:* The drivetrain (motor + inverter + transmission) loses 12% of the energy. To deliver 45,000 Wh to the wheels, the battery must supply slightly more.

### Step 3 — Account for round-trip efficiency (charge + discharge losses)

Assume battery round-trip efficiency $\eta_{\text{RT}} = 0.92$:

$$E_{\text{batt,stored}} = \frac{E_{\text{batt,out}}}{\eta_{\text{RT}}}$$

$$E_{\text{batt,stored}} = \frac{51{,}136.36}{0.92}$$

$$E_{\text{batt,stored}} = 55{,}582.0 \text{ Wh}$$

*Explanation:* The battery itself dissipates heat during charge and discharge. We must store more energy than we ultimately consume.

### Step 4 — Compute battery mass using specific energy

$$m_{\text{cell}} = \frac{E_{\text{batt,stored}}}{\text{SE}_{\text{cell}}}$$

$$m_{\text{cell}} = \frac{55{,}582.0}{250}$$

$$m_{\text{cell}} = 222.33 \text{ kg}$$

*Explanation:* Dividing required stored energy by cell-level specific energy yields the minimum cell mass. Real packs are 30–50% heavier once enclosures, BMS, and cooling are added.

### Step 5 — Add pack-level overhead

With pack overhead factor $\phi = 1.35$ (typical):

$$m_{\text{pack}} = \phi \cdot m_{\text{cell}} = 1.35 \times 222.33$$

$$m_{\text{pack}} \approx 300.15 \text{ kg}$$

### Result Summary

| Quantity | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| Energy at wheels | $E_{\text{wheels}}$ | 45,000 Wh |
| Energy out of battery | $E_{\text{batt,out}}$ | 51,136 Wh |
| Energy stored (charged) | $E_{\text{batt,stored}}$ | 55,582 Wh |
| Cell mass | $m_{\text{cell}}$ | 222.33 kg |
| Pack mass (with overhead) | $m_{\text{pack}}$ | ≈ 300 kg |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Supercapacitor Pack Sizing for Regenerative Braking

**Problem Statement:** A 1,500 kg EV brakes from **60 km/h** to rest. Available regenerative energy (30% of kinetic energy recoverable at brake) must be absorbed by a supercapacitor pack. Compute the required pack capacitance, assuming each cell is **2.7 V / 3000 F**, with operating window **1.35 V to 2.7 V**.

### Step 1 — Compute kinetic energy at start

$$v = 60 \text{ km/h} = 16.667 \text{ m/s}$$

$$E_{\text{kin}} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

$$E_{\text{kin}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (16.667)^2$$

$$E_{\text{kin}} = 0.5 \times 1500 \times 277.79$$

$$E_{\text{kin}} = 208{,}342 \text{ J}$$

### Step 2 — Compute recoverable braking energy

$$E_{\text{reg}} = \alpha \cdot E_{\text{kin}}$$

$$E_{\text{reg}} = 0.30 \times 208{,}342$$

$$E_{\text{reg}} = 62{,}502.6 \text{ J}$$

### Step 3 — Compute capacitance needed for usable energy window

Supercapacitor usable energy between $V_{\max}$ and $V_{\min}$:

$$E_{\text{reg}} = \frac{1}{2} C_{\text{pack}} (V_{\max}^2 - V_{\min}^2)$$

Solving for $C_{\text{pack}}$:

$$C_{\text{pack}} = \frac{2 E_{\text{reg}}}{V_{\max}^2 - V_{\min}^2}$$

$$C_{\text{pack}} = \frac{2 \times 62{,}502.6}{(2.7)^2 - (1.35)^2}$$

$$C_{\text{pack}} = \frac{125{,}005.2}{7.29 - 1.8225}$$

$$C_{\text{pack}} = \frac{125{,}005.2}{5.4675}$$

$$C_{\text{pack}} = 22{,}864 \text{ F}$$

### Step 4 — Number of series cells

Required pack voltage (matching DC bus, typically 48 V or higher). For a 48 V bus:
$$n_s = \frac{V_{\text{pack}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{48}{2.7} \approx 18 \text{ cells (in series)}$$

Series capacitance:
$$C_{\text{series}} = \frac{C_{\text{cell}}}{n_s} = \frac{3000}{18} = 166.67 \text{ F}$$

To reach 22,864 F equivalent, parallel strings:
$$n_p = \frac{C_{\text{pack}}}{C_{\text{series}}} = \frac{22{,}864}{166.67} \approx 138 \text{ parallel strings}$$

Total cells = $n_s \times n_p = 18 \times 138 = 2{,}484$ cells.

### Verification

$$E_{\text{usable}} = \frac{1}{2} \times 22{,}864 \times (2.7^2 - 1.35^2) = \frac{1}{2} \times 22{,}864 \times 5.4675 = 62{,}502 \text{ J} \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Flywheel Stored Energy and Burst Power

**Problem Statement:** A composite flywheel rotor of mass **m = 12 kg**, outer radius **r = 0.15 m**, operates at **maximum rim speed 1,000 m/s**. Compute stored kinetic energy, peak power if discharged over **10 seconds**, and specific energy.

### Step 1 — Angular velocity from rim speed

$$\omega = \frac{v_{\text{rim}}}{r} = \frac{1000}{0.15}$$

$$\omega = 6{,}666.67 \text{ rad/s}$$

### Step 2 — Moment of inertia (hollow thin-walled rotor approximation)

$$I = m r^2 = 12 \times (0.15)^2$$

$$I = 12 \times 0.0225 = 0.27 \text{ kg·m}^2$$

### Step 3 — Kinetic energy

$$E_{\text{kin}} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$$

$$E_{\text{kin}} = 0.5 \times 0.27 \times (6{,}666.67)^2$$

$$E_{\text{kin}} = 0.135 \times 4.444 \times 10^7$$

$$E_{\text{kin}} = 6.0 \times 10^6 \text{ J}$$

$$E_{\text{kin}} = 6.0 \text{ MJ} = 1.667 \text{ kWh}$$

### Step 4 — Specific energy

$$\text{SE}_{\text{FW}} = \frac{E_{\text{kin}}}{m} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^6}{12}$$

$$\text{SE}_{\text{FW}} = 500{,}000 \text{ J/kg} = 138.9 \text{ Wh/kg}$$

### Step 5 — Average burst power over 10 s

$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{E_{\text{kin}}}{\Delta t} = \frac{6.0 \times 10^6}{10} = 600 \text{ kW}$$

### Result Summary

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Angular velocity $\omega$ | 6,666.67 rad/s |
| Moment of inertia $I$ | 0.27 kg·m² |
| Stored energy $E_{\text{kin}}$ | 6.0 MJ (1.67 kWh) |
| Specific energy | 138.9 Wh/kg |
| 10-s burst power | 600 kW |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Fuel Cell Stack Sizing for a Mid-Size FCEV

**Problem Statement:** Size a PEMFC stack for a vehicle requiring **60 kW** continuous propulsion power at an average operating cell voltage of **0.65 V** and current density **1.0 A/cm²**. Cell active area: **200 cm²**.

### Step 1 — Voltage per cell at operating point

Given: $V_{\text{cell}} = 0.65 \text{ V}$

### Step 2 — Required number of series cells for system voltage (assume 300 V DC bus)

$$n_s = \frac{V_{\text{bus}}}{V_{\text{cell}}} = \frac{300}{0.65} \approx 462 \text{ cells}$$

### Step 3 — Current per cell from current density

$$I_{\text{cell}} = j \cdot A = 1.0 \times 200 = 200 \text{ A}$$

### Step 4 — Power per cell

$$P_{\text{cell}} = V_{\text{cell}} \cdot I_{\text{cell}} = 0.65 \times 200 = 130 \text{ W}$$

### Step 5 — Total stack power

$$P_{\text{stack}} = n_s \cdot P_{\text{cell}} = 462 \times 130$$

$$P_{\text{stack}} = 60{,}060 \text{ W} \approx 60 \text{ kW} \checkmark$$

### Step 6 — Hydrogen consumption rate

Using LHV of hydrogen (120 MJ/kg) and stack efficiency at 0.65 V:

$$\eta_{\text{stack}} = \frac{V_{\text{cell}}}{E^0} = \frac{0.65}{1.229} \approx 0.529 \;(52.9\%)$$

Hydrogen mass flow:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{P_{\text{stack}}}{\eta_{\text{stack}} \cdot \text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2}}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{60{,}000}{0.529 \times 120 \times 10^6}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = \frac{60{,}000}{6.348 \times 10^7}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{H}_2} = 9.45 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} = 0.945 \text{ g/s}$$

For a 5-hour drive, total H₂ needed:

$$m_{\text{H}_2,\text{total}} = 0.945 \times 5 \times 3600 = 17{,}010 \text{ g} = 17.01 \text{ kg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows why FCEVs typically use **~5–6 kg tanks** — continuous 60 kW draw quickly depletes hydrogen. Practical FCEVs use a **battery buffer** for peaks, dramatically reducing H₂ consumption.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Ragone Plot-Style Tabulation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pipe-Symbol Markdown Rule:** Inside tables, conditional/absolute-value bars use `\vert` or `\mid` (LaTeX), never the raw `\vert` character.

| Storage Type | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Specific Power (W/kg) | Cycle Life | RT Eff. (%) | Self-Discharge |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lead-Acid | 30–50 | 75–300 | 500–800 | 70–80 | 4–6%/month |
| NiMH | 60–120 | 200–1,000 | 1,000–2,000 | 65–75 | 20%/month |
| Li-ion (NMC) | 150–250 | 250–1,000 | 1,500–3,000 | 85–95 | 2–3%/month |
| Li-ion (LFP) | 90–160 | 200–800 | 3,000–6,000 | 88–92 | 2–3%/month |
| Solid-State Li | 350–500 | 300–1,000 | 5,000+ | 90–95 | <1%/month |
| EDLC Supercapacitor | 4–10 | 5,000–15,000 | 500,000–1,000,000 | 92–98 | High (leakage) |
| Hybrid SC (Li-ion cap) | 20–60 | 1,000–5,000 | 20,000+ | 90–95 | Low |
| Flywheel (composite) | 50–200 | 1,000–10,000 | 100,000+ | 85–95 | Friction loss |
| H₂ Tank + PEMFC | 800–1,200 (system) | 300–600 | N/A (consumable) | 40–60 | Negligible |
| Gasoline (benchmark) | ~12,000 | — | — | 20–30 | — |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — EV/HEV Energy Storage System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Energy Source] --> B{Storage Type Selector}
    B --> C[Battery Pack BESS]
    B --> D[Fuel Cell Stack FCESS]
    B --> E[Supercapacitor Pack SCESS]
    B --> F[Flywheel FESS]
    C --> G[DC DC Converter]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> H[AC DC Converter]
    H --> G
    G --> I[DC Bus]
    I --> J[Traction Inverter]
    J --> K[Electric Traction Motor]
    K --> L[Wheels]
    I --> M[Auxiliary Loads HVAC Lights]
    I --> N[BMS / EMS Controller]
    N -. Feedback .- C
    N -. Feedback .- D
    N -. Feedback .- E
```

> **Figure 4.1 — Functional block architecture showing all four ESS topologies feeding a common DC bus.** Each ESS is selected by mission profile (range vs. peak power vs. refuel time) and orchestrated by an Energy Management System (EMS).

---

## 4.2 Battery Cell Internal Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Anode LiC6] --> B[Electrolyte LiPF6 in EC DMC]
    B --> C[Cathode LiMO2]
    A -. Discharge Li plus e .-> D[External Circuit Load]
    D -. e minus current .-> C
    C --> E[Separator Microporous PP PE]
```

> **Figure 4.2 — Lithium-ion cell internal structure.** During discharge, Li⁺ ions migrate anode → cathode through the electrolyte; electrons flow externally.

---

## 4.3 PEM Fuel Cell Stack Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hydrogen Tank 700 bar] --> B[Pressure Regulator]
    B --> C[Anode Inlet Manifold]
    D[Air Compressor] --> E[Humidifier]
    E --> F[Cathode Inlet Manifold]
    C --> G[FC Stack PEMFC]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Water Vapor Exhaust]
    G --> I[Coolant Loop Radiator]
    I -. heat removal .- G
    G --> J[DC DC Boost Converter]
    J --> K[DC Bus High Voltage]
```

> **Figure 4.3 — PEMFC system BoP (Balance of Plant) flow.** H₂ and air are humidified and fed to the stack; product water and heat are managed by auxiliary subsystems.

---

## 4.4 Supercapacitor Ragone-Plot Position Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[High Energy Low Power] --> B[Battery Region]
    B --> C[Fuel Cell Region]
    C --> D[Flywheel Mid Zone]
    D --> E[Supercapacitor Region]
    E --> F[High Power Low Energy]
```

> **Figure 4.4 — Conceptual Ragone plot** showing where each ESS occupies the energy-vs-power space. Batteries dominate the energy axis; supercapacitors dominate the power axis; flywheels bridge the mid-zone.

---

## 4.5 Hybrid ESS Topology (Battery + Supercapacitor)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Brake Pedal Released] --> B[Regenerative Energy Capture]
    B --> C[Supercapacitor Pack Fast Absorption]
    B --> D[Battery Pack Slow Absorption]
    C --> E[DC DC Equalizer]
    D --> E
    E --> F[DC Bus]
    F --> G[Traction Motor]
    G --> H[Wheels]
    H --> I[Accelerator Demand]
    I --> C
    I --> D
```

> **Figure 4.5 — Passive/active hybrid ESS topology.** Supercapacitors absorb high-frequency regenerative pulses; batteries absorb sustained loads. This prolongs battery cycle life.

---

## 4.6 Flywheel KERS Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Braking Energy] --> B[MG Set Motor Generator]
    B --> C[Clutch Engagement]
    C --> D[Flywheel Rotor Acceleration]
    D --> E[High Speed Spinning Vacuum Housing]
    E -. kinetic energy stored .- D
    D --> F[Deceleration Need]
    F --> B
    B --> G[Traction Power Boost]
```

> **Figure 4.6 — Mechanical KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery System) flow.** During braking, the M/G acts as a motor spinning up the flywheel; during acceleration, it acts as a generator feeding traction power.

---

## 4.7 Comparison Flow — Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Mission Profile] --> B{Range Requirement}
    B -->|Over 400 km| C[Fuel Cell + H2 Tank]
    B -->|Under 400 km| D{Daily Commute}
    D -->|Yes| E[Li-ion Battery Pack]
    D -->|No Aggressive Drive| F[Hybrid Battery + SuperCap]
    F --> G[Add Flywheel for Burst Boost]
    G --> H[Williams F1 KERS Style]
```

> **Figure 4.7 — Decision tree for ESS selection.** Mission profile dictates architecture: long-range → FCESS; urban → BESS; performance → Hybrid (BESS + SC + FESS).

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
Define *specific energy* and *energy density* of an EV energy storage system. Why is specific energy more critical for range than energy density?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Specific energy** is the energy stored per unit mass of the storage system, expressed in **Wh/kg** (1 mark).
- **Energy density** is the energy stored per unit volume, expressed in **Wh/L** (1 mark).
- Specific energy is more critical for range because vehicle range is inversely proportional to the *mass* that must be hauled; a heavier battery reduces net propulsion energy via increased rolling resistance and climbing load. Energy density affects packaging only (1 mark).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
What is **State of Charge (SOC)**? Why is SOC estimation critical for Li-ion battery management?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **SOC** is the ratio of remaining capacity to rated (maximum) capacity of a cell, expressed as a percentage between 0% and 100% (1.5 marks).
- Accurate SOC estimation is critical because:
  - Prevents **over-charge** (electrolyte decomposition, plating) and **over-discharge** (irreversible copper dissolution, capacity fade) (1 mark).
  - Provides user with reliable range prediction and enables efficient energy management strategy (0.5 mark).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module internal choice — students answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Each sub-part is 7 marks.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 MARKS)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3 Choice A]**

**(a)** Compare the four major energy storage systems used in modern hybrid and electric vehicles (battery, fuel cell, supercapacitor, flywheel) with respect to **specific energy, specific power, cycle life, and round-trip efficiency**. Use a comparison table. (7 marks)

**(b)** A fuel cell electric vehicle carries **4.5 kg of compressed hydrogen** at 700 bar. The PEMFC stack operates at an average cell voltage of **0.65 V**, with a system efficiency of **50%** (LHV basis). The vehicle consumes an average of **10 kWh per 100 km** at the wheels.
  - (i) Compute the total chemical energy stored in the hydrogen tank. (2 marks)
  - (ii) Compute the usable electrical energy available at the FC output. (2 marks)
  - (iii) Estimate the vehicle range in km, assuming drivetrain efficiency is **85%**. (3 marks)

#### ⭐ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION A

**Part (a) — Comparison Table (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Li-ion Battery | PEMFC + H₂ Tank | Supercapacitor | Flywheel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | 150–250 | 800–1,200 (system) | 4–10 | 50–200 |
| Specific Power (W/kg) | 250–1,000 | 300–600 | 5,000–15,000 | 1,000–10,000 |
| Cycle Life | 1,500–3,000 | N/A (consumable) | 500,000+ | 100,000+ |
| Round-Trip Efficiency | 85–95% | 40–60% | 92–98% | 85–95% |
| Charge Time | 30 min – 8 h | 3–5 min (refuel) | Seconds | Seconds |
| Best For | Range | Long range, fast refuel | Peak power | Burst boost |

> Examiner expects: Tabulated comparison (3 marks), explanation of *why* each excels (2 marks), clear identification of application (2 marks).

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Chemical energy stored in hydrogen**

Use **LHV of hydrogen = 120 MJ/kg = 33.33 kWh/kg** (1 mark).

$$E_{\text{chem}} = m_{\text{H}_2} \times \text{LHV}_{\text{H}_2}$$

$$E_{\text{chem}} = 4.5 \times 33.33 = 150.0 \text{ kWh}$$ (1 mark)

**(ii) Usable electrical energy at FC output**

$$\eta_{\text{stack}} = 0.50$$

$$E_{\text{FC,elec}} = \eta_{\text{stack}} \times E_{\text{chem}}$$

$$E_{\text{FC,elec}} = 0.50 \times 150.0 = 75.0 \text{ kWh}$$ (2 marks)

**(iii) Vehicle range**

Energy at wheels after drivetrain losses:

$$E_{\text{wheels}} = \eta_{\text{drivetrain}} \times E_{\text{FC,elec}}$$

$$E_{\text{wheels}} = 0.85 \times 75.0 = 63.75 \text{ kWh}$$

Range:

$$\text{Range} = \frac{E_{\text{wheels}}}{e_{\text{cons}}} = \frac{63.75}{10/100} = \frac{63.75}{0.1} = 637.5 \text{ km}$$ (3 marks)

**Final Answer Range ≈ 637.5 km**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 MARKS) — INTERNAL CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3 Choice B]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labelled schematic, explain the working principle of a **Proton Exchange Membrane Fuel Cell (PEMFC)**. State the half-cell reactions and the standard reversible EMF. (7 marks)

**(b)** A supercapacitor pack of capacitance **100 F** is charged to **48 V** in an EV. The pack is then used to provide auxiliary power for the vehicle cabin.
  - (i) Compute the maximum energy stored in the pack. (2 marks)
  - (ii) If the pack discharges to **24 V**, compute the usable energy delivered. (3 marks)
  - (iii) If the pack's equivalent series resistance (ESR) is **0.05 Ω**, compute the peak instantaneous power it can deliver when fully charged. (2 marks)

#### ⭐ MODEL SOLUTION FOR QUESTION B

**Part (a) — PEMFC Operation (7 marks)**

**Working Principle:**

A PEMFC generates electricity by electrochemically combining hydrogen and oxygen across a proton-conducting polymer membrane (e.g., Nafion). Hydrogen is fed to the **anode**, where a platinum catalyst splits it into protons and electrons. Protons migrate through the membrane to the cathode; electrons are forced through the external circuit (the vehicle's traction motor), producing useful work (2 marks).

**Half-Cell Reactions:**

- **Anode (HOR):** $\text{H}_2 \rightarrow 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^-$ (1 mark)
- **Cathode (ORR):** $\tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}^+ + 2e^- \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (1 mark)
- **Overall:** $\text{H}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{O}$ + electrical energy + heat (1 mark)

**Standard Reversible EMF:**

$$E^0_{\text{rev}} = 1.229 \text{ V at } 25°\text{C} \; (1 \text{ mark})$$

Theoretical efficiency using HHV: ≈ 83%. Using LHV: ≈ 94%. Practical stack efficiency (BoP-inclusive): 40–60% (1 mark).

**Labelled Schematic** (textual reference for examiner):

```
    H₂ in → [Anode | Pt Catalyst | PEM Membrane | Pt Catalyst | Cathode] ← O₂ in
                  ↑              ↑                ↑              ↑
                  H⁺ migrates through membrane; e⁻ flow externally
                                  ↓
                          External load (traction motor)
                                  ↓
                          H₂O + heat out
```

**Part (b) — Supercapacitor Numerical (7 marks)**

**(i) Maximum energy stored**

$$E_{\max} = \frac{1}{2} C V_{\max}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times (48)^2$$

$$E_{\max} = 50 \times 2304 = 115{,}200 \text{ J} = 32.0 \text{ Wh}$$ (2 marks)

**(ii) Usable energy delivered down to 24 V**

$$E_{\text{used}} = \frac{1}{2} C (V_{\max}^2 - V_{\min}^2) = \frac{1}{2} \times 100 \times (48^2 - 24^2)$$

$$E_{\text{used}} = 50 \times (2304 - 576)$$

$$E_{\text{used}} = 50 \times 1728$$

$$E_{\text{used}} = 86{,}400 \text{ J} = 24.0 \text{ Wh}$$ (3 marks)

**(iii) Peak instantaneous power**

$$P_{\text{peak}} = \frac{V^2}{4 \cdot \text{ESR}} = \frac{(48)^2}{4 \times 0.05}$$

$$P_{\text{peak}} = \frac{2304}{0.20}$$

$$P_{\text{peak}} = 11{,}520 \text{ W} \approx 11.52 \text{ kW}$$ (2 marks)

**Final Answer Summary:**
- Max energy: 32.0 Wh
- Usable energy to 24 V: 24.0 Wh
- Peak power: 11.52 kW

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 COMMON MARKS-LOSS PITFALLS — READ BEFORE WRITING YOUR EXAM**
>
> 1. **LHV vs HHV confusion (lose 1–2 marks):** Always state explicitly whether you used LHV (120 MJ/kg) or HHV (142 MJ/kg) for hydrogen. KTU board examiners deduct marks if the basis is unclear.
>
> 2. **Mixing units:** Many students compute FC stack energy in **MJ** but compare with battery energy in **kWh**. Always convert to a common unit. $1 \text{ kWh} = 3.6 \text{ MJ}$.
>
> 3. **Forgetting drivetrain efficiency:** Don't jump from $E_{\text{battery}}$ directly to range. KTU expects a 3-step chain: $E_{\text{stored}} \rightarrow E_{\text{out}} \rightarrow E_{\text{wheels}} \rightarrow \text{Range}$.
>
> 4. **Supercapacitor energy formula trap:** Many students write $E = CV^2$ instead of $E = \frac{1}{2} CV^2$. This **doubles** your answer — automatic 1-mark cut.
>
> 5. **Fuel cell efficiency confusion:** $\eta = \Delta G / \Delta H$ is **thermodynamic** (upper limit). Practical efficiency is $V_{\text{cell}} / E^0$. Students often quote both incorrectly.
>
> 6. **Flywheel inertia formula:** Wrong geometry → wrong answer. Solid cylinder: $I = \tfrac{1}{2} m r^2$. Hollow cylinder: $I = m r^2$. Read the problem carefully.
>
> 7. **PEMFC sign convention:** The anode reaction is **oxidation** (HOR) in fuel cell mode (current flows out of anode). Students often reverse this.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 HIGH-DENSITY REVISION SUMMARY — Module 3, Energy Storage in HEVs/EVs**

### Core Concepts to Remember
- ☐ **Four storage families:** Battery (BESS), Fuel Cell (FCESS), Supercapacitor (SC-ESS), Flywheel (FESS).
- ☐ **Ragone plot positioning:** Battery = high energy / low power; Supercapacitor = low energy / high power; Flywheel = mid-energy / mid-power; FCESS = highest system-level energy density but limited power density.
- ☐ **Selection rule:** Choose BESS for range, SC-ESS for peak power, FCESS for fast refuel + long range, FESS for burst boost.

### Key Numerical Benchmarks (Memorize These)
- ☐ Faraday constant $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol
- ☐ PEMFC standard EMF $E^0 = 1.229$ V at 25 °C
- ☐ LHV(H₂) = 120 MJ/kg = 33.33 kWh/kg
- ☐ HHV(H₂) = 142 MJ/kg = 39.44 kWh/kg
- ☐ Battery RT efficiency ≈ 85–95%
- ☐ Supercapacitor energy $E = \tfrac{1}{2} CV^2$
- ☐ Flywheel KE $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I\omega^2$; thin hollow cylinder $I = mr^2$
- ☐ Li-ion specific energy range: 150–250 Wh/kg
- ☐ Solid-state Li target: 350–500 Wh/kg

### Formula Quick-Reference
- ☐ $E_{\text{cell}} = V_{\text{nom}} \cdot Q$ [Wh]
- ☐ $E_{\text{SC}} = \tfrac{1}{2} C (V_{\max}^2 - V_{\min}^2)$ [J]
- ☐ $E_{\text{FW}} = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ [J]
- ☐ $E_{\text{tank}} = m_{\text{H}_2} \cdot \text{LHV}$ [J]
- ☐ $\eta_{\text{FC,thermo}} = \Delta G / \Delta H$
- ☐ $\eta_{\text{FC,practical}} = V_{\text{cell}} / E^0$
- ☐ Peukert: $Q_{\text{act}} = Q_{\text{rated}} (I_{\text{rated}}/I)^{k-1}$

### Likely KTU Exam Topics (Weight-Ordered)
- ☐ **High Weight:** Numerical on battery sizing, FC stack sizing, H₂ tank range, supercapacitor energy.
- ☐ **Medium Weight:** Comparison tables, Ragone plot interpretation, working principles.

### One-Line Memory Hooks
- ☐ **"BESS = Range, SCESS = Power, FCESS = Refuel+Range, FESS = Burst"**
- ☐ **"Supercap fires fast, battery runs long, flywheel spins hard, fuel cell pumps steady"**
- ☐ **"Faraday 96,485; LHV 120 MJ/kg; $E^0$ 1.229 V — three numbers save three marks"**

topics.title: Hybridization of Energy Storage Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT741 - HYDROGEN ENGINES AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Hybridization of Energy Storage Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Hybridization of Energy Storage Devices

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> **Hybridization of Energy Storage Devices (HESD)** refers to the systematic integration of **two or more complementary energy storage technologies** — typically a **high-energy-density source** (e.g., fuel cell, battery) and a **high-power-density source** (e.g., ultracapacitor, flywheel) — into a single powertrain such that the combined system exhibits a **wider operating envelope** than any single device could provide alone. The objective is to simultaneously satisfy the **sustained energy demand** and the **transient peak power demand** of an electric or fuel-cell vehicle (FCV) while optimizing **efficiency, life, weight, and cost**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, hybridization is treated as a **multi-source energy management problem**, where the Fuel Cell (acting as the primary energy converter from hydrogen) is paired with a **secondary buffer storage** (battery, ultracapacitor, or both) to form a **Hybrid Electric Energy Storage System (HEESS)**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sprinter–Marathoner" Model

Imagine a relay race team:
- The **marathon runner** = Fuel Cell or Battery (slow, steady, long-lasting energy supply).
- The **sprinter** = Ultracapacitor (instantaneous, powerful, but tires out quickly).
- The **baton (hand-off)** = the **DC-DC converter / Energy Management Controller (EMC)**.

Neither the marathoner nor the sprinter alone can win a mixed sprint-endurance race. But together, with smart coordination, they dominate. **Hybridization is exactly this — handing off power between a steady source and a burst source through an intelligent controller.**

### 1.3 Why We Cannot Use Only One Storage Device?

Every storage device sits at a specific point on the **Ragone plane** (Energy density vs. Power density). Vehicles demand *both* axes simultaneously:
- Cruise → requires **Wh/kg** (energy).
- Acceleration / hill-climb → requires **W/kg** (power).
- Braking regeneration → requires **fast charge acceptance**.

A battery alone overheats during peaks; an ultracapacitor alone empties in minutes. **Hybridization bridges this gap.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The hybridization study focuses on the **topology**, **energy management strategy (EMS)**, **sizing**, and **power-flow dynamics** of combined **Fuel Cell + Battery + Ultracapacitor** systems for automotive propulsion.

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Always Bolded in Answers)

- **Energy Density (Wh/kg):** Total stored energy per unit mass.
- **Power Density (W/kg):** Instantaneous deliverable power per unit mass.
- **Cycle Life (cycles):** Number of full charge–discharge cycles before capacity drops to 80%.
- **State of Charge (SOC, %):** Available capacity relative to full capacity.
- **State of Health (SOH, %):** Maximum current deliverable relative to rated maximum.
- **Round-Trip Efficiency (η, %):** Energy out ÷ Energy in × 100.
- **C-Rate:** Charge/discharge current normalized to rated capacity.
- **Specific Energy of Hydrogen (Lower Heating Value) = 120 MJ/kg ≈ 33.33 kWh/kg.**
- **Faraday's Constant F = 96,485 C/mol.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Canonical Hybrid Topologies

| Topology | Configuration | Prime Source | Buffer | Typical Use |
|----------|---------------|--------------|--------|-------------|
| **FC + Battery** | Passive or semi-active | Fuel Cell | Li-ion / NiMH | Mid-size FCV (e.g., Toyota Mirai gen-1) |
| **FC + Ultracapacitor** | Active | Fuel Cell | EDLC | High transient load, transit buses |
| **FC + Battery + UC (Triple HESS)** | Fully active | Fuel Cell | Battery + UC | Heavy-duty, long-range, fast acceleration |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Convention:** A system with **one reversible storage + one fuel-based converter** is called a *Hybrid Energy Storage System (HESS)*. A system with **two reversible storages + one fuel converter** is called a *Triple-Source HESS* or *Full HESS*.

## 2.2 Role of Each Source in the Hybrid Stack

### A. Fuel Cell (Primary Energy Converter)
- Converts H₂ chemical energy → electrical energy.
- **Slow dynamic response** (time constant ~ 1–5 s for PEMFC).
- Cannot absorb regenerative braking energy (no recharge ability).
- Acts as the *base-load supplier* during steady cruising.

### B. Battery (Mid-Range Energy + Modest Power Buffer)
- Stores electrical energy electrochemically.
- Handles moderate transients and absorbs regen energy.
- Acts as the *load-leveler* between FC and traction demand.

### C. Ultracapacitor (Peak Power Buffer)
- Stores energy electrostatically (no chemical reaction).
- **Very high power density (5–10 kW/kg)**, low energy density (5–10 Wh/kg).
- Responds in **milliseconds**.
- Ideal for **acceleration peaks and regenerative capture**.

## 2.3 Energy Management Strategies (EMS)

1. **Rule-Based (Thermostatic / State-Machine):**
   - If SOC_bat < threshold → FC charges battery.
   - If SOC_bat > threshold → FC reduces output.
   - Simple, robust, no optimization.

2. **Power-Following Strategy:**
   - FC delivers only the *average* load power.
   - Battery + UC handle all transients.
   - Protects FC from dynamic stress.

3. **Frequency-Decoupling / Wavelet Strategy:**
   - Low-frequency demand → FC + Battery.
   - High-frequency demand → UC only.
   - Implemented via low-pass / high-pass filters.

4. **Optimization-Based (Equivalent Consumption Minimization Strategy — ECMS):**
   - Minimizes instantaneous equivalent hydrogen consumption.
   - Uses equivalence factor to convert battery energy into "virtual H₂."

5. **Model Predictive Control (MPC):**
   - Uses short driving-cycle prediction horizon.
   - Solves an optimization problem at each step.
   - KTU 2024 frequently asks the *block diagram* of MPC.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Equation | Symbolic Form | Variables & Units | Typical Use |
|---|---------------------|---------------|--------------------|-------------|
| 1 | Instantaneous Power Balance | $P_{load}(t) = P_{FC}(t) + P_{bat}(t) + P_{UC}(t)$ | All in **W** | Energy audit at any instant |
| 2 | FC Sizing (Average Load) | $P_{FC,rated} = \dfrac{1}{\eta_{FC}} \cdot \overline{P_{load}}$ | $\eta_{FC}$ = FC efficiency (~ **0.5**) | Selecting FC stack rating |
| 3 | H₂ Mass for Trip | $m_{H_2} = \dfrac{E_{trip}}{\eta_{sys} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}$ | $LHV_{H_2} = \mathbf{120\,MJ/kg}$ | Range estimation |
| 4 | Ragone Energy Density | $E_{d} = \dfrac{C \cdot V}{m}$ | Wh/kg | Comparing storage |
| 5 | Ragone Power Density | $P_{d} = \dfrac{V^{2}}{4 \cdot R_{int} \cdot m}$ | W/kg | Comparing storage |
| 6 | Battery Energy Stored | $E_{bat} = V_{nom} \cdot Q_{Ah}$ | Wh | Battery sizing |
| 7 | UC Energy Stored | $E_{UC} = \dfrac{1}{2} C_{UC} V_{UC}^{2}$ | J or Wh | UC sizing |
| 8 | UC Bank Capacitance | $C_{bank} = \dfrac{C_{cell}}{N_{series}} \cdot N_{parallel}$ | F | UC pack design |
| 9 | Battery C-Rate Limit | $I_{max} = C_{rate} \cdot Q_{rated}$ | A | Current limit check |
| 10 | FC Voltage (Nernst + Losses) | $V_{FC} = N \left[E_{nernst} - \eta_{act} - \eta_{ohm} - \eta_{conc}\right]$ | V | FC polarisation |
| 11 | Energy of Regen Pulse | $E_{regen} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^{2} \cdot \eta_{mech}$ | J | Braking energy captured |
| 12 | Vehicle Power Demand | $P_{load} = \dfrac{v}{\eta_{drivetrain}} \left( m g f_r + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A v^{2} + m a \right)$ | W | Longitudinal dynamics |
| 13 | SOC Update (Battery) | $SOC(t+1) = SOC(t) + \dfrac{\eta_c I_c \Delta t - \dfrac{I_d \Delta t}{\eta_d}}{Q_{rated}}$ | dimensionless | Time-domain simulation |
| 14 | H₂ Flow Rate (Stack) | $\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{N \cdot I}{2F} \cdot M_{H_2}$ | kg/s | From Faraday's law |
| 15 | Equivalent H₂ Consumption (ECMS) | $\dot{m}_{eq} = \dot{m}_{H_2} + s \cdot P_{bat}$ | kg/s | ECMS cost function |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked to "design a hybrid energy storage," examiners expect you to **derive power balance**, **size the FC**, **size the buffer**, and **draw the architecture** — in that exact order.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive:** Toyota Mirai, Hyundai NEXO, Honda Clarity — all use FC + Battery hybrid.
- **Rail:** Alstom Coradia iLint hydrogen train — FC + Li-ion + UC.
- **Aviation (Drones):** PEMFC + Li-Po — extended endurance.
- **Heavy-Duty Trucks:** Cummins, Hyundai XCIENT — FC + high-power battery.
- **Grid Storage:** FC + Battery for renewable firming.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Balance and Source Sizing

**Problem Statement:** A fuel-cell vehicle requires a peak traction power of **$P_{peak} = 90$ kW** during acceleration, and a cruise power of **$P_{cruise} = 20$ kW**. The PEMFC efficiency is **$\eta_{FC} = 0.50$**, drivetrain efficiency **$\eta_{dt} = 0.90$**, and the battery + ultracapacitor system can supply the difference.

### Step 1 — Mechanical Power Required at Wheels

The traction load equation (longitudinal dynamics) for flat road at constant acceleration $a$ is:

$$
P_{load}(t) = \dfrac{v(t)}{\eta_{dt}} \Big( m\, g\, f_r + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\, C_d\, A\, v(t)^{2} + m\, a(t) \Big)
$$

**Explanation of each term:**
- $m\,g\,f_r$ → rolling resistance (vehicle weight × coefficient).
- $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A v^{2}$ → aerodynamic drag.
- $m\,a$ → inertial force during acceleration.
- $v/\eta_{dt}$ → converts wheel power to electrical power needed at bus.

### Step 2 — Power Split Between FC and Buffer

For the *power-following* EMS, the FC supplies the **average (cruise + slow ramps)** power, while the battery + UC bridge the **transient gap**:

$$
P_{FC}(t) = \overline{P_{load}(t)} = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} P_{load}(t)\, dt
$$

$$
P_{buffer}(t) = P_{load}(t) - P_{FC}(t)
$$

**Conversion logic:** We integrate load over a representative drive cycle (e.g., UDDS, NEDC, or WLTP) to get the average.

### Step 3 — Numerical Sizing (Cruise-Dominated Cycle)

Assume the average power demand = cruise power (≈ 20 kW at wheels). At the DC bus, after drivetrain losses:

$$
P_{FC,rated} = \dfrac{P_{cruise}}{\eta_{dt}} = \dfrac{20\,000}{0.90} \approx 22.22\, \text{kW (electrical at bus)}
$$

Now correcting for FC efficiency (only 50% of H₂ energy becomes electricity):

$$
P_{FC,rated} = \dfrac{22.22}{\eta_{FC}} = \dfrac{22.22}{0.50} = 44.44\, \text{kW (thermal from H₂)}
$$

Equivalently, **H₂ consumption at cruise:**

$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{P_{FC,elec}}{LHV_{H_2} \cdot \eta_{FC}} = \dfrac{22\,222\,W}{120 \times 10^{6}\,J/kg \cdot 0.5} \approx 3.70 \times 10^{-4}\, kg/s
$$

### Step 4 — Peak Buffer Requirement

$$
P_{buffer,peak} = \dfrac{P_{peak}}{\eta_{dt}} - P_{FC,rated,elec}
$$

$$
P_{buffer,peak} = \dfrac{90\,000}{0.90} - 22\,222 = 100\,000 - 22\,222 = 77\,778\, \text{W} \approx 77.8\, \text{kW}
$$

### Step 5 — Ultracapacitor Sizing for an 8-Second Acceleration Pulse

$$
E_{UC,required} = P_{buffer,peak} \cdot t_{peak} = 77\,778 \times 8 = 622\,222\, \text{J}
$$

Convert to Wh:

$$
E_{UC} = \dfrac{622\,222}{3600} \approx 172.84\, \text{Wh}
$$

For a UC bank rated at **$V_{UC} = 48$ V** operating between **$V_{max}=48$ V** and **$V_{min}=24$ V**:

$$
E_{UC} = \dfrac{1}{2}\, C_{bank}\, (V_{max}^{2} - V_{min}^{2})
$$

$$
172.84 \times 3600 = \dfrac{1}{2}\, C_{bank}\, (48^{2} - 24^{2})
$$

$$
622\,224 = 0.5 \cdot C_{bank} \cdot (2304 - 576) = 0.5 \cdot C_{bank} \cdot 1728
$$

$$
C_{bank} = \dfrac{622\,224}{864} \approx 720\, \text{F}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 720 F, 48 V ultracapacitor bank would meet the 8-second peak demand.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — H₂ Mass Required for a Given Trip

**Given:** A vehicle consumes average electrical load $P_{avg} = 15$ kW over a 4-hour trip.

### Step 1 — Total Electrical Energy

$$
E_{trip} = P_{avg} \cdot t = 15\,000 \times 4 \times 3600 = 216\,000\,000\, \text{J} = 216\, \text{MJ}
$$

### Step 2 — System Efficiency

Assume $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{FC} \cdot \eta_{dt} = 0.50 \times 0.90 = 0.45$.

### Step 3 — Chemical Energy from H₂

$$
E_{H_2} = \dfrac{E_{trip}}{\eta_{sys}} = \dfrac{216}{0.45} = 480\, \text{MJ}
$$

### Step 4 — H₂ Mass

$$
m_{H_2} = \dfrac{E_{H_2}}{LHV_{H_2}} = \dfrac{480 \times 10^{6}}{120 \times 10^{6}} = 4.0\, \text{kg}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 4 kg hydrogen tank (≈ 700 bar, 122 L volume) supports a 4-hour, 15 kW trip.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Storage Options

| Property | PEM Fuel Cell | Li-ion Battery | Ultracapacitor |
|----------|---------------|----------------|----------------|
| Energy Density | 350–800 Wh/kg (system) | 150–250 Wh/kg | 5–10 Wh/kg |
| Power Density | 200–500 W/kg | 200–1000 W/kg | 5000–10 000 W/kg |
| Cycle Life | ~ 5000 h (stack hours) | 1000–3000 | > 500 000 |
| Response Time | 1–5 s | 100 ms–1 s | 1–10 ms |
| Refuel/Recharge | Minutes (H₂ fill) | 30 min–8 h | Seconds |
| Cold Start | −30 °C (with aux) | OK | OK |
| Role in Hybrid | **Energy source** | **Mid-buffer** | **Peak buffer** |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Frequency-Decoupling EMS

Given a load power signal $P_{load}(t)$ decomposed via low-pass filter into low-frequency and high-frequency components:

$$
P_{load}(t) = P_{LF}(t) + P_{HF}(t)
$$

The FC (slow) handles $P_{LF}$, the battery handles medium swings, and the UC handles $P_{HF}$:

$$
P_{FC}(t) = \alpha \cdot P_{LF}(t), \quad P_{bat}(t) = \beta \cdot P_{HF,low}(t), \quad P_{UC}(t) = \gamma \cdot P_{HF,high}(t)
$$

where $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$ (instantaneous power balance).

**First-order low-pass filter implementation:**

$$
P_{LF}(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \tau s}\, P_{load}(s)
$$

$$
P_{HF}(s) = P_{load}(s) - P_{LF}(s) = \dfrac{\tau s}{1 + \tau s}\, P_{load}(s)
$$

**Tuning $\tau$:** Larger $\tau$ shifts more load to UC (more FC protection). Smaller $\tau$ allows FC to follow load dynamically (risky).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Full Hybrid Architecture — Fuel Cell + Battery + Ultracapacitor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENERGY_SOURCES
        H2TANK[High Pressure H2 Tank 700 bar]
        FCSTACK[PEM Fuel Cell Stack]
        BATPACK[Li-ion Battery Pack]
        UCBANK[Ultracapacitor Bank]
    end

    subgraph POWER_ELECTRONICS
        DCFC[DC-DC Converter FC side]
        DCBAT[DC-DC Converter Battery side]
        DCUC[DC-DC Converter UC side]
        DCBUS[High Voltage DC Bus 350 to 650 V]
    end

    subgraph CONTROL_UNIT
        EMS[Energy Management Strategy]
        VCU[Vehicle Control Unit]
        SENSOR1[SOC Sensor Battery]
        SENSOR2[Voltage Sensor UC]
        SENSOR3[Current Sensor FC]
    end

    subgraph TRACTION
        INVERTER[Three Phase Inverter]
        MOTOR[Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motor]
        TRANS[Transmission and Differential]
        WHEEL[Drive Wheels]
    end

    H2TANK --> FCSTACK
    FCSTACK --> DCFC
    BATPACK --> DCBAT
    UCBANK --> DCUC

    DCFC --> DCBUS
    DCBAT --> DCBUS
    DCUC --> DCBUS

    DCBUS --> INVERTER
    INVERTER --> MOTOR
    MOTOR --> TRANS
    TRANS --> WHEEL

    SENSOR1 --> EMS
    SENSOR2 --> EMS
    SENSOR3 --> EMS
    EMS --> VCU
    VCU --> DCFC
    VCU --> DCBAT
    VCU --> DCUC
```

## 4.2 Power-Following Energy Management Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Vehicle Ignition ON]) --> READ[Read Driver Pedal and Vehicle Speed]
    READ --> COMPUTE[Compute Pload from Drive Cycle Model]
    COMPUTE --> DECIDE{Pload Greater Than Pfc Rated}

    DECIDE -- YES --> BUFFER_SUPPLY[Battery plus UC supply delta P]
    DECIDE -- NO --> CHARGE_LOGIC{SOC of Battery Above Threshold}

    CHARGE_LOGIC -- YES --> FC_FOLLOW[FC follows Pload and Battery idles]
    CHARGE_LOGIC -- NO --> FC_CHARGE[FC supplies Pload plus charges Battery]

    BUFFER_SUPPLY --> REGEN_CHECK{Braking Detected}
    FC_FOLLOW --> REGEN_CHECK
    FC_CHARGE --> REGEN_CHECK

    REGEN_CHECK -- YES --> REGEN[UC and Battery absorb regenerative energy]
    REGEN_CHECK -- NO --> LOOP[Update SOC and continue to next time step]

    REGEN --> LOOP
    LOOP --> READ
```

## 4.3 Frequency Decoupling Filter Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PLOAD[Pload t] --> LPF[Low Pass Filter tau large]
    PLOAD --> HPF[High Pass Filter tau small]

    LPF --> PFC[PFC assigned to FC]
    LPF --> PBAT[Pbat portion to Battery]
    HPF --> PUC[PUC assigned to Ultracapacitor]

    PFC --> DCBUS1[DC Bus Contribution]
    PBAT --> DCBUS1
    PUC --> DCBUS1
```

## 4.4 HESS Sizing Logic Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INPUT[Inputs: Trip Energy Etrip MJ and Peak Power Ppeak kW] --> AVG[Compute Average Power Pavg]
    AVG --> SIZEFC[Size FC at 1 point 1 to 1 point 3 times Pavg]
    SIZEFC --> SIZEBAT[Size Battery for Etrip minus FC contribution and SOC window 30 to 80]
    SIZEBAT --> SIZEUC[Size UC for Ppeak minus Pfc rated over short pulse 5 to 15 s]
    SIZEUC --> VALIDATE{Validate Mass and Volume and Cost}
    VALIDATE -- FAIL --> ITERATE[Iterate Sizing]
    VALIDATE -- PASS --> OUTPUT[Final HESS Specification]
    ITERATE --> SIZEFC
```
