# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define hybridization of energy storage devices. Why is a single storage source insufficient for fuel cell vehicles?**

**Model Answer (Examiner-Ready):**

> Hybridization of energy storage devices is the integration of two or more complementary storage technologies (typically a high-energy source and a high-power source) into a unified powertrain, managed by an Energy Management Strategy, to simultaneously meet the **sustained energy demand** and the **transient peak power demand** of a vehicle.
>
> A single storage device is insufficient because:
> 1. The **Ragone plot** shows energy density and power density are inversely related — no single device excels at both.
> 2. A **fuel cell** alone has slow dynamic response (~1–5 s) and **cannot absorb regenerative braking energy**.
> 3. A **battery alone** overheats during repeated high-C pulses, degrading cycle life.
> 4. An **ultracapacitor alone** has too little energy density for sustained driving.
> 5. Hybridization combines the **energy strength of FC**, the **mid-range versatility of battery**, and the **peak power capability of UC**.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**List the three main hybridization topologies and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Topology | Advantage |
|----------|-----------|
| FC + Battery | Absorbs regen energy; cost-effective |
| FC + Ultracapacitor | Excellent transient response; long FC life |
| FC + Battery + UC (Full HESS) | Optimal load sharing across all drive cycles |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — Option 1 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Fuel Cell + Battery + Ultracapacitor** hybrid energy storage system (HESS) for an automotive application. Label all major components and describe the role of the Energy Management Strategy. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the sizing equations for the fuel cell, battery, and ultracapacitor banks given the peak power demand $P_{peak}$, the cruise power demand $P_{cruise}$, the trip energy $E_{trip}$, the system efficiency $\eta_{sys}$, and the LHV of hydrogen. Show all steps. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to A(a):

The architecture comprises three energy/power stages joined at a **common DC bus** through **DC-DC converters**:

1. **Fuel Cell Stage:** A PEMFC stack fed from a **700-bar H₂ tank**. The output is unregulated DC (~250–450 V), stepped to bus voltage via a **unidirectional boost DC-DC converter**.
2. **Battery Stage:** A Li-ion pack (e.g., 300 V nominal) interfaced via a **bidirectional DC-DC converter** to absorb regen and supply peaks.
3. **Ultracapacitor Stage:** A 48–100 V EDLC bank with a **bidirectional DC-DC converter** for millisecond response.
4. **DC Bus:** Common 350–650 V bus feeding the **three-phase inverter** and **PMSM traction motor**.
5. **Energy Management Strategy (EMS):** Reads SOC of battery, voltage of UC, current of FC, and driver demand. Generates switching commands for each DC-DC converter to maintain instantaneous power balance:
$$P_{FC}(t) + P_{bat}(t) + P_{UC}(t) = P_{load}(t) / \eta_{dt}$$

**Block diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid figure.)

#### Model Solution to A(b):

**Step 1 — FC Sizing:**

Average load over the drive cycle:

$$
\overline{P_{load}} = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} P_{load}(t)\, dt \approx P_{cruise}
$$

FC electrical rating (accounting for drivetrain):

$$
P_{FC,elec} = \dfrac{\overline{P_{load}}}{\eta_{dt}}
$$

H₂ mass flow at rated output:

$$
\dot{m}_{H_2} = \dfrac{P_{FC,elec}}{\eta_{FC} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}
$$

H₂ mass for trip:

$$
m_{H_2,trip} = \dfrac{E_{trip}}{\eta_{sys} \cdot LHV_{H_2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Battery Sizing:**

Energy that battery must handle:

$$
E_{bat} = (E_{trip} - E_{FC,trip}) = E_{trip} - P_{FC,elec} \cdot t_{trip}
$$

Battery capacity in Ah:

$$
Q_{bat} = \dfrac{E_{bat}}{V_{nom} \cdot \Delta SOC}
$$

where $\Delta SOC = SOC_{max} - SOC_{min} = 0.8 - 0.3 = 0.5$ (typical window).

**Step 3 — Ultracapacitor Sizing:**

Peak power buffer requirement:

$$
P_{UC,peak} = \dfrac{P_{peak}}{\eta_{dt}} - P_{FC,elec}
$$

Required energy for a peak pulse of duration $t_{pulse}$:

$$
E_{UC} = P_{UC,peak} \cdot t_{pulse}
$$

Required capacitance at voltage range $V_{max} \to V_{min}$:

$$
C_{bank} = \dfrac{2 E_{UC}}{V_{max}^{2} - V_{min}^{2}}
$$

---

### Question B — Option 2 (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Supplementary Model]**

**(a)** Explain **Power-Following** and **Frequency-Decoupling** energy management strategies for hybridizing a fuel cell vehicle. Compare them using a table on response time, FC stress, and implementation complexity. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A fuel-cell hybrid bus consumes an average electrical load of **$P_{avg} = 30$ kW** for a **6-hour shift**. The PEMFC operates at $\eta_{FC} = 0.55$, the drivetrain at $\eta_{dt} = 0.92$, and hydrogen LHV is **120 MJ/kg**. The peak demand during acceleration is **$P_{peak} = 120$ kW** for **10 seconds**. Assuming the fuel cell is sized at $1.2 \times P_{avg}$, calculate:
  (i) H₂ mass consumed in one shift.
  (ii) Peak power that the ultracapacitor must supply.
  (iii) Required UC bank capacitance if the bank operates between 60 V and 30 V. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to B(a):

**Power-Following Strategy:**
- The FC provides only the **slowly varying (averaged)** portion of the load.
- Battery + UC handle **all transients and peaks**.
- Simple low-pass filter on $P_{load}$ produces $P_{FC}$ reference.
- FC operates near its **best-efficiency point**, reducing degradation.

**Frequency-Decoupling Strategy:**
- Decomposes $P_{load}(t)$ into **low-frequency** and **high-frequency** bands using complementary low-pass / high-pass filters.
- Low-frequency band → FC + Battery.
- High-frequency band → Ultracapacitor.
- Allows *parallel* operation with optimal frequency allocation.

**Comparison Table:**

| Criterion | Power-Following | Frequency-Decoupling |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Response time | Slow (filter τ = 5–10 s) | Fast (τ tuned to FC dynamics) |
| FC stress | Very low | Moderate |
| Battery cycles | High (handles all transients) | Low (only mid-frequency) |
| UC utilization | High | Very high |
| Implementation complexity | Low | Moderate (filter design) |
| Real-time feasibility | Excellent | Good |

#### Model Solution to B(b):

**Given:** $P_{avg} = 30$ kW, $t = 6$ h, $\eta_{FC} = 0.55$, $\eta_{dt} = 0.92$, $LHV = 120$ MJ/kg, $P_{peak} = 120$ kW, $t_{pulse} = 10$ s, $V_{max} = 60$ V, $V_{min} = 30$ V.

**(i) H₂ Mass for the Shift:**

Electrical energy at bus for the trip:

$$
E_{elec} = P_{avg} \cdot t = 30\,000 \times 6 \times 3600 = 648 \times 10^{6}\, \text{J} = 648\, \text{MJ}
$$

H₂ chemical energy required (FC efficiency converts H₂ → electricity):

$$
E_{H_2} = \dfrac{E_{elec}}{\eta_{FC}} = \dfrac{648}{0.55} \approx 1178.18\, \text{MJ}
$$

H₂ mass:

$$
m_{H_2} = \dfrac{E_{H_2}}{LHV_{H_2}} = \dfrac{1178.18}{120} \approx 9.82\, \text{kg}
$$

**(ii) Peak UC Power:**

FC is sized at:

$$
P_{FC,elec} = 1.2 \times P_{avg} = 1.2 \times 30 = 36\, \text{kW}
$$

Peak electrical demand at bus:

$$
P_{peak,elec} = \dfrac{P_{peak}}{\eta_{dt}} = \dfrac{120\,000}{0.92} \approx 130.43\, \text{kW}
$$

UC peak power:

$$
P_{UC,peak} = P_{peak,elec} - P_{FC,elec} = 130.43 - 36 = 94.43\, \text{kW}
$$

**(iii) UC Bank Capacitance:**

Energy required for 10 s peak:

$$
E_{UC} = P_{UC,peak} \cdot t_{pulse} = 94\,435 \times 10 = 944\,350\, \text{J}
$$

Using the energy relation $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C (V_{max}^{2} - V_{min}^{2})$:

$$
C_{bank} = \dfrac{2 E_{UC}}{V_{max}^{2} - V_{min}^{2}} = \dfrac{2 \times 944\,350}{60^{2} - 30^{2}} = \dfrac{1\,888\,700}{3600 - 900} = \dfrac{1\,888\,700}{2700}
$$

$$
C_{bank} \approx 699.5\, \text{F}
$$

**Result:** A 700 F ultracapacitor bank operating in the 60 V → 30 V window suffices.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting efficiency in power balance:** Always include $\eta_{dt}$ when converting wheel power to bus power. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using $LHV = 142$ MJ/kg instead of 120 MJ/kg:** KTU 2024 papers prefer the **LHV = 120 MJ/kg** convention. Confirm from the question.
> 3. **UC energy equation mistake:** Using $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ with full $V_{max}$ instead of $(V_{max}^{2} - V_{min}^{2})$ — UC is never allowed to discharge to 0 V.
> 4. **Battery SOC window:** Always specify usable SOC window (typically **30%–80%**); full 0–100% is non-realistic and penalized.
> 5. **Not drawing a block diagram:** A HESS question without architecture sketch loses 2 marks minimum.
> 6. **Mixing up power and energy:** Power (kW) is *rate*; energy (kWh) is *capacity*. KTU examiners actively check this.

---

## Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Hybridization** = combining ≥ 2 storage technologies to overcome the Ragone trade-off.
- ✅ **Primary source** = Fuel Cell (steady energy, slow response, cannot regen).
- ✅ **Mid-buffer** = Battery (energy + moderate power, can regen).
- ✅ **Peak buffer** = Ultracapacitor (high power, milliseconds response, near-infinite cycles).
- ✅ **Instantaneous power balance:** $P_{FC} + P_{bat} + P_{UC} = P_{load} / \eta_{dt}$.
- ✅ **H₂ mass formula:** $m_{H_2} = E_{trip} / (\eta_{sys} \cdot LHV_{H_2})$; use **LHV = 120 MJ/kg**.
- ✅ **UC energy stored:** $E_{UC} = \tfrac{1}{2} C (V_{max}^{2} - V_{min}^{2})$.
- ✅ **Battery sizing:** $Q_{bat} = E_{bat} / (V_{nom} \cdot \Delta SOC)$, with $\Delta SOC \approx 0.5$.
- ✅ **FC sizing:** typically $1.1\times$ to $1.3\times$ average load.
- ✅ **EMS options:** Rule-based, Power-Following, Frequency-Decoupling, ECMS, MPC.
- ✅ **Power-Following:** FC = average load; buffer = transients.
- ✅ **Frequency-Decoupling:** LF → FC+Battery, HF → UC.
- ✅ **Topologies:** FC+Bat, FC+UC, FC+Bat+UC (full HESS).
- ✅ **Regen absorption:** Battery and UC only — never the FC.
- ✅ **Real-world examples:** Toyota Mirai (FC+Battery), Alstom Coradia (FC+Bat+UC).
- ✅ **Faraday constant:** $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol (for H₂ flow derivation).
- ✅ **Round-trip efficiency** order: Battery (85–95%) > UC (90–95%) > FC system (45–60%).
- ✅ **Cycle life** order: UC (500 000+) > Battery (1000–3000) > FC stack (~5000 h).
- ✅ **KTU keywords to memorize:** HESS, EMS, Ragone plot, ECMS, MPC, SOC, SOH, C-Rate, LHV.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Short-Term Storage Systems: Flywheel Accumulators and Ultra-Capacitors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT741 - HYDROGEN ENGINES AND FUEL CELL VEHICLES
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Short-Term Storage Systems: Flywheel Accumulators and Ultra-Capacitors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Introduction to Short-Term Energy Storage in Vehicles

In the context of a fuel cell vehicle (FCV) or a hydrogen internal combustion engine vehicle, the prime mover (fuel cell stack or H₂ ICE) cannot respond instantaneously to the highly transient power demands of road vehicles. Acceleration, regenerative braking, and hill-climbing demand power pulses that are several times the average cruising power. **Short-term energy storage systems (ESS)** act as a "buffer" or "power-shuttle" that bridges the gap between the slow-response primary energy source and the fast-response traction load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Short-term storage systems are energy reservoirs with high cycle-life, high power density, and moderate energy density, designed to deliver/absorb power for durations ranging from a few seconds to a few minutes. The two principal physical realizations discussed in Module 3 are **Flywheel Accumulators (mechanical kinetic storage)** and **Ultra-Capacitors (electrostatic double-layer storage)**.

### 1.2 Flywheel Accumulator — Definition and Analogy

A **Flywheel Accumulator** is a mechanical energy storage device that stores energy in the form of **rotational kinetic energy** of a spinning mass. Energy is input by applying torque to accelerate the rotor to a high angular velocity, and energy is recovered by drawing torque from the decelerating rotor (acting as a generator).

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a spinning top that you wind up — it stores energy as long as it keeps spinning. When you push a bicycle pedal rapidly to spin the wheel and then stop pedaling, the wheel keeps moving on its own — that residual "spin energy" is what a flywheel harvests. In a fuel cell vehicle, instead of using chemical energy directly during every short burst of acceleration, the flywheel "spins up" to absorb excess fuel cell power during idle/cruising and "spins down" (acting as a generator) to deliver peak power during acceleration.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern flywheel energy storage systems (FESS) used in vehicles typically operate in vacuum containment with **magnetic bearings** and reach surface speeds of the order of **600 m/s to 1000 m/s**, with rotors made from carbon-fibre composites for high specific strength.

### 1.3 Ultra-Capacitor (Supercapacitor) — Definition and Analogy

An **Ultra-Capacitor** (also called a **Supercapacitor** or **Electric Double-Layer Capacitor, EDLC**) is an electrochemical capacitor that stores energy by separation of charge at the electrode–electrolyte interface. Unlike a conventional capacitor, the effective "plate separation" is reduced to a few angstroms, and the surface area is enormously amplified using **activated carbon**, yielding capacitances of thousands of farads per cell.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a sponge that can soak up water (energy) almost instantly and release it almost instantly, but cannot hold as much total water as a large water tank. Conventional batteries are like a tank — high capacity, slow fill/discharge. Ultra-capacitors are like a sponge — lower capacity, but extremely fast fill/discharge and practically unlimited cycles.

> [!NOTE]
> The key figure of merit is the **electric double layer** formed at the interface — described by the **Helmholtz model**, where two layers of opposite charge (one on the electrode side, one in the electrolyte side) are separated by a distance of only **~0.3 to 0.5 nm**, yielding a colossal capacitance per unit area.

### 1.4 Why These Two Together?

The KTU 2024 scheme places Flywheels and Ultra-Capacitors in the same module because both serve the *same functional role* in the vehicle powertrain — **buffering high-power transients** — but realize it through *opposite physical domains* (mechanical vs. electrochemical). Together they form the high-power shoulder of the energy storage pyramid that complements the high-energy, low-power base (hydrogen tank + fuel cell or battery).

---

### 1.5 Comparison Snapshot

| Parameter | Flywheel Accumulator | Ultra-Capacitor |
|---|---|---|
| Storage Domain | Mechanical (kinetic) | Electrochemical (EDLC) |
| Energy Equation | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | $E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ |
| Typical Specific Energy | **10 – 100 Wh/kg** | **5 – 10 Wh/kg** |
| Typical Specific Power | **1000 – 5000 W/kg** | **1000 – 10000 W/kg** |
| Cycle Life | ~10⁵ – 10⁶ | ~10⁶ – 10⁷ |
| Charge Time | Seconds to minutes | Seconds |
| Self-Discharge | High (friction, drag) | Moderate (leakage current) |
| Operating Temperature | Wide | -40 °C to +65 °C |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard constant for KTU problems:** Permittivity of free space $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \text{ F/m}$. The double-layer separation distance is typically taken as **$d \approx 0.3 \text{ nm}$** for theoretical capacitance calculations.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Flywheel Accumulator — Operating Principle

A flywheel stores energy in the form of **rotational kinetic energy** of a rigid rotating mass. The energy stored depends on two parameters:
1. The mass moment of inertia $I$ of the rotor (determined by geometry and mass distribution)
2. The square of the angular velocity $\omega$

The **why** behind the energy equation: Kinetic energy of a moving particle is $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2}$. For a rigid body rotating about a fixed axis, every elemental mass $dm$ at radius $r$ moves with tangential velocity $v = \omega r$. Integrating over the body gives the moment of inertia $I$, and the total kinetic energy is $\tfrac{1}{2}I\omega^{2}$.

**Key structural insights:**

- **Hoop-stress design constraint:** As rotor speed increases, the centrifugal tensile stress $\sigma$ in the rim increases. For a thin rotating rim of radius $r$ and density $\rho$, the hoop stress is:
$$\sigma = \rho \, v^{2}$$
where $v = \omega r$ is the rim linear speed. To avoid rupture, the material's allowable stress $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$ must satisfy $v_{\max} < \sqrt{\sigma_{\text{allow}} / \rho}$. This is why composite carbon-fibre rims are preferred (high $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$, low $\rho$).

- **Energy density limit:** The volumetric energy density of a flywheel is fundamentally limited by the material's specific strength $\sigma_{\text{allow}} / \rho$. The theoretical specific energy (per unit mass) for a thin-rim flywheel is:
$$e_{\max} = \frac{1}{2} v_{\max}^{2} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}{2\rho}$$

- **Bearing losses and containment:** To minimize parasitic drag, modern FESS operate in **vacuum chambers** and use **active magnetic bearings** (AMB). Total energy recovery over a cycle depends on the **round-trip efficiency**, typically 80–95%.

- **Power capability:** The power that can be drawn is limited by the rotor's torque capacity, bearing/motor limits, and electronics — not by the storage capacity. Hence flywheels are **power-dense** even though their energy density is moderate.

### 2.2 Ultra-Capacitor (EDLC) — Operating Principle

An ultra-capacitor stores energy via the **Electric Double Layer (EDL)** — two charge layers separated by a sub-nanometre distance at the porous carbon–electrolyte interface.

**Step-by-step physical model:**

1. **Porous activated carbon electrode:** The electrode is a highly porous carbon structure with an internal surface area (BET area) of **1500 – 3000 m² per gram** of carbon. This vastly amplifies the effective plate area $A$.

2. **Ionic double layer formation:** When voltage is applied, ions in the electrolyte migrate to the electrode surface. A layer of solvated ions is tightly held at the electrode (Helmholtz plane), forming a "double layer" with separation $d \approx 0.3 - 0.5 \text{ nm}$.

3. **Capacitance equation:** For a parallel-plate approximation:
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$
With $A$ in thousands of m²/g and $d$ in 0.3 nm, $C$ reaches values of **100 F/g** to **200 F/g** of carbon — orders of magnitude higher than a conventional electrolytic capacitor.

4. **Voltage window:** Limited by electrolyte decomposition. For organic electrolytes (common in commercial supercapacitors): ~2.7 V per cell. For aqueous: ~1.0 V per cell. Cells must be balanced, often in series with voltage equalization.

5. **Energy storage:** With a typical supercapacitor cell of 3000 F at 2.7 V, energy stored is:
$$E = \tfrac{1}{2} C V^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 3000 \times (2.7)^{2} \approx 10.9 \text{ kJ} \approx 3.0 \text{ Wh}$$

### 2.3 Vehicle Powertrain Integration

In a fuel cell vehicle, the ultra-capacitor (or flywheel) is connected to the DC bus through a **bidirectional DC-DC converter**. The control strategy is:

- During **acceleration** (peak power demand): ESS discharges → DC bus voltage is supported → FC stack does not need to ramp up to peak power.
- During **braking / deceleration** (regenerative): ESS absorbs regenerated current → motor acts as generator.
- During **steady cruise**: FC stack provides average power; ESS tops up/down as needed.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|

| 2 | Moment of inertia — solid disc | $I = \tfrac{1}{2} m r^{2}$ | kg·m² | Solid cylinder about its axis |
| 3 | Moment of inertia — thin rim | $I = m r^{2}$ | kg·m² | Rim mass concentrated at radius $r$ |
| 4 | Moment of inertia — solid sphere | $I = \tfrac{2}{5} m r^{2}$ | kg·m² | Spinning sphere |

| 6 | Hoop stress in rim | $\sigma = \rho \, v^{2}$ | Pa | $\rho$ = material density |

### 2.5 Engineering Utility and Field Deployment

- **Formula-1 KERS (Kinetic Energy Recovery System):** 2009–2013 era F1 cars used a 60 kW, 6-second flywheel-based MGU-K before transitioning to hybrid ERS systems. This is the most famous commercial validation of vehicle flywheel storage.
- **Mazda i-ELOOP and similar systems** in production road cars use ultra-capacitors to recover braking energy and power auxiliaries, with an ultra-capacitor bank rated at ~25 V / several kJ.
- **Tram, bus, and train regenerative braking systems** (e.g., ABB, Siemens, Alstom) use supercapacitor banks of 100s of kW to recover and re-deploy braking energy at stations.
- **Grid-level FESS:** Beacon Power, Helion, and Temporal Power have deployed flywheel systems rated 20 kW – 1 MW for grid frequency regulation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Stored in a Solid-Disc Flywheel

**Given:** A solid cylindrical flywheel of mass $m$, radius $r$, rotating at angular velocity $\omega$ about its central axis.

**Step 1 — Set up the elemental mass element.**
Consider a thin annular ring at radius $\rho$ (using $\rho$ here to avoid confusion with material density) of thickness $d\rho$ and axial height $h$. The elemental volume is:
$$dV = 2\pi \rho \, h \, d\rho$$

**Step 2 — Express the elemental mass.**
$$dm = \rho_m \, dV = 2\pi \rho_m \, h \, \rho \, d\rho$$
where $\rho_m$ is the material density.

**Step 3 — Moment of inertia of the elemental ring.**
For a thin ring of mass $dm$ at radius $\rho$:
$$dI = \rho^{2} \, dm = 2\pi \rho_m \, h \, \rho^{3} \, d\rho$$

**Step 4 — Integrate from 0 to $r$.**
$$I = \int_{0}^{r} 2\pi \rho_m h \, \rho^{3} \, d\rho = 2\pi \rho_m h \left[ \frac{\rho^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{r} = \frac{\pi \rho_m h r^{4}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Replace $\pi r^{2} h$ with the total volume, and $\rho_m \times V$ with mass $m$.**
$$I = \frac{1}{2} m r^{2}$$

**Step 6 — Write the kinetic energy.**
$$E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2} \left( \frac{1}{2} m r^{2} \right) \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{4} m r^{2} \omega^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Express in terms of rim speed $v = \omega r$.**
$$E = \frac{1}{4} m v^{2}$$

> This shows that for a solid disc, only **one-quarter** of $mv^{2}$ is stored (because the inner mass moves slower than the rim), explaining why **thin-rim** flywheels (where all mass is at radius $r$) store $\tfrac{1}{2}mv^{2}$ — twice as much energy at the same rim speed and mass.

### 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Specific Energy of a Flywheel (Material-Limited)

**Step 1 — Start with the specific energy of a thin-rim flywheel.**
The mass $m$ is concentrated at radius $r$, so $I = m r^{2}$ and:
$$e = \frac{E}{m} = \frac{1}{2} r^{2} \omega^{2} = \frac{1}{2} v^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the hoop-stress constraint.**
For a thin rotating rim, the centrifugal hoop stress is balanced by the tensile stress in the cross-section:
$$\sigma = \rho_m v^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $v$ at the stress limit.**
$$v_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}{\rho_m}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into specific energy.**
$$e_{\max} = \frac{1}{2} v_{\max}^{2} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}{2 \rho_m}$$

**Step 5 — Express in Wh/kg.**
$$e_{\max, \text{Wh/kg}} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}{2 \rho_m} \times \frac{1}{9.81 \times 1000} \times \frac{1000}{3600} \times 9.81 / 1 = \frac{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}{7200 \, \rho_m} \text{ (in Wh/kg if }\sigma \text{ in Pa)}$$

A cleaner form, using $1 \text{ J/kg} = 1/3600 \text{ Wh/kg}$:
$$e_{\max} \text{[Wh/kg]} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{allow}}}{7200 \, \rho_m}$$

> **Example:** Forged steel: $\sigma_{\text{allow}} \approx 700 \text{ MPa}$, $\rho_m = 7850 \text{ kg/m}^3$ gives $e_{\max} \approx 12.4 \text{ Wh/kg}$. Carbon-fibre composite: $\sigma_{\text{allow}} \approx 2500 \text{ MPa}$, $\rho_m = 1600 \text{ kg/m}^3$ gives $e_{\max} \approx 217 \text{ Wh/kg}$ — justifying the move to composites.

### 3.3 Derivation: Energy Stored in an Ultra-Capacitor Cell

**Step 1 — Definition of capacitance.**
A capacitor with charge $Q$ at voltage $V$ has capacitance $C = Q/V$, so $Q = C V$.

**Step 2 — Energy is the work done to charge.**
The incremental work to add charge $dQ$ at voltage $V$ is $dW = V \, dQ = (Q/C) \, dQ$.

**Step 3 — Integrate from 0 to $Q_{\text{final}}$.**
$$E = \int_{0}^{Q_{\text{final}}} \frac{Q}{C} \, dQ = \frac{1}{2} \frac{Q_{\text{final}}^{2}}{C}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $Q_{\text{final}} = C V_{\text{final}}$.**
$$E = \frac{1}{2} C V_{\text{final}}^{2}$$

**Step 5 — Cell-level energy using the Helmholtz model.**
For a single EDLC electrode with effective area $A$, relative permittivity $\varepsilon_r$, and Helmholtz layer distance $d$:
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

For a symmetric two-electrode cell (both electrodes have the same capacitance $C_e$, in series):
$$C_{\text{cell}} = \frac{C_e}{2} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{2d}$$

**Step 6 — Energy density of the cell.**
$$\frac{E}{\text{Volume}} = \frac{1}{2} \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{2d} \cdot \frac{V^{2}}{\text{Volume}} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A V^{2}}{4 d \cdot \text{Volume}}$$

> For activated carbon: $A \approx 2000 \text{ m}^2/\text{g}$, $d \approx 0.3 \text{ nm}$, $\varepsilon_r \approx 6$ (organic electrolyte), $V \approx 2.7 \text{ V}$ cell. The result is consistent with the **5–10 Wh/kg** observed in commercial supercapacitors.

### 3.4 Derivation: Energy Balance for Vehicle Acceleration

**Step 1 — Vehicle dynamics.**
A vehicle of mass $M$ (including ESS) accelerating from $u_1$ to $u_2$ (m/s) requires kinetic energy:
$$\Delta E_{\text{kin}} = \frac{1}{2} M (u_{2}^{2} - u_{1}^{2})$$

**Step 2 — Plus rolling and aerodynamic losses over the distance $s$ travelled during acceleration.**
$$\Delta E_{\text{loss}} = (M g f_r + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho_{\text{air}} C_d A_f u_{\text{avg}}^{2}) \cdot s$$

**Step 3 — Total power requirement over the acceleration time $\Delta t$.**
$$P_{\text{avg}} = \frac{\Delta E_{\text{kin}} + \Delta E_{\text{loss}}}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 4 — ESS contribution.**
If the FC stack can deliver a maximum steady power $P_{\text{FC,max}}$, and the peak demand is $P_{\text{peak}} = P_{\text{avg}} \cdot k_{\text{transient}}$ (where $k > 1$ during transient phases), then the ESS must provide:
$$P_{\text{ESS}} = P_{\text{peak}} - P_{\text{FC,max}}$$

**Step 5 — Time for which ESS must sustain this power:**
$$\Delta t_{\text{ESS}} = \frac{E_{\text{ESS,min}}}{P_{\text{ESS}}}$$

This determines the **minimum required energy** of the ESS, and therefore the *minimum capacitance* (for ultracapacitor) or *minimum $I\omega^{2}$* (for flywheel) of the storage device.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Ragone-style)

| Property | Lead-Acid Battery | Lithium-Ion | Ultra-Capacitor | Flywheel |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | 30–50 | 100–250 | 5–10 | 10–100 |
| Specific Power (W/kg) | 75–300 | 250–1000 | 1000–10000 | 1000–5000 |
| Cycle Life | 300–800 | 500–3000 | 10⁶–10⁷ | 10⁵–10⁶ |
| Charge Time | Hours | 0.5–2 h | Seconds | Seconds–minutes |
| Self-Discharge / Month | 4–6% | 1–2% | 20–40% | ~50–100% (friction) |
| Efficiency | 70–85% | 90–98% | 90–98% | 80–95% |
| Operating Temp (°C) | -20 to 50 | -20 to 60 | -40 to 65 | -40 to 200 |
| Calendar Life (years) | 3–6 | 8–15 | 10–15 | 15–20 |

> [!NOTE]
> For vehicle **short-term** storage, the high-power, fast-charge, and long-cycle-life columns dominate the selection criteria — making ultra-capacitors and flywheels the preferred choice over batteries.

```mermaid

    }

    }
```
